name: HMS Ursula (N 59)
events: The history of HMS Ursula as compiled on this page is extracted from Ursula's patrol reports and logbooks and was compiled in November 2012.
Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in February 2026.

31 Aug 1939 Around 1735 A/31. HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) departed Blyth to take up a patrol position in the Heligoland Bight. When war broke out between Britain and Germany this became her 1st war patrol.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ursula during this patrol see the map below.

4 Sep 1939 At 1350 A/4, in approximate position 54°33'N, 06°33'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) sighted an enemy trawler ahead. HMS Ursula went deep to avoid. The enemy was heard to pass overhead 20 minutes later.
At 1625 A/4, an enemy trawler was sighted. Course was altered to attack.
At 1650 A/4, the attack was broken off. Lt.Cdr. Phillips had decided that the enemy vessel was not of sufficient importance to justify revealing HMS Ursula's position. (1)
5 Sep 1939 At 1020 A/5, in approximate position 54°35'N, 06°40'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) sighted a small westbound merchant vessel. It was carrying a cargo of wood.
At 1445 A/5, in approximate position 54°40'N, 06°50'E, HMS Ursula sighted another small merchant vessel which was also carrying a cargo of wood. (1)
6 Sep 1939 At 0500 A/6, in approximate position 50°45'N, 06°40'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) picked up loud HE bearing 270°. Nothing could be seen though.
At 0525 A/6, the HE disappeared after apparently stopping close to.

At 1010 A/6, the Asdic detected an unseen vessel, thought to be possibly a submarine, bearing 040° at a range of 3500 yards. Altered course to close to attack if possible. The vessel passed 700 yards ahead. No torpedoes were fired and it was thought possible this was HMS Unity (Lt. J.F.B. Brown, RN). Unity's call sign was transmitted on SS/T but no reply was received. [No German submarine was in this area as far as we could determine. If this was indeed a submarine it may indeed well have been HMS Unity.]
At 1255 A/6, in approximate position 50°48'N, 06°40'E, an enemy trawler was sighted. (1)

7 Sep 1939 At 2135 A/7, while running on the surface in approximate position 54°30'N, 06°25'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) sighted a trawler for which she dived to avoid detection. After diving course was also changed to avoid. HMS Ursula surfaced again at 2230 A/7. (1)
8 Sep 1939 At 0600 A/8, in approximate position 54°30'N, 06°30'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) sighted an enemy trawler. Course was altered to avoid.

At 2150 A/8, HMS Ursula altered course to proceed to a new patrol positon in direction 200° - 42 miles from her current patrol position as had just been ordered in the Rear Admiral Submarines signal timed 2101 A/8. (1)

9 Sep 1939 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) fired the first British submarine torpedoes of the war when attacking German U-boat U-35 about 23 nautical miles north of the island of Schiermonnikoog, Netherlands in position 53°52'N 06°05'E. The U-boat escaped only to be sunk roughly two months later.
Another German U-boat was then sighted but it was not possible to attacked due to the darkness and the close proximity.
U-35 was proceeding westwards in close proximity with U-31, U-36, U-21 and U-23. All had left Wilhelmshaven around 1200/1300 hours that day for patrol.
0530 A/9 - Arrived in the new patrol position off the Brokum Riff light vessel. Proceeded to patrol, dived, in an east-west direction.
1911 A/9 - The Asdic operator reported loud HE.
1913 A/9 - Sighted a German Uboat (oceangoing-type). Started attack.
1923 A/9 - Fired four torpedoes from 1000 yards.
1926 A/9 - The enemy was observed to have altered course towards, combing the tracks. It was also seen she increased speed as heavy exhaust smoke was seen. Ursula meanwhile turned for another attack.
1933 A/9 - Fired one torpedo. It missed. The target could only just be seen in the fading light.
1940 A/9 - Heard an underwater explosion, most likely the torpedo exploding upon the end of run.

1941 A/9 - Sighted a second submarine at very close range. But owning to the darkness it could not be attacked. There would have been one loaded torpedo left in the tubes at that time, since five had already been fired.
2045 A/9 - Surfaced.
2106 A/9 - Passed an enemy report by W/T.
2145 A/9 - Dived on sighting an enemy trawler.
2210 A/9 - Bottomed. Commenced reloading the internal torpedo tubes.
2220 A/9 - Detected HE crossing the stern. The contact was stopping and starting it's engines. The HE disappeared after about 45 minutes.
0015 A/10 - Unbottomed.
0030 A/10 - Surfaced and commenced charging the batteries. (1)
10 Sep 1939 At 0130 A/10, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) altered course to proceed to position '9' on receipt of the Rear-Admiral Submarines signal timed 1941 A/9.
[We do not have info on where position '9' exactly was but it appears to be in roughly 54°30'N, 06°30'E]
At 0750 A/10, in position 54°28'N, 06°20'E, an enemy trawler was sighted which was evaded. (1)
11 Sep 1939 At 2008 A/11, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) surfaced in position 56°27'N, 03°55'E, after a day of submerged patrol. Course was set to leave patrol and return to Blyth. (1)
13 Sep 1939 Around 1800 A/13, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Blyth.
All three U-class boats suffered from engine defects upon returning from patrol. It was estimated HMS Ursula would be out of action for about six weeks during the required repairs. (1)
14 Oct 1939 Around 0400 A/14, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) departed Blyth for Rosyth where she arrived around 1800 A/14. (2)
28 Oct 1939 Around 1310 A/28, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) departed Rosyth for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the North Sea.
[No patrol report is available for this patrol, only the logs (October and November 1939. Some details may thus be missing.]
For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during this patrol see the map below.

29 Oct 1939 At 1500 A/29, in position 55°28'N, 02°03'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN), which was running on the surface, spotted a torpedo which later exploded on hitting the bottom ahead. The torpedo had been fired on HMS Ursula on a very broad track.
HMS Ursula dived and remained submerged until 1948 A/29.
No German U-boat was in the area though so the attack must have been bogus. (2)
5 Nov 1939 At 2126 A/5, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) left patrol to return to Rosyth. (3)
7 Nov 1939 Around 1300 A/7, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Rosyth. (3)
14 Nov 1939 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Rosyth Dockyard. (3)
17 Nov 1939 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) is undocked. (3)
19 Nov 1939 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) is again docked at the Rosyth Dockyard but now in the floating dock (AFD 3). (3)
20 Nov 1939 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) is undocked. (3)
22 Nov 1939 Around 1250 Z/22, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) departed Rosyth for her 3rd war patrol. She was recalled later on the same day. The recall signal was received at 2018 Z/22. (3)

23 Nov 1939 Around 0800 Z/23. HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Blyth. She was to proceed on patrol again later this day but was unable to do so due to engine defects. (3)
4 Dec 1939 Around 1400 Z/4, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) departed Blyth for her 4th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off the west coast of Denmark and in the Heligoland Bight.
6 Dec 1939 Shortly before 2000 Z/6, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) arrived in 'Area E'. She proceeded to take up a patrol position near the Horns Reef light vessel. (1)

7 Dec 1939 At 1500 Z/7, in approximate position 55°36'N, 07°04'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) sighted an enemy trawler. It was intended to attack the enemy vessel but tue frequent course and speed changes this was not possible. When closely approached they invariably altered course away. It was presumed these enemy A/S trawlers are fitted with hydrophones.
It was not considered that a trawler justifies the expenditure of more than one torpedo at point blank range, and such a position could not be attained. (4)
8 Dec 1939 At 0835 Z/8, in approximate position 55°37'N, 07°19'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) sighted an enemy trawler ahead. An attack was commenced.
At 0909 Z/8, another enemy trawler was sighted.
At 0926 Z/8, the attack was broken off. Like the day before an attack proved to be not possible. (4)
9 Dec 1939 In a signal timed 1917 Z/9, the Rear Admiral Submarines ordered HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) to patrol off Esbjerg. Course was set to patrol near the Vyl light vessel. (1)

11 Dec 1939 At 1330 Z/11, in approximate position 55°14'N, 07°30'E, (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) picked up HE ahead. Six trawlers were subsequently sighted. They appeared to be minesweeping. They were closed to attack but a large alteration of course by the enemy left HMS Ursula in a hopeless position and the attack was abandoned. The enemy kept making large alterations of course while operating in the area and further attempts in the evening were all spoiled by these course changes. (4)

12 Dec 1939 At 1000 Z/12, in approximate position 55°22'N, 07°26'E, (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) sighted six trawlers, apparently minesweeping. This may well be the same ships that had been sighted the previous day. Again no attack setup could be made due to the frequent radical course changes.

At 1140 Z/12, HMS Ursula surfaced and proceeded westwards at full surfaced speed on having received a contact report from which had sighted the German liner Bremen (51656 GRT, built 1929) in position 57°02'N, 05°52'E. However five minutes later two trawlers were sighted and HMS Ursula submerged again. At 1230 Z/12, the two trawlers were seen to be M-class minesweepers. [This must have been M-boats from the 2nd minesweeper flotilla.]
At 1833 Z/12, six vessels, thought to be trawlers, were seen proceeding on course 320°. They were burning various while lights. These may be Danish fishing vessels.
At 2037 Z/12, the signal timed 1608 Z/12 from the Capt. S.6 was received which ordered HMS Ursula to patrol hear Heligoland. Course was set accordingly. (4)

13 Dec 1939 At 0607 Z/13, in position 54°24'N, 07°23'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) had to dive for a darkened ship that came towards. This may well have been a fishing vessel. HMS Ursula remained dived for the remainder of the day during daylight hours.

At 1310 Z/13, in approximate position 54°23'N, 07°26'E, six M-class minesweepers were sighted. They were sweeping northwards in line abreast. They came from the direction of Heligoland. [This must have been M-boats from the 2nd minesweeper flotilla.]
At 1500 Z/13, HE was picked up on both sides. Course was altered as required. (4)
14 Dec 1939 Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) torpedoed and sank the German escort vessel F 9 about 6 miles south-west of Heligoland in approximate position 54°06'N, 07°45'E.

F 9 had been part of the escort of the damaged light cruiser Leipzig. Other escorts were the destroyers Z 4 / Richard Beitzen, Z 8 / Bruno Heinemann, the escort vessel F 7 and the minesweepers M 9, M 10, M 12 and M 13. Also in the area were the motor minesweepers (R-boats) R 33, R 35, R 36, R 37, R 38 and R 39.
Lt.Cdr. Philips thought he had hit the cruiser and that it was sunk. Apparently F 9 caught two of the torpedoes fired.
The cruiser seen at 1000 Z/14, was the Köln escorted by the destroyers Z 7 / Hermann Shoemann and Z 14 / Friedrich Ihn.
1000 Z/14 - Sighted an enemy light cruiser escorted by three destroyers steering 170°. An attack was not possible as HMS Ursula was 7000 yards off track.

1115 Z/14 - In approximate position 54°06'N, 07°45'E sighted a Koln-class light cruiser escorted by six destroyers. Started attack. During the attack a Ursula had to go deep to evade one of the escorting destroyers.

1131 Z/14 - Fired four torpedoes from 1200 yards. 1 min, 10 sec after firing a tremendous explosion occurred followed 6 seconds later by another even heavier explosion. This last explosion even broke some lights in Ursula. HE of four of the destroyers were heard to come towards Ursula but no depth charges were dropped. Ursula managed to slip away from the scene. (1)
16 Dec 1939 In the evening, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) set course to proceed northwards and subsequently leave patrol and proceed to Blyth. (4)
20 Dec 1939 Around 0845 Z/20, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. G.C. Phillips, RN) ended her 4th war patrol at Blyth. (4)
10 Jan 1940 Around 1530 Z/10, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) departed Blyth for her 5th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off the west coast of Denmark / entrance to the Skagerrak.

12 Jan 1940 At 0927 Z/12, in position 56°29'N, 06°10'E, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN), which was conducting a surface patrol to search for survivors from a crashed British aircraft, had to dive for an approaching vessel. This was later seen to be most likely a fishing vessel.
At 1249 Z/12, HMS Ursula surfaced and continued the search. (5)
21 Jan 1940 In the evening, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) left patrol to proceed to Rosyth. (5)
24 Jan 1940 Around 1140 Z/24, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) ended her 5th war patrol at Rosyth. (1)

25 Jan 1940 HMS H 34 (Lt. E.F. Balston, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Rosyth with HMS Vega (Cdr. C.I Horton, RN) and HMS Grimsby (Cdr. K.J. D'Arcy, RN). Upon completion of these exercises HMS H 34 departed Rosyth together with HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) and ORP Wilk (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.D. Krawczyk). HMS Ursula was to proceed to Blyth, the other two submarines to Portsmouth. They were however ordered to return to Rosyth where they arrived the next day. The reason for them being recalled was due to the threat of enemy submarines operating along the east coast of the U.K. further to the south. (6)
1 Feb 1940 Around 1430 Z/1, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) departed Rosyth for Blyth where she arrived around 0930 Z/2.
Around 1600 Z/1, HMS H 34 (Lt. E.F. Balston, RN) joined. This submarine too, was to proceed to Blyth.
Also joining at the same time was the destroyer HMS Encounter (Lt.Cdr. E.V.St J. Morgan, RN) which escorted the submarine until around 0335 Z/2, when she parted company to return to Rosyth. (7)
3 Feb 1940 HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) is docked in No.4 graving dock at Blyth. (8)
12 Feb 1940 HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) is undocked. (8)
17 Feb 1940 HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) conducted post repair trials off Blyth. (8)
20 Feb 1940 Around 1440 Z/20, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) departed Blyth for her 6th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off the West coast of Denmark.

3 Mar 1940 Around 0815 Z/3, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) ended her 6th war patrol at Blyth. The patrol had been uneventful. Only two small merchant vessels had been sighted proceeding inside Danish territorial waters. Furthermore some small motor fishing vessels had been sighted. (9)
5 Mar 1940 HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) is docked at Blyth. (10)
7 Mar 1940 HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) is undocked. (10)
12 Mar 1940 Around 2025 A/12, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) departed Blyth for her 7th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Kattegat / Skagerrak in area 'C 5'.

17 Mar 1940 While patrolling off Gotenburg, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) sighted a southbound German merchant vessel proceeding inside Swedish territorial waters. Also a northbound merchant vessel carrying one gun forward and one gun aft was seen. This may have been the Swedish icebreaker Ymer (3053 GRT, built 1933) but a positive identification was not possible due to refraction and a land background.
[No further details as times and positions are not given in the patrol report and the logbook.] (1)

19 Mar 1940 At 1108 A/19, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) damaged (bent) her after periscope when it struck heavily against floating ice. As it was considered undesirable to risk damage to the other periscope, the submarine withdrew to the northward into clear water. On surfacing in the evening an attempt was made to straighten the periscope using chains but this was unsuccessful. Another attempt to bend the periscope downwards also failed.
On account of ice the remainder of the patrol was carried out in the northern portion of area C 5. (9)
Anti-shipping raid into the Skagerrak and the Northern part of the Bight.

Around 2330 A/20, the light cruisers HMS Arethusa (Capt. Q.D. Graham, RN), HMS Aurora (Capt. L.H.K. Hamilton, DSO, RN), HMS Galatea (Capt. B.B. Schofield, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral G.F.B. Edward-Collins, CB, KCVO, RN) and HMS Penelope (Capt. G.D. Yates, RN) and the destroyers HMS Fame (Cdr. P.N. Walter, RN), HMS Firedrake (Lt.Cdr. S.H. Norris, DSC, RN), HMS Foresight (Lt.Cdr. G.T. Lambert, RN), HMS Foxhound (Lt.Cdr. G.H. Peters, RN), HMS Mashona (Cdr. W.H. Selby, RN), HMS Matabele (Cdr. G.K. Whitmy-Smith, RN), HMS Sikh (Cdr. J.A. Giffard, RN) and HMS Somali (Capt. R.S.G. Nicholson, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Operation DU, an anti-shipping raid into the Skagerrak and the Northern part of Bight.

At 1000 A/21, the force was in position 59°28'N, 01°54'E and at 1800 A/21 in position 58°06'N, 05°15'E. During the afternoon the wind was strong from the south-east and the sea moderate to rough but by 2200 A/21 both had decreased and the visibility was maximum.

By 1930 A/21, two groups had been formed. 'Force B' was made up of the Galatea, Arethusa, Firedrake, Foxhound, Sikh and Somali. while 'Force C' was made up of the Aurora (SO), Penelope, Fame, Foresight, Mashona and Matabele. Force C was stationed two miles astern of Force B and each force took up the night cruising formation that had been ordered. Force C had been ordered to act independently from 2000 A/21, when the whole force arrived in position 270°, Lister lighthouse, 12 miles, and was seen overtaking Force B at 2038 A/21 steering 143° steering for Thyborøn, Denmark.
Force B set course 143° at 17 knots for 26 miles to pass 16 miles off Lindesnes in order to avoid being sighted by any patrol or shipping close inshore.
At 2127 A/21, Force C was observed to the south-westward examining by searchlight a vessel showing its navigation lights.

At 2145 A/21, Force B altered course to 108° towards position 'T' which was 57°30'N, 08°23'E. The light of several steamers steering to the westwards were seen to the northward between 2230 A/21 and 2300 A/21. It was however Vice-Admiral Tovy's plan to proceed to position 'T' unobserved so no destroyers were detached to investigate them.

At 2223 A/21, HMS Arethusa intercepted a message from the German merchant vessel Heddernheim (4947 GRT, built 1921) and reported her bearing to be 034° first class. From the latest information (Admiralty's signal 1227 A/21), it seemed probable that this ship was the only large ship laden with iron ore which was south of Haugesund this night. It was possible she might steer for the Skaw. Vice-Admiral Tovey therefore changed the operations plan and altered course to 040° and increased speed to 20 knots at 2327 A/21 so as to intercept this merchant vessel off Oksøy about 0030 A/22.

Oskøy lighthouse was sighted at 0024 A/22 bearing 026° and at 0031 A/22 a ship, showing dimmed navigation lights was sighted but it proved to be the Danish merchant vessel Viborg (2028 GRT, built 1919). At 0044 A/22, course was altered to 245° to search westwards at 15 knots. It was not until 0059 A/22 that the Admiralty signal 2317 A/21 was received which stated that the Heddernheim had been stopped by the submarine HMS Ursula 8 miles east of the Skaw. No cause for the large error in the D/F bearing of HMS Arethusa has been found.

During the rest of the night Force B patrolled off Ryvingen and searched westwards along the coast. HMS Firedrake and HMS FoxhoundHMS Somali and HMS Sikh parted company at 0223 A/22 to search close inshore. They did not rejoin until daylight. At 0501 A/22, HMS Firedrake and HMS Foxhound were again detached to investigate ships and at 0545 A/22, HMS Firedrake reported that she sighted Force C bearing 135° returning to make rendezvous with Force B. They came back from the area of Hantsholm.
At daybreak the weather was overcast, wind was east force 4, visibility was good. Force B encountered a few merchant vessels which were examined.

At 0646 A/22, HMS Sikh and HMS Somali were sighted bearing 080°, 6 miles. Force C rejoined at 0658 A/22. At 0730 A/22, the combined force in the agreed rendezvous position and was steering 321°, speed 19 knots.

Both HMS Sikh and HMS Somali had sighted several merchant vessels. Some might have been German but they could not be closed in time and and could not be inspected as they were also in territorial waters.
At 0805 A/22, it was snowing heavily, and the visibility was reduced at times to two miles. Windwas east-south-east, force 2. The combined force was sweeping up the coast of Norway at 19 knots.
At 0915 A/22, HMS Galatea sighted a torpedo track but investigation left little doubt that it had not been a torpedo but a fish as a large school of black fish was seen.

At 0922 A/22, HMS Somali reported a German vessel ahead and was ordered to capture her but not to enter territorial waters. HMS Somali fired a gun to bring the ship to, but was not able to stop her before she reached territorial waters. When the ship was one mile off the shore, the crew were seen to take to the boats. It is not known whether they had scuttled their ship. At 0932 A/22, HMS Somali was ordered to rejoin the force. On her return HMS Somali reported that the German vessel was the Butt (800 GRT, built 1909), homeport Bremen.
At 0948 A/22, HMS Firedrake was detached to examine a merchant vessel making a lot of smoke. At 0955 A/22, HMS Firedrake reported that it was a Danish merchant vessel.
At 1020 A/22, HMS Somali reported two merchant vessels ahead. HMS Somali then proceeded ahead to investigate. She rejoined at 1048 and reported that both vessels were also Danish.
At noon, HMS Somali spoke two more Danish vessels. Weather was now overcast, wind south, force 2, visibility maximum.
At 1203 A/22, a small warship very close inshore was sighted by HMS Galatea. It was identified as a Norwegian torpedo boat.
At 1212 A/22, in position 197°, 8 miles, Utsire lighthouse, course was altered to 263° in ordered tht it should appear from shore that the force was returning to Scapa Flow.

At 1315 A/22, course was altered to 240° and to 162° at 1340 A/22 and speed was adjusted to pass trough the following positions, 58°25'N, 04°28'E at 1600 A/22 and 58°06'N, 05°15'E. During the afternoon the wind freshened from the south-eastward, to force 6, and the sea became rather rough.

Vice-Admiral Tovey's intentions for the night were that the two forces were to be stationed 15 miles apart to avoud sight one another during the night. Both forces were to move 30 miles westward at 0130 A/23 so that whatever delay had occurred owing to investigations and boardings, they would still be at least 15 miles apart.
At 1810 A/22, Force C was ordered to take station 15 miles 270° from HMS Galatea.
At 1915 A/22, Force B took up night cruising formation, HMS Somali and HMS Sikh on the port quarter, HMS Foxhound and HMS Firedrake on the starboard quarter.
At 2050 A/22, the sky was overcast, visibility good, wind south-east, force 5, sea rough but decreasing.
About 2200 A/22, when 5 miles south of Lindesnes, HMS Galatea sighted a merchant vessel which turned out to be Danish and HMS Foxhound sighted a Norwegian merchant vessel.

The sweep was continued troughout the night according to plan and without incident. The wind at 0200 was east-southeast force 4, sky clear, slight east-south-east swell, visibility good. Around 0500/3, the force ran into rather rough water.
At 0610 A/23, when south of the approach to Farsund, HMS Foxhound was detached to investigate a large merchant ship which was found out to be Swedish.
At 0713 A/23, Force B sighted Force C and at 0740 A/23 the whole force was in formation again. Course was set to 272°, speed 22 knots.
The weather deteriorated rapidly. There were heavy snow showers and a south-easterly gale blowing by 0900 A/23.

At 1143 A/23, HMS Aurora and all destroyers were detached to Scapa Flow. Visibility was then about 1.5 miles. The three remaining cruisers altered course and proceeded to Rosyth where they arrived around 0030 A/24.
HMS Aurora and the destroyers arrived at Scapa Flow around 1130 A/24. (11)

21 Mar 1940 HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) torpedoed and sank the German merchant vessel Heddernheim (4947 GRT, built 1921) in the Skagerrak about 10 nautical miles east-north-east of Skagen, Denmark in position 57°48'N, 10°53'E.
2050 A/21 - Altered course to eastward to intercept an unknown merchant vessel steering west.
2112 A/21 - The ship was identified as the Norwegian merchant vessel Ottar Jarl (1459 GRT, built 1929, offsite link). Broke off the approach and set course to 190°.

2146 A/21 - Sighted a dim lights of a ship. Closed to investigate. When close signalled the ship to stop. This they did not do. After another attempt the ship was seen to increase speed so a practice round was fired with the gun as a warning shot. The ship now stopped and was asked to identify her self. She signalled 'Estonian'. Meanwhile Ursula crossed close astern of the ship and with the Aldis light the name 'Heddernheim - Bremen' could be read. The crew was ordered to abandon ship. Meanwhile they were also signalling. The crew eventually sent a boat over but this all took a very long time, it was obvious they were 'buying time'. Once again they were ordered to abandon ship, which they did but again very slow. A second warning round was now fired and this speeded up things a bit.
While Ursula was manoeuvred into a position to torpedo the ship no.1 torpedo tube was fired by accident. This torpedo missed the ship.

Due to ice damage to Ursula only no.2 and no.5 torpedo tubes could be fired. so these were now brought to the ready. No.5 torpedo tube was now fired but the torpedo failed to run. Possibly it was damaged upon discharge als due to ice damage.
Now no.2 tube was fired. This torpedo hit the ship amidships and it sank shortly afterwards.
One prisoner, the ships chief engineer, was taken from the boats. (1)
22 Mar 1940 Shortly before 0300 A/22, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) left patrol to return to Blyth. (9)

23 Mar 1940 At 0010 A/23, in approximate position 58°00'N, 08°50'E, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) stopped the Danish merchant vessel Sejrö (1489 GRT, built 1929) but released her after examination. (1)
25 Mar 1940 Around 1030 A/25, HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) ended her 7th war patrol at Blyth.

HMS Ursula had sustained quite a bit of weather damage during this patrol by ice, Almost all torpedo tube outer doors were damaged and could not be opened and the after periscope was also bent by hitting ice and had to be replaced. (1)
27 Mar 1940 HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) is docked in No.4 graving dock at Blyth. (9)
2 Apr 1940 HMS Ursula (Cdr. G.C. Phillips, DSO, RN) is undocked. (12)
8 Apr 1940 Around 0440 A/8, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) departed Blyth for her 8th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off the west coast of Denmark / entrance to the Skagerrak.

9 Apr 1940 At 0031 A/9, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) received the Vice Admiral Submarines signal timed 2339 A/8 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 57°00'N, 05°00'E (Position MBND). Course was set to comply with these orders. (13)

11 Apr 1940 At 2200 A/11, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) left her current patrol area in accordance with the orders received the Vice Admiral Submarines signal timed 1836 A/11 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 57°00'N, 06°00'E (Position MBRU). (13)

12 Apr 1940 At 0845 A/12, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) altered course to 270° to intercept enemy warships reported in the Vice Admiral Submarines signal timed 0913 Z/12 [note the one hour time difference]. This signal reported that an enemy battlecruiser, pocket battleship and cruiser had been sighted in position MBND 2000 (57°20'N, 05°00'E) proceeding on course 140° at 15 knots at 0900 Z/12.
Nothing was however sighted of the reported enemy ships.
At 1925 A/12, HMS Ursula surfaced to search for survivors of a British aircraft down in position DKND 5738 (56°57'N, 05°38'E). The search was not successful. (14)
18 Apr 1940 At 0200 A/18, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) left patrol to return to Blyth.
At 0937 A/18, course was altered to proceed back to the patrol position in accordance with the Vice Admiral Submarines signal timed 0828 Z/18.
At 2025 A/18, HMS Ursula left patrol to return to Blyth in accordance with the Vice Admiral Submarines signal timed 1644 Z/18. (1)
20 Apr 1940 Around 1320 A/20, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) ended her 8th war patrol at Blyth. (1)

22 Apr 1940 Around 1145 A/22, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) departed Blyth for Wallsend where she arrived around 1500 A/22. At Wallsend she was immediately docked and taken in hand for a refit by the Swan Hunter and Wigham Richardson Ltd. yard. (12)
4 Jul 1940 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) is undocked. She continued her refit at Wallsend. (15)
18 Jul 1940 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) ended her refit at Wallsend. She proceeded to South Shields where she was wiped (degaussed). (15)

19 Jul 1940 Around 0810 A/19, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) departed South Shields for Blyth where she arrived around 1600 A/19. En-route engine trials had been carried out and also a test dive. On entering Blyth harbour D/G trials were carried out. (15)

24 Jul 1940 Around 0520 A/24, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) and HMS Spearfish (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Forbes, DSO, RN) proceeded from Blyth to Rosyth where they arrived around 2145 A/24. In the evening exercises were carried out near May Island with HMS White Bear (Capt. R. Gill, RNR). (15)
25 Jul 1940 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) conducted noise trials in a dock at the Rosyth Dockyard. (15)
27 Jul 1940 In the evening HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) proceeded from Rosyth to Methil where she anchored for the night. (15)
28 Jul 1940 Around 0450 A/28, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) left the anchorage off Methil to Blyth where she arrived around 1430 A/28. (15)
30 Jul 1940 Around 1500 A/30, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) departed Blyth for her 9th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the North Sea (area H 2).

4 Aug 1940 At 0029 A/4, in position 53°02'N, 03°45'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) sighted flashing white light astern on which she dived. This turned out to be a diesel A/S craft which was transmitting on Asdic and kept stopping and starting at short intervals. HMS Ursula kept stern one and crept away at slow speed.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (16)

5 Aug 1940 At 0212 A/5, in approximate position 52°53'N, 03°42'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) dived on sighting a flashing white light on the port quarter. No vessel was sighted and no HE was picked up. At 0304 A/5, HMS Ursula surfaced again. (16)
7 Aug 1940 At 1155 A/5, in 52°56'N, 04°13'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) sighted a fast moving motor vessel which passed half a mile astern. This may have been a fishing vessel.
At 2200 A/7, HMS Ursula proceeded clear of the area in accodance with the Capt. S.6's signal timed 1746 A/2. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (16)

8 Aug 1940 At 0706 A/5, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) received the Capt. S.6's signal timed 0306 A/8 which ordered to to patrol in the northern part of area H 2 and area H 1. Course was set to comply with this signal at 0730 A/5. (16)
10 Aug 1940 At 2200 A/10, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) left patrol to return to Blyth. (1)
12 Aug 1940 Around 1350 A/12, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) ended her 9th war patrol at Blyth. (1)
25 Aug 1940 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) conducted compass adjustment and D/G trials at Blyth. (17)
28 Aug 1940 Around 1930 A/28, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) departed Blyth for her 10th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off the south-west coast of Norway, near Stavanger.
1 Sep 1940 Around midnight during the night of 1/2 September 1940, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) arrived in her patrol area. (1)

3 Sep 1940 During the night of 3/4 September 1940, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) had to dive three times due to unidentified lights being sighted or flares being dropped. This took place at 2335 A/3, 0042 A/4 and 0122 A/4. No shipping was sighted and no HE was picked up on all three occasions. HMS Ursula remained dived after she had dived on the last occasion only surfacing at 2146 A/4 in position 58°56'N, 04°32'E. (18)
6 Sep 1940 At 01345 A/6, in position 58°54'N, 04°20'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) had to crash dive when flares were dropped and also some flashing lights were sighted. (18)

8 Sep 1940 At 0244 A/8, in position 58°55'N, 04°14'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) had to crash dive when flares were dropped and also some flashing lights were sighted. She remained dived until surfacing at 2035 A/8 in position 58°50'N, 04°12'E. (18)

9 Sep 1940 At 2300 A/9, in position 58°56'N, 04°32'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) had to crash dive when flares were dropped and lights were sighted. She surfaced again at 0042 A/9 in position 58°56'N, 04°26'E. (18)

10 Sep 1940 At 2030 A/10, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) surfaced in position 58°40'N, 03°22'E, and left patrol to return to Blyth in accordance with the orders of the Vice Admiral Submarines received earlier that day. (18)
13 Sep 1940 Around 1330 A/13, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) ended her 10th war patrol at Blyth. (18)
16 Sep 1940 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) conducted D/G and compass adjustment trials at Blyth. (19)
17 Sep 1940 Around 1530 A/17, HMS Swordfish (Lt. P.J. Cowell, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) departed Blyth for Porstmouth. Later they was ordered to proceed to Sheerness instead.
Around 1930 A/17, they joined the southbound convoy FS 84.
[For more info on this convoy see the event ' Convoy FS 84 ' for 17 September 1940.] (20)
This convoy departed Methil around 1000 A/17.
On departure from Methil the convoy was made up of only one ship; Royal Fusilier (British, 2186 GRT, built 1924).
On departure from Methil the convoy was escorted by the sloop HMS Black Swan (Capt. Albert Lawrence Poland, DSO, DSC, RN) and the escort destroyer HMS Verdun (Lt.Cdr. F.J. Cartwright, RN).
Around 1930 A/17, the submarines HMS Swordfish (Lt. P.J. Cowell, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) joined coming from Blyth.
Six merchant vessel were to have joined the convoy coming from Blyth but only the Foreland (British, 1870 GRT, built 1939) and Hawkwood (British, 2033 GRT, built 1934) joined.
Five merchant vessel were to have joined the convoy coming from the Tyne but none actually joined.
The merchant vessel Sanfry (946 GRT, built 1930) joined the convoy coming from Sunderland.
On the 18th, near the Humber the A/S trawlers HMS Basset (T/Lt. N.S. Heriot, RNVR) and HMS Tourmaline (Lt. H.P. Carse, RNVR) joined the convoy coming from convoy FN 83.
The convoy arrived at Southend in the afternoon of the 19th. The two submarines proceeded to Sheerness. (21)
19 Sep 1940 Around 1600 A/19, HMS Swordfish (Lt. P.J. Cowell, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) arrived at Sheerness. (19)

20 Sep 1940 Around 1430 A/20, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) departed Sheerness for her 11th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the English Channel, in area D 4, due to the threat of the invasion of England by the Germans.

On departure from Sheerness HMS Swordfish (Lt. P.J. Cowell, RN), which was to proceed to Portsmouth was in company. The submarines were escorted by HMS Widgeon (Lt.Cdr. R. Frederick, RN). HMS Widgeon joined the submarines off the Medway gate at 1515 A/20.
Around 0515 A/21, HMS Ursula parted company to proceed on patrol. (1)
20 Sep 1940 For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during her 11th patrol see the map below.
23 Sep 1940 At 0945 A/23, in position 49°48'N, 00°26'E, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) sighted three small vessels proceeding westwards close inshore.
At 1220 A/23, twelve fishing vessels were seen to enter Fécamp. They came from the west.
At 1320 A/23, in position 49°48'N, 00°25'E, an armed trawler was seen proceeding on course 285° close inshore. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 2232 A/23, the Capt. S.5's signal timed 2232 A/23 was received which gave information of Allied light forces operating in the area during the night on an anti shipping sweep.

This referred to a sweep by the destroyers HMS Wolverine (Cdr. R.H. Craske, RN), HMS Witherington (Lt.Cdr. J.B. Palmer, RN), HMS Viscount (Lt.Cdr. M.S. Townsend, DSC, OBE, RN) and HMS Brilliant (Lt.Cdr. F.C. Brodrick, RN). (22)
24 Sep 1940 At 0527 A/24, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) dived and closed the coast again.
At 0930 A/24, in position 49°54'N, 00°34'E, HMS Ursula sighted an armed trawler three miles from the shore proceeding on course 265°. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]

At 1130 A/24, in position 49°54'N, 00°37'E, HMS Ursula sighted two armed trawlers proceeding on course 070°. These were obviously A/S trawlers and were searching along the coast about 5 miles from the shore. They showed some interest in the position of HMS Ursula for about 15 minutes. HMS Ursula turned stern on until the enemy withdrew to the east. No A/S transmissions were picked up. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.]
At 2015 A/24, the Capt. S.5's signal timed 1845 A/24 was received. HMS Ursula left her patrol area so as to enter area B 3 in accordance with the orders received. (22)
26 Sep 1940 At 0100 A/26, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) left patrol to proceed to Portsmouth in accordance with the orders received in the Capt. S.5's signal timed 1441 A/25.
At 1125 A/26, HMS Ursula ended her 11th war patrol at Portsmouth (Fort Blockhouse / Gosport). Before proceeding to Fort Blockhouse D/G trials were carried out. (1)
4 Oct 1940 Around 1545 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) departed Portsmouth for her 12th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the English Channel.
Around 1625 A/4, she was joined by the A/S trawler HMS Hugh Walpole (Skr. D. Upson, RNR) which escorted her until around 1905 A/4.
[There is no patrol report is available, only the log.] (23)
4 Oct 1940 For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during this patrol see the map below.
5 Oct 1940 In a signal timed 0425 A/5, the Capt. S.5 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) to patrol in area D 4.
The signal was received at 0512 A/5 and course was set in accordance with these orders.
At 1300 A/5, in approximate position 49°51'N, 00°16'E, HMS Ursula sighted a small coaster bearing 120° at a range of 6000 yards. Enemy course was 240°.
At 1800 A/5, course was changed to the west in accordance with the signal timed 1331 A/5 from the Capt. S.5. in which she was ordered to patrol in area W 2. (23)
6 Oct 1940 At 2000 A/6, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) set course to the north to leave patrol and return to Portsmouth. (23)
7 Oct 1940 At 1045 A/7, HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) ended her 12th war patrol at Portsmouth (Gosport, Haslar Creek, Fort Blockhouse, HMS Dolphin).
She was escorted in by the A/S trawler HMS Warwick Deeping (Skr. J.R. Bruce, RNR) which had joined around 0655 A/7. (23)
14 Oct 1940 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) is docked in AFD 2 at Fort Blockhouse / Gosport. (23)
15 Oct 1940 HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) is undocked. (23)

20 Oct 1940 Around 1245 A/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Portsmouth for Gibraltar to join the Mediterranean Fleet. She is to be based at Malta to operate against Italian traffic towards North Africa.

HMS Ursula first carried out a diving exercises before she proceeded to be boom defence where she was joined at 1540 A/20 by an escort which escorted out until shortly before 1800 A/20. [Identity of the escort not giving in the patrol report and the logbook.]
On 21 Octover, HMS Ursula was ordered to perform an anti-uboat patrol in the Bay of Biscay for two days making this her 13th war patrol.

21 Oct 1940 At 2024 A/21, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) received the Vice Admiral Submarines signal timed 1733 A/21 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 'B 3' (47°30'N, 10°00'W) until 1200 A/26.

The reason for this was a grim one, in the last 72 hours many merchant ships had been sunk in the Atlantic by German submarines and it was expected a fair number of them would have to return to France due to the fact that they were out of torpedoes. (24)

22 Oct 1940 At 2131 A/22, in position 48°54'N, 07°00'W, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted two white lights about 6 cables apart which were thought to be fishing trawlers. Nothing could be heard on Asdic. HMS Ursula turned away to avoid. (24)

26 Oct 1940 At 1630 A/26, in position 46°38'N, 10°20'W, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) was at periscope depth when a submarine was observed suddenly at a distance of only 100 yards. Ursula surfaced for gun action but the weather was too rough to man the gun and the submarine disappeared from view. This was either the Italian Maggiore Barraca or Alpino Bagnolini sailing out for a patrol. Neither reported this encounter. (24)
30 Oct 1940 Shortly before 1700 A/30, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Portsmouth.
She was escorted in by HMS Griffin (Lt.Cdr J. Lee-Barber, DSO, RN) which had joined around 1100 A/30 to the west of the Straits of Gibraltar in position 35°58'N, 06°29'W.
HMS Ursula arrived at Gibraltar with various defects and required several weeks of repairs before she could proceed to Malta. Eventually repairs and trials would take 10 weeks. (24)
29 Dec 1940 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Gibraltar Dockyard. (25)
3 Jan 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) is undocked. (26)
10 Jan 1941 During the night of 10/11 January, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) conducted a 12 hours dockside full power trial of her port generator. (26)
11 Jan 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Gibraltar. (26)
12 Jan 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. These included A/S exercises with A/S trawlers. (26)

16 Jan 1941 Around 0945 A/16, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 14th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Genoa and to proceed to Malta afterwards.
En route to the Gulf of Genoa she had to make her presence known off the Spanish coast.

Before proceeding on patrol A/S exercises were carried out with the British destroyers HMS Faulknor (Capt A.F. de Salis, RN), HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. T.C. Robinson, RN) and HMS Duncan (A/Capt. A.D.B. James, RN). After the A/S exercises were completed two practice attacks were made on HMS Faulknor. At 1815 A/16, HMS Ursula left to proceed on patrol. (1)
16 Jan 1941 For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during her 14th war patrol see the map below.

18 Jan 1941 At 1900 A/18, in approximate position 38°27'N, 00°06'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a merchant vessel on the port bow. It was burning it's navigation lights. At 1925 A/18, after closing the vessel it was seen to be the Vichy-French Caudebec (French, 1471 GRT, built 1910). (27)

19 Jan 1941 At 0030 A/1, south-east of Valencia, Spain, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a merchant vessel burning it's navigation and deck lights. At 0100 A/1, the vessel was identified as the Spanish merchant vessel Ciudad de Sevilla (5943 GRT, built 1928). (27)

20 Jan 1941 While patrolling off Barcelona, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted four Vichy-French merchant vessels during the day. Also an unidentified merchant vessel of about 1500 tons was sighted. This vessel was painted grey and was showing no flag or other identification signals.
[We are not aware of an Italian or German vessel being in the area.]
At 1200 A/20, in position 41°19'N, 02°17'E, a Vichy-French merchant vessel though to be one of the 'San Antonio'-class was sighted proceeding on course 066°.
At 1326 A/20, the Vichy French merchant vessel Sidi Mabrouck (3809 GRT, built 1906) was sighted proceeding on course 060°.
At 1620 A/20, an unidentified merchant vessel, of about 1500 tons without any flags or other identification signals was sighted. It was making for Barcelona. Interception was not possible.
[Once again, we are not aware of an Italian or German vessel being in the area.]
At 1700 A/20, an unidentified Vichy French merchant vessel was sighted.
At 1915 A/20, the Vichy French merchant vessel Congo (5202 GRT, built 1914) was sighted proceeding on course 246°. (27)

25 Jan 1941 At 0550 A/25, off the west coast of Sardinia, in approximate position 39°45'N, 08°00'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted an unescorted convoy of four Vichy-French merchant vessels proceeding on course 000°.

At 1715 A/25, off south-west Sardinia, in approximate position 39°15'N, 08°00'E, a convoy of three Vichy-French merchant vessels was sighted proceeding southwards. The convoy was escorted by four destroyers of the L'Adroit-class.
[We have been unable to identify this convoy.] (27)
29 Jan 1941 Around 0720 A/29, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (1)

4 Feb 1941 Around 2315 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol along the shipping route from Sicily to Tripoli near the Kuriat Islands off the east coast of Tunisia in area 'J' between latitudes 35°15'N and 35°45'E and longitudes 11°28'E and 12°05'E.
5 Feb 1941 At 1230 A/5, in approximate position 35°42'N, 13°25'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) picked up HE. She turned towards to investigate but nothing was seen. (28)
6 Feb 1941 In a signal timed the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) to extend your patrol area 10 miles to westward to 11°15'E. (29)
8 Feb 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) made a torpedo attack on an Italian convoy in position 35°15'N, 11°30'E. Four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.

These were the Italian transports Nirvo (5270 GRT, built 1919) and the Italian tankers Berbera (2093 GRT, built 1931) and Caucaso (2082 GRT, built 1921). They were escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Attilio Deffenu (3510 GRT, 1929). No torpedo boats were present according the the passage report of the Deffenu. The convoy sailed from Naples at 0910 B/6. At 2045 B/8, in position 35°11'50"N, 11°19'19"E, Attilio Deffenu heard distant explosions, probably the torpedoes hitting the seabed. Nothing was observed though. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1210 B/10.

The 'torpedo boats' sighted at 2211 hours were probably two of these four destroyers, Lanzerotto Malocello, Luca Tarigo, Freccia and Saetta which had sailed from Palermo at 0920 B/8-1140 B/8 and arrived at Tripoli at 1030 B/9-1120 B/9. If the vessels sighted really looked like Spica-class torpedo boats with one funnel it were probably Freccia and Saetta.
1314 A/8 - Sighted smoke bearing 100°.
1323 A/8 - No more smoke had been seen. Surfaced and proceeded at full speed in the direction in which the smoke had been seen.
1406 A/8 - There had been no trace of smoke or shipping so dived in position 35°30'N, 11°21'E, and continued patrol.
1719 A/8 - Sighted masts bearing 240°. Closed.

1730 A/8 - The masts by now had developed into a convoy of three merchant ships escorted by two torpedo boats, thought to be of the Spica-class. Enemy course was 135° at an estimated speed of 11 knots. Range was 18000 yards. Started attack.
1800 A/8 - Surfaced.
1935 A/8 - At a range of 6000 yards and no possibility to get any closer fired four torpedoes in position 35°15'N, 11°30'E.
1940 A/8 - Dived as one of the torpedo boats was seen to turn towards.
1944 A/8 - Heard one distant explosion. No other explosions were heard. No counter attack developed.
1956 A/8 - Surfaced and proceeded in chase of the convoy but could not regain touch. Also no damaged ship was seen so the torpedo explosion was most likely a torpedo hitting the bottom.
2100 A/8 - Abandoned the chase and set course to the north to charge the batteries.
2211 A/8 - Dived to avoid two torpedo boats proceeding to the south-east at 18 knots. (1)
9 Feb 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) made a torpedo attack on an Italian convoy near position 35°42'N, 11°15'E. Two torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.
These were Italian transport Pomona (2311 GRT, built 1921) and Italian tanker Fianona (6723 GRT, built 1916).

They had sailed at 0800 B/7 at 9 knots from Naples for Sfax. After the attack, the two vessels dropped anchor off Sousse and requested to enter harbour due to the submarine threat. Admiral Derrien, commanding French naval forces in Tunisia, refused. Admiral Farina of the CIAF (Italian Armistice Commission with France) made a special demand to Admiral Ollive (Amiral Sud, Commanding the Mediterranean Theatre) who accepted as long as the two ships were empty and this would be an exception. The two ships entered Sousse and remained there until the 11th and, after anchoring in the Sousse roads, departed on the 12th.

0150 A/9 - Sighted a darkened ship bearing 340°, steering 140°. Closed on the surface. Shortly afterwards sighted a second ship about 1.5 nautical miles on the first ships port quarter. Attained a firing position on the port side of the first ship, a lightly laden tanker of about 6000 tons. As only two torpedoes were ready after the attack of previous evening Lt. decided to attack both ships with one torpedo each.
0209 A/9 - Fired one torpedo at the tanker from 500 yards. The torpedo was seen to pass underneath the tanker without exploding.

0215 A/9 - Fired the last torpedo at the second vessel from 1200 yards. Shortly after firing this ship changed course as the tanker fired a rocket and changed course as to ram Ursula. So this torpedo missed. Ursula meanwhile dived to avoid being rammed. (1)
10 Feb 1941 At 1325 A/10, HMS Ursula received the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1127 A/10 in which she was ordered to leave patrol and return to Malta. (1)
11 Feb 1941 Around 1720 A/11, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (1)
19 Feb 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) is wiped (degaussed) at Malta. (30)

20 Feb 1941 Around 1845 A/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). As in her previous patrol she was ordered to patrol along the shipping route from Sicily to Tripoli near the Kuriat Islands off the east coast of Tunisia in area 35°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 30 - 40.

22 Feb 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Sabbia (5788 GRT, built 1926) about 20 nautical miles east of Susah, Tunisia in position 35°47'N, 11°16'E. Following this attack HMS Ursula was depth charged by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari.

The other ship in the convoy was the Italian transport Silvia Tripcovich (2365 BRT, built 1925). [Nothing could be found in the Italian archives about the identity of two other torpedo boats sighted and according to the report of Montanari she was the only escort.]

Sabbia escorted by Montanari sailed from Palermo at 0910 B/21 and arrived at Trapani at 1810 B/21 where they joined up with the Silvia Tripcovich and sailed at 1930 B/21 for Tripoli. At 1705 B/22, Sabbia was torpedoed astern in position 35°47'N, 11°13'E, and the torpedo boat charged at 22.5 knots toward a release of bubbles caused by the firing of torpedoes. She was assisted by two Italian escorting aircraft which dived on the submarine and fired a burst of machine gun to mark the spot. At 1712 B/9, Montanari dropped a buoy followed by four depth charges set at a depth of 25 metres, observing a large patch of oil and some debris. A second pattern of five depth charges followed, set at the same depth, and produced a large bubble. Believing the submarine sunk, the torpedo boat left the scene to catch up with Sabbia which managed to make 6-6.5 knots. They arrived at Tripoli at 1300 B/24. Silvia Tripcovich had not lingered on the scene and fled alone, only to be sunk by HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) the following night, there were no survivors.

1315 A/22 - Sighted the wreck of a merchant (cargo) vessel of approximately 5000 tons aground to the north-east of Kuriat in position 35°49.5'N, 11°06'E. This vessel was not in this position when HMS Ursula was last in this area on 9 February.

1521 A/22 - Sighted a convoy consisting of one transport and one cargo vessel. They were escorted by three torpedo boats. At least one aircraft was overhead. Course of the convoy was 156°. Started attack.
1608 A/22 - In position 35°47'N, 11°16'E fired three torpedoes at the transport ship from 2500 yards. After 1 minute and 43 seconds one hit was obtained. Ursula went to 70 feet.
1616 A/22 - 10 depth charges were dropped astern and drawing closer. Reduced speed to make less noise.
1637 A/22 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the transport to be deeper in the water and sinking on an even keel. All the lifeboats were gone. Returned to 70 feet.

1818 A/22 - Returned to periscope depth. The transport was seen with decks awash, still on even keel down slightly by the stern. It was considered that she would not last for more than a few minutes. Bad light conditions prevented Ursula from attacking again from periscope depth. Returned to 70 feet.
1918 A/22 - Surfaced in position 35°55'N, 11°14'E.
2009 A/22 - Aircraft dropped flares over HMS Ursula. Dived.
2030 A/22 - Surfaced in approximate position 35°56'N, 11°16'E.

2045 A/22 - Aircraft dropped more flares over HMS Ursula. Dived again. Decided to remain submerged until this aircraft had expended either all its flares or all its petrol. Went to 70 feet. Reloaded no. 3 and 4 torpedo tubes.
2207 A/22 - Surfaced. (1)
24 Feb 1941 At 2240 A/24, to the east of Mahdia, Tunisia, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) had to dive to avoid darkened small vessels apparently carrying out an A/S search.

Perhaps this were Vichy-French patrol boats. French records however indicate some activities of Italian armed schooners in Tunisian waters. Italian records do not mention any. For example, French records show that the Italian armed schooners Carla, Effisia Serra and Emma dropped anchor off Kelibia at 1700 B/17 February but nothing is given on Italian vessels on this day.
At 2336 A/24, HMS Ursula surfaced and continued patrol. (28)

25 Feb 1941 At 2049 A/25, to the east of Mahdia, Tunisia, in approximate position 35°26'N, 11°20'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) had to dive to avoid darkened small vessels apparently carrying out an A/S search.

Again perhaps this were Vichy-French patrol boats. French records however indicate some activities of Italian armed schooners in Tunisian waters but no details are given for this specific day. Italian records do not mention any.
At 2139 A/25, HMS Ursula surfaced and continued patrol. (28)
27 Feb 1941 At 0244 A/27, to the east of Kuriat, Tunisia, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a darkened ship which was closed on the electric motors.
At 0305 A/27, HMS Ursula dived when it was seen that the 'darkened ship' was to be three small torpedo boats carrying out an A/S search. One of the torpedo boats was showing a white stern light.
At 0353 A/27, HMS Ursula surfaced and continued patrol diving again for submerged patrol at 0529 A/29 when daylight was breaking. (28)

27 Feb 1941 In a signal timed 1337 A/27, the Cdr. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN); Leave area and proceed through position 35°40'N, 14°07'E to arrive off to arrive Marsamxett harbour, Malta at 0700 A/3. Do not break W/T silence unless necessary. (29)

2 Mar 1941 At 0344 A/2, north-west of Lampedusa, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) had to crash dive when a destroyer or torpedo boat was sighted at only 1000 yards range. The enemy was coming straight towards. Visibility at this time was not more than 2000 yards owing to low lying haze or fog.

At 0347 A/2, the enemy passed overhead. Subsequently HE of a second destroyer or torpedo boat was heard. HMS Ursula came to periscope depth intending to surface and investigate but a report of HE coming towards forced the submarine to go back to 60 feet and almost immediately afterwards this HE passed overhead. It appeared that a convoy was present.

0412 A/2, Surfaced and searched but the vessels heard could not be seen. Visibility was bad only 1000 to 2000 yards. These vessels were heard in position 35°45'N, 12°15'E and appeared to be steering a course of 270° which seems to be an unlikely course for either a northbound or southbound enemy convoy.

This must have been the convoy made up of the German transports Alicante (2140 GRT, built 1934), Leverkusen (7382 GRT, built 1928), Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) and Arcturus (2586 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi and the torpedo boats Procione, Orsa and Calliope sailed from Tripoli at 1215 B/1-1300 B/1 and arrived at Naples at 1100 B/3-1130 B/3. Their sailing orders indicate that they ought to have been NE of Lampedusa at 0245 B/2 and off Pantelleria at 1200 B/2, so they must have been a couple of hours late on their schedule.

Discounted possibilities: The convoy made up of the Italian transport Amsterdam (8673 GRT, built 1927) and the German transports Ruhr (5955 GRT, built 1926), Castellon (2068 GRT, built 1935) and Maritza (2910 GRT, built 1936) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Clio, Orione and Pegaso sailed from Naples at 0200 B/1-0405 B/1 and arrived at Tripoli at 1800 B/3-1940 B/3. However, they were off Marettimo at 0324 B/2, so it could not be this convoy.
The Italian torpedo boats Pleiadi and Partenope sailed from Augusta at 1900 B/1-1910 B/1 and arrived at Tripoli at 1245 B/2-1300 B/2. They passed east of Malta and the time does not match.
The Italian torpedo boat Sagittario sailed from Pantelleria at 1005 B/2 for a patrol and arrived at Lampedusa at 0805 B/3, but the time does not match. (1)
3 Mar 1941 Around 0800 A/3, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (1)
6 Mar 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) is docked in No/4 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (31)
7 Mar 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) is undocked. (31)
15 Mar 1941 At 2355 A/15, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was to patrol in the Sicilian Channel. (31)

16 Mar 1941 At 2012 A/16, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) surfaced to the north-east of Linosa, in position 36°10'N, 13°20'E. The Asdic had been giving trouble and on surfacing it was noticed that there was a large dent on the after side of the cage which prevented the oscillator from being turned round.
Repairs were not possible at sea and at 2119 A/16, course was set to return to Malta to dock the submarine and make repairs. (31)
17 Mar 1941 Around 0910 A/17, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) returned to harbour. The flotilla A/S officer attempted to make repairs but were unsuccesful. (31)
18 Mar 1941 In the afternoon, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) is docked in No.5 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard to make repairs to the Asdic dome cage. (31)

19 Mar 1941 In the afternoon HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) was undocked and she sailed around 2000 A/19 to resume her 17th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) a few hours later. This was her 17th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was now to patrol to the south of Lampedusa to intercept an important Italian convoy that had left Naples for Tripoli.

This referred to the convoy made up of the German transports Arcturus (2576 GRT, built 1937), Procida (1842 GRT, built 1927), Santa Fe (4627 GRT, built 1921) and Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the Italian destroyers Saetta, Fulmine and Baleno. The convoy sailed from Naples at 0050-0130 B/19 and arrived at Tripoli at 1327-1535 B/21.

20 Mar 1941 At 1707 B/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) received a signal timed 1441 B/20, from the Cdr. S.1 ordering her to carry out operation 'Sich'. This was an operation to carry out a reconnaissance to the north and the east of Cape Bon to establish enemy convoy routes in that area and to find out the positions of enemy minefields (HMS Ursula was fitted with a MDU (Mine Detector Unit)).
The enemy convoy was thought to have gone past or evaded the position of HMS Ursula by that time. (1)

20 Mar 1941 In a signal timed 1403 B/20, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) that a convoy of six merchant vessels escorted by four destroyers had passed Kelibia Roads Light southwards at 0945 B/20.

This referred to the convoy made up of the German transports Arcturus (2576 GRT, built 1937), Procida (1842 GRT, built 1927), Santa Fe (4627 GRT, built 1921) and Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the Italian destroyers Saetta, Fulmine and Baleno on passage from Naples (departed 0050-0130 B/19) to Tripoli (arrived 1327-1535 B/21).
In a signal timed 1153 C/20, the Capt. S.1, ordered HMS Ursula and HMS Utmost that if the convoy is escorted by a cruiser the primary object is to attack the transports.

In a signal timed 1811 B/20, the Vice-Admiral Malta, informed HMS Ursula and HMS Utmost of the results of the air reconnaissance on this day in the Sicilian Channel. The following had been reported (All times GMT) (A) One M.V. 5000 ton in position 36°20'N, 12° 20'E, course 270° at 1046 hours (B) One large M.V. in position 37°10'N, 11°40'E, course 180° at 1208 hours (C) Four destroyers and five 5 large M.V’s, 20 miles north-west of Pantellaria, course 350° with flying boat escort at 1215 hours
At 1845 B/20, smoke was sighted on the horizon bearing 206° at an estimated range of 10 miles. This was probably the convoy referred to in the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1403 B/20.

At 2300 B/20, HMS Utmost proceeded to patrol in an area bounded by 35°00'N to 35°40'N and 12°10'E to 13°00'E in accordance with the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1441 B/20. In this signal she was also ordered not to enter this area before 0600 B/21. (32)
24 Mar 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) makes a torpedo attack on a convoy to the east of Cape Bon, Tunisia in position 37°05'N, 11°13'E. No hits were obtained.

The convoy attacked was most likely the one made up of the Italian transports San Luigi (4419 GRT, built 1905) and Tenace (1219 GRT, built 1881) and was escorted by the torpedo boat Generale Achille Papa.
This convoy had departed Palermo, Sicily at 0810 B/23-0900 B/23 and arrived at Tripoli at 0830 B/26.
0330 B/24 - Picked up HE.
0333 B/24 - Sighted a merchant vessel against the hazy horizon. Closed.
0345 B/24 - Sighted a torpedo boat coming towards. Dived to avoid detection.
0350 B/24 - The torpedo boat passed close.

0357 B/24 - Surfaced. Sighted one large merchant vessel of 10000 tons and one smaller merchant vessel of 3000 tons in line ahead. Range was 3000-4000 yards. Started attack on the larger merchant vessel.

0427 B/24 - In position 37°05'N, 11°13'E fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards. Just as the fourth torpedo left the tube the target altered course 45° away to a course of 185°. All torpedoes now missed the target. Ursula meanwhile chased the convoy bit a firing position could not be reached before sunrise.

0510 B/24 - Dived on seeing a small vessel, looking like a MAS boat on the port quarter at a range of only 300 yards. A noise had been heard earlier which was at first thought to be an aircraft but was now thought to have been another MAS boat. [No details available to us on possible MAS boat operations in the area.]
0525 B/24 - Surfaced as no aggressive action had been undertaken by the possible MAS boats.
0600 B/24 - Dived in position 37°06'N, 11°13'E.

1239 A/24 - In approximate position 37°15'N, 11°11'E, sighted a small motor vessel of about 2000 tons bearing 100°. The vessel was proceeding on a course of 040°. Range was 12000 yards. It was not possible to close the range sufficiently to attack with the deck gun of torpedoes. (1)

25 Mar 1941 Around 1000 B/25, while patrolling to the north-east of Cape Bon, Tunisia, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) heard approximately thirty very loud explosions. It was thought that HMS Rorqual (Cdr R.H. Dewhurst, DSO, RN), which might be to the north of HMS Ursula was being depth charged. This was not the case as HMS Rorqual was near Palermo, Sicily on this day.

The Italian auxiliary minesweepers Sinibaldo, Nettuno, Agata, Madre and Domenicelli sailed from Trapani at 0455 B/25 for Pantelleria. They could have been not very far from HMS Ursula but no A/S activities were reported in the Supermarina Diaries. [Reports of the small auxiliary minesweepers are not available to us.]
At 1300 A/25, what is thought to be a tanker was seen proceeding southwards. She was hugging the coast about half a mile from the shore. This vessel is believed to have been Vichy-French. (1)
26 Mar 1941 At 0720 B/26, while patrolling to the north-north-east of Cape Bon, Tunisia, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) picked up HE bearing 090°.
At 0730 B/26, the topmasts of two destroyers were sighted. HMS Ursula closed at full submerged speed to investigate. It was thought these may be escorts of a convoy.

At 0750 B/26, a convoy was now visible which consisted of four medium sized motor vessels of about 6000 tons each escorted by three Spica-class torpedo boats. Enemy course was 190°. An attack was not possible as HMS Ursula was already abaft the beam of the rear ship at a range of 7000 yards and the attack was broken off at 0810 B/26.

This convoy was made up of the Italian transports Amsterdam (8673 GRT, built 1921), Col di Lana (5891 GRT, built 1926), Capo Orso (3149 GRT, built 1916), Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1925) and Caffaro (6476 GRT, built 1924) screened by the Italian torpedo boats Circe (escort leader), Clio and Calliope. They sailed from Naples at 0300 B/25-0450 B/25 at 10.5 knots for Tripoli. At 1915 B/25, off Cape San Vito, they were reinforced by the torpedo boat Pegaso and at 1955 B/25, Circe was detached to Trapani, arriving at 2348 B/25 while Clio assumed the role of escort leader. According to their sailing orders, they were expected to be off Cape Bon at 0830 B/26. At 2145 B/26-2152 B/26, the torpedo boats Centauro and Castore sailed from Tripoli and at 0723 B/27, joined the convoy to reinforce it in its last leg. They arrived at Tripoli at 1345 B/27-1452 B/27. Thus, at the time of the sighting by HMS Ursula, the convoy consisted of five transports (not four), screened by Clio, Calliope and Pegaso. (1)

28 Mar 1941 At 0555 B/28, to the east of Cape Bon, Tunisia, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) dived in position 37°04'N, 11°15'E and adjusted courses to pass through the suspected minefield (QBB 86). The Mine Detector Unit (MDU) was run for five minutes every twenty minutes. No mines were detected. Passage was made at 70 feet coming up to periscope depth for all round searches and to fix position every half-hour.

At 1150 B/28, while in position 36°53'N, 11°12'E, HMS Ursula sighted smoke to the northward. A Vichy-French armed trawler had been seen off Cape Bon three hours earlier and it was thought she was the source of the smoke.

At 1158 B/28, two loud explosions were heard. On looking an aircraft was seen circling around and HMS Ursula must have been sighted from the air as a smoke bomb or smoke float had been dropped about 75 feet away. Went deep.
At 1214 B/28, three more explosions were heard.

At 1247 B/28, HMS Ursula returned to periscope depth to investigate the situation. The sea was glassy calm. An Italian torpedo boat of the single-funnel type was seen about 3000 yards away. No HE was picked up. The enemy was apparently searching. Two, possibly three, aircraft were also searching in the area. Went deep again.

At 1305 B/28, HMS Ursula returned to periscope depth and the torpedo boat was seen to leave the area but she was still proceeding at slow speed, apparently still hunting. Range was between 6000 and 8000 yards. Two more Spica-class torpedo boats were seen bearing 120° at a range of 12000 yards. Also a convoy was seen of at least two but probably more, merchant vessels. These appeared to be single funnel transports of about 6000 tons each. The convoy's course was 160°. An attack was not possible from the position HMS Ursula was in. It was now considered that HMS Ursula had been sighted by an aircraft that was patrolling in front of the convoy and that the convoy had made a large alteration of course to the eastward to evade. One of the escorts apparently been detached to hunt the submarine / keep the submarine down.

This was the 15th Seetransportstaffel with the German transports Adana (4176 GRT, built 1922), Heraklea (1927 GRT, built 1922), Samos (2576 GRT, built 1923), Ruhr (5954 GRT, built 1926) and the Italian transport Galilea (8040 GRT, built 1916) escorted by Italian destroyers Dardo and Folgore and Strale which sailed from Naples at 2030 B/26-2345 B/26. At 2203 B/28, Heraklea and Ruhr were hit by torpedoes, the former sank, the latter was taken in tow by Dardo and arrived at Trapani at 1845 B/30. The torpedo boats Polluce and Partenope sailed from Tripoli at 0940 B/29 to reinforce the escort. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 0700 B/30-0820 B/30.
At 1600 A/28, HMS Ursula was clear of area QBB 86. As the presence of HMS Ursula in this area was now compromised set course for the north-east corner of area 'L 2'. (1)
29 Mar 1941 At 0845 B/29, to the south-west of Pantelerria, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) picked up HE to port from a very high revving source. Nothing was seen.
At 0900 B/29, similar HE was heard to starboard and it was seen to be a MAS boat proceeding to the southward at high speed.
At 0945 B/29, more HE as from a MAS boat was heard astern. During the day more HE from fast small craft was heard on several occasions.
[We have no info in these possible MAS boat patrols. The Supermarina War Diaries do not give many MAS boat movements.]
At 1645 B/29, HMS Ursula shaped course to proceed to the known convoy route to the south of Kelibia. (1)

30 Mar 1941 At 0001 B/30, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a darkened ship on the port quarter. No HE was picked up. HMS Ursula turned to close and attack. It was hoped that this object was an enemy submarine but the vessel was soon identified as a small darkened three-masted schooner which turned and pointed towards. It was thought this may have been the 'Q-ship' HMS Ursula was warned about in a signal of 19 March. Also HMS Ursula was to the north of the 'free for all' area and it was not allowed to sink, without warning, what may have been a Vichy-French fishing vessel.
At 0027 B/30, HMS Ursula dived in position 36°39'N, 11°12'E and turned away.
At 0121 B/30, HMS Ursula surfaced. A darkened vessel was seen astern and this one was identified as a small torpedo boat proceeding at slow speed and zigging frequently, apparently searching.
At 0140 B/30, HMS Ursula dived in position 36°40'N, 11°11'E and altered course to the northward.
At 0310 B/30, HMS Ursula surfaced in position 36°44'N, 11°26'E. The torpedo boat was now on the port quarter at a range of 2000 yards. HMS Ursula turned end on and proceeded to the eastward.
At 0545 B/30, having cleared the area, HMS Ursula dived in position 36°43'N, 11°22'E.

At 1715 B/30, HE was heard to the eastward which subsequently moved into a southerly direction and faded out. Nothing was seen through the periscope. The weather was clear with a visibility of about 7 miles.

At 2043 B/30, HMS Ursula surfaced in position 36°35'N, 11°48'E, and left patrol to return to Malta proceeding through positions 36°30'N, 12°20'E and 35°44'N, 14°20'E as had been ordered in the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1125 B/28. (33)
1 Apr 1941 Around 0810 B/1, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (1)

9 Apr 1941 Around 1430 B/9, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was to patrol in area 'H' off Kelibia, 36°10'N, 11°00'E, 000° - 40 - 40.

10 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 1728 B/10, to HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) the Cdr. S.1's stated that a convoy was in position 36°10'N, 11°58'E (south of Pantelleria) at 1540 B/10 and that Swordfish torpedo aircraft would be operating against it after 2200 B/10.

This convoy was expected to be off Pantelleria at 1230 B/10 (sailing orders). These were the Italian transports Andrea Gritti (6338 GRT, built 1939), Barbarigo (5293 GRT, built 1930), Birmania (5305 GRT, built 1930), Rialto (6099 GRT, built 1927) and Sebastiano Venier (6310 GRT, built 1940). It was escorted by the destroyer Dardo and the torpedo boats Clio and Enrico Cosenz. They had sailed from Naples at 1300-1445 B/9 and were expected to be off Marettimo at 0800 B/10. The torpedo boat Generale Achille Papa sailed from Palermo at 2350 B/9 to reinforce the convoy, while Enrico Cosenz was detached to Trapani, arriving at 1445 B/10. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1100-1150 B/11.
It was expected by Lt. Mackenzie that this convoy would proceed through HMS Ursula's area around 1900 B/10. The convoy was however neither seen nor heard. (34)
12 Apr 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) attacked a convoy South of Cape Bon in approximate position 36°40'N, 11°12'E. A hit is claimed but actually no ships were hit.

This convoy was made up of the German transports Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937), Marburg (7564 GRT, built 1928), Kybfels (7764 GRT, built 1937), Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936) and the Italian transport Galilea (8040 GRT, built 1916). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli and Lanzerotto Malocello. Reichenfels reported being missed by about 10-25 metres.

This convoy had sailed from Naples at 1530 B/11-1730 B/11 for Tripoli. It was carrying the first elements of the 15th Panzer Division, was expected to be off Kuriat at 2100 B/12 and off Lampedusa at 0330 B/13 and arrive at Tripoli at 1600 B/13 (Vivaldi sailing orders). At 2030 B/12, the convoy had been sighted by enemy aircraft 10 miles north-east of Kuriat. They were under air attack without success at 2315 B/12 and 0012 B/13. They waited for the arrival of air escort provided by X.Fliegerkorps before resuming their course to Tripoli and finally arrived at 0600 B/14-1000 B/14.
1715 B/12 - Sighted an Italian Navigatori-class destroyer bearing 350°, range approximate 10000 yards. Started attack.

1725 B/12 - It was now seen that the destroyer was escorting a convoy of five merchant ships of about 6000 tons. Two destroyers were present. The target that was selected was the second ship of the port column.
1735 B/12 - Two single engined aircraft were seen overhead, most likely fighters.
1739 B/12 - In approximate position 36°40'N, 11°12'E fired four torpedoes from 2500 yards.
1747 B/12 - One explosion was heard that sounded like a hit. Given the range to the target it was thought that a hit was obtained on the third ship in the starboard column.
1756 B/12 - Three explosions were heard, thought to be the remained of the torpedo salvo exploding upon hitting the bottom at the end of their run.
1801 B/12- Fairly loud explosions were heard, thought to be depth charges. No further depth charges were dropped.

1820 B/12 - Returned to periscope depth. A Navigatori-class destroyer was seen near the position where the torpedo would have hit the merchant vessel. The convoy was hull down to the southward. After starting and stopping a couple of times the Navigatori-class destroyer swept round in a large circle at revs. for 18 knots and then turned south to rejoin the convoy.

2124 B/12 - Received the Cdr. S.1's signal imed 1110 B/12 which ordered HMS Ursula to patrol in area 'L' off Cape Bon (37°10'N, 10°55'E - 000° - 40 - 30). Course was set to comply with these orders. (1)

18 Apr 1941 At 1540 B/18, north-west of Pantelleria, in position 37°06'N, 11°46'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) picked up HE bearing 050°. This was seen to originate from a small patrol vessel of about 400 tons which was proceeding towards Pantelleria from the westward. The enemy passed four miles to the northward of HMS Ursula.

At 1700 B/18, while in position 37°00'N, 11°45'E, HE was reported on bearing 230°. This was seen to be the Italian torpedo boat Ernesto Giovannini making for Pantelleria from the westward. The enemy passed three miles to the northward of HMS Ursula.

At 1900 B/18, while in position 36°58'N, 11°47'E, HE was reported and this was seen to originate from a small armed tug of about 100 tons making for Pantelleria from the westward. The enemy passed at a range of one mile.

At 2222 B/18, HMS Ursula left patrol to return to Malta. She was to proceed through the following positions on the way back; 37°18'N, 11°17'E, 37°18'N, 11°33'E, 36°56'N, 11°51'E and 36°48'N. 12°16'E. (35)
20 Apr 1941 Around 0645 B/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (1)
27 Apr 1941 Around 1200 B/27, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Kerkennah.

She was ordered to patrol between Kerkennah, Tunisia and Tripoli, Libya on a patrol line 4 - 34°40'N, 11°40'E - 090° - 10 - 180° - 40 with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) in position 'W' (34°40'N, 11°40'E), HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) in position 'X' (34°40'N, 11°52'E), HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) in position 'Y' (34°40'N, 12°04'E) and HMS Truant (Lt.Cdr. H.A.V. Haggard, RN) in position 'Z' (34°40'N, 12°18'E). This patrol line was deployed to intercept an important southbound enemy convoy.

This convoy was made up of the transports Marburg (German, 7564 GRT, built 1928), Kibfels (German, 7764 GRT, built 1937), Reichenfels (German, 7744 GRT, built 1936), Birmania (Italian, 5305 GRT, built 1930) and Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927) from Naples to Tripoli. It was escorted by the Italian destroyers Fulmine, Euro and the Italian torpedo boats Orione, Castore and Procione. A distant cover force made up of the heavy cruisers Trieste, Bolzano, light cruiser Eugenio di Savoia and the destroyers Ascari, Carabiniere and Vincenzo Gioberti was also deployed.
