The convoy was however diverted to Italian ports due to the threat of Allied surface ships. It was expected to continue it's voyage at a later date.

28 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 2305 B/28, the Cdr. S.1 sent the following orders to the four submarines on the patrol line, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN), HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) and HMS Truant (Lt.Cdr. H.A.V. Haggard, RN); While submerged submarines should patrol north and south in the vicinity of their respective positions and not east and west. Freedom of action allowed at night as weather conditions dictate. Today's air rec reconnaissance report was blank. (37)

1 May 1941 Between 1440 B/1 and 1530 B/1, several depth charge explosions were heard by HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN). A torpedo boat was seen bearing 260° at a range of about 4 miles. She was most likely hunting. Position was 34°37'N, 11°51'E.
The sighting of this torpedo boat is a bit odd, as no escort was detached from the convoy attacked by HMS Undaunted to hunt any submarine.
At 1520 B/1, the torpedo was seen to alter course to 360° leave the area.
Intermittent explosions were heard until 2015 B/1.
At 1912 B/1, turbine HE was heard on bearing 293° but nothing could be seen through the periscope.
At 2018 B/1, very fast HE was picked up on bearing 315° but again nothing was seen.

At the time it was thought that HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) was being hunted. HMS Upholder however claims that she was not being counter attacked. [It is unlikely that the A/S action heard and seen by HMS Ursula has anything to do with HMS Undaunted (Lt. J.L. Livesey, RN) which did not return from patrol. HMS Undaunted was to the east of HMS Ursula and had reported and attacked the expected enemy southbound convoy early in the afternoon.]
At 2117 B/1, HMS Ursula received the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1237 B/1, which ordered her to patrol in area 'K', 034°20'N, 11°4O'E - 000° - 40 - 40. Course was set to comply with these orders. (38)

2 May 1941 In a signal timed 1446 B/2, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) of the following; Convoy attacked by Blenheims in position 34°42'N, 12°23'E. Three merchant ships and one destroyer hit and stooped.
In a follow up signal timed 1608 B/2, HMS Ursula was ordered to close and sink the damaged ships.
This last signal was received by HMS Ursula at 1740 B/2. She surfaced and proceeded at full speed towards the position of the damaged ships.
At 2200 B/2, HMS Ursula was in position 34°42'N, 12°23'E. She searched all night but no enemy ships were seen.

This convoy, consisting of the German transports Tilly M. Russ (1600 GRT, built 1926), Brook (1225 GRT, built 1927), the Italian transport Bainsizza (7933 GRT, built 1930), the Italian tanker Sanandrea (5077 GRT, built 1908) and the German tug Max Berendt (766 GRT, built 1923) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Clio and Enrico Cosenz sailed from Palermo at 2200 B/30 April-0000 B/1 May. The Italian torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari sailed from Trapani at 0646 B/1 and at 0843 B/1 joined off Marettimo the convoy which had sailed from Palermo and detached to Trapani the torpedo boat Cosenz which arrived at 1100 B/1. At 1716 B/1, off Pantelleria, the convoy was reinforced by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Ramb III (3667 GRT, built 1938). At 1300 B/2, the convoy was attacked by four Blenheim bombers of 21 Squadron (RAF) without suffering any damage (the bombers claimed more than four direct hits!). The torpedo boats Centauro and Polluce sailed from Tripoli at 1314 B/2-1325 B/2 and at 1900 B/2 reinforced the escort. They arrived at Tripoli at 0750 B/3-0920 B/3. (39)
4 May 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) attacked an armed merchant vessel or auxiliary with four torpedoes near the Kerkennah bank in approximate position 34°36'N, 11°54'E.
This was the Italian transport Palermo (former Greek Athinai, 2897 GRT, built 1910) who sighted three torpedo tracks.

Palermo was carrying phosphate; she was not an AMC. She had sailed from Naples for Sfax at 0700 B/21 at 9.5 knots. She sailed from Sfax for Palermo on 4 May but returned the same day after the torpedo attack. She finally sailed at 0900 B/14 May.

1405 B/4 - Sighted a merchant vessel, bearing 030°. Enemy course was 260°. Closed to attack. The target was seen to be a merchant vessel or auxiliary of about 5000 tons. She was armed with two guns on the poop and painted grey.

1428 B/4 - In approximate position 34°36'N, 11°54'E fired four torpedoes from 2100 yards. Most likely all torpedoes missed astern as she zigged away and her speed was about 14 knots while during the attack 10 knots was estimated. (36)

6 May 1941 In a signal timed 1300 B/6, the Cdr. S.1, ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) to leave area 'K' at dusk the following day and proceed through position 35°50'N, 11°30'E to operate in area 'H'. North of 36°N and west of 011°20'E. (40)

7 May 1941 At 1120 B/7, east-north-east of Kerkennah, in position 34°51'N, 11°56'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a three-masted topsail schooner bearing 235° at a range of 8 miles. The schooner was not attacked.

In a signal timed 1855 B/7, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Ursula of the following; Information received three-masted sailing ship attempted salvage from wreck near Kerkennah No.3 buoy. This sailing ship was suspected to be a well-armed Q ship.
This signal may well have referred to the schooner seen by HMS Ursula at 1120 B/7.
[We have been unable to identify this schooner.] (38)

7 May 1941 In a signal timed 1302 B/7, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) of the following; Eight unescorted merchant ships in position 36°06'N, 11° 57'E. Course 180° at 6 knots at 1100 B/7. Blenheim aircraft are proceeding to attack.

This referred to the following convoy; At 0500 B/4, the steamer Petsamo (Italian (a trawler-type vessel), 305 GRT, built 1924), schooner Maria Immacolata (Italian, 248 GRT, built 1921), the naval tanker (water carrier) Flegetonte (Italian, 611 GRT, built 1917) and the Italian minesweeper RD 14 sailed from Trapani for Pantelleria where they arrived in the afternoon.

At 0400 B/6-0540 B/6, a convoy consisted of the steamer Pro Patria (549 GRT, built 1905) escorted by the Italian auxiliary gunboats Scilla (470 tons, built 1904), Valoroso (434 tons, built 1929) and Mario Bianco (385 tons, built 1911) sailed from Trapani for Tripoli via Pantelleria where they arrived later in the day. At 0545 B/7, the two convoys merged and sailed from Pantelleria for Tripoli. At 1340 B/7-1345 B/7, they came under air attack by five Blenheim bombers of 21 Squadron (RAF) escorted by three Beaufighters. The bombers sighted eight small vessels with two escorts in position 35°50'N, 12°14'E and claimed hits on a 3000-ton and a 1500-ton merchant vessel; the first was left on fire, and the other was listing badly. In fact, all the Italian vessels escaped damage or casualties. They took refuge in Lampedusa on the 7th. Swordfish were sent to attack them there, but found that the attack was impossible, and only one Swordfish dropped a torpedo at the entrance of Lampedusa harbour. The convoy only reached Tripoli at 1800 B/12.

Other convoys at sea on this day were the following; At 2200 B/7-2315 B/7, a convoy consisting of the Italian transports Ernesto (7399 GRT, built 1914), Col di Lana (5891 GRT, built 1926), Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1925) and the German transports Wachtfels (8467 GRT, 1928) and Preussen (8224 GRT, built 1922), screened by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Grecale, Camicia Nera sailed from Naples for Tripoli (Geniere sailed at 0300 B/8 and returned at 1632 B/9). They turned back when British naval forces were reported in the Western Mediterranean (Force H and the Tiger-convoy), and arrived at Naples at 1843 B/8-2400 B/8. They sailed again at 2200 B/10-2315 B/10 and arrived at Tripoli at 0951 B/13-1200 B/13.

At 0800 B/5, a convoy consisting of the steamers Giuseppe Leva (Italian, 1430 GRT, built 1907), Giovinezza (Italian, 2362 GRT, built 1925) and Cadamosto (italian, 1010 GRT, built 1905), escorted by the italian torpedo boat Calliope sailed from Palermo for Tripoli. The Italian torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz sailed from Trapani at 1700 B/5 and reinforced the escort. They were diverted to Trapani where they arrived at 0552 B/7. They sailed again at 1500 B/11, this time joined by the transport Nita (Italian 6813 GRT, built 1913), escorted by the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz and reinforced by Circe which had sailed from Palermo at 0840 B/11. They were joined by the torpedo boats Giuseppe Sirtori and Generale Carlo Montanari which sailed from Tripoli at 1825 B/11-2020 B/11 and first reinforced the Calitea convoy. They arrived at Tripoli at 1620 B/13-1720 B/13. (40)

8 May 1941 At 2100 B/8, off the Gulf of Hammamet, Tunisia, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) surfaced in position 34°51'N, 11°56'E after a day of submerged patrol in position 36°06'N, 11°10'E. Course was then set to return to Malta. (38)
11 May 1941 Around 0715 B/11, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (36)

24 May 1941 Around 1930 B/24, HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN in command as her regular Commanding Officer Lt. Mackenzie was sick) departed Malta for her 20th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the approaches to Tripoli, Libya between longitudes 15°E and 12°E.

On departure from Malta it had been intended to carry out a practice attack on HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR) but due to a defect in the minesweeper this was not possible. It was intended to carry out a practice attack on HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) when she would leave Malta half an hour after HMS Ursula but it was too dark to carry out a submerged attack on a submarine and HMS Ursula surfaced and proceeded on patrol without having made a practice attack. (36)
24 May 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ursula during her her 20th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) see the map below.

25 May 1941 In a signal timed 0055 B/25, the Cdr. S.1 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) to make her best speed during darkness tonight. Two or three escorted transports may he encountered approaching from N.E. at dawn. HMS Ursula received this signal at 0305 B/25. She ceased zig-zagging, came to full buoyancy and was able to make 10.5 knots until having to dive at 0520 B/25 due to daylight being almost in.

This referred to the convoy made up of the Italian troop transports (liners) Conte Rosso (17879 GRT, built 1922), Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912), Victoria (13098 GRT, built 1931) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920), escorted by the Italian destroyer Freccia and the Italian torpedo boats Procione, Orsa and Pegaso which sailed from Naples at 0250 B/24-0440 B/24. At 1610 B/24, the convoy crossed the Straits of Messina, reinforced by the Italian torpedo boats Calatafimi and Perseo which sailed from Messina at 1500 B/24-1517 B/24 and Calliope which had sailed from Trapani at 0500 B/24. The three torpedo boats only escorted them for a few hours through the Straits of Messina passage and returned to Messina at 2045 B/24-2100 B/24. The Italian heavy cruisers Trieste, Bolzano (Third Cruiser Division) and the Italian destroyers Corazziere, Lanciere and Ascari sailed from Messina at 1535 B/24-1650 B/24 to provide a close screen. At 2040 B/24, in position 270° - Murro di Porco - 10 miles, the Conte Rosso was torpedoed and sunk by HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN). Freccia had given the alarm and all ships turned to starboard, except Conte Rosso which turned to port and was torpedoed. Procione picked up 542 survivors (7 died on board) and brought them to Augusta at 0610 B/25, Pegaso followed at 0800 B/25. The destroyers Corazziere and Lanciere and the Italian hospital ship Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) (which was arriving from Benghazi) and later the Italian hospital Sicilia (9646 GRT, built 1924) also proceeded to search for survivors, the two destroyers arriving at Augusta at 0315 B/25 and then returned to Messina at 1934 B/25-2035 B/25. There were ,297 victims from a total of 2729. At 0125 B/25, the Italian torpedo boats Cigno, Pallade and Clio sailed from Messina to join the search, they returned at 1720 B/25. The Third Cruiser Division, the liners Marco Polo, Esperia and Victoria screened by Freccia, Ascari and Orsa proceeded on. On the morning of the 25th, Trieste, Bolzano and Ascari turned back and arrived at Messina at 1935 B/25-2020 B/25. At 2003 B/24, the Italian torpedo boats Generale Carlo Montanari and at 0520 B/25, Climene sailed from Tripoli and at 1230 B/25 reinforced the convoy. At 0900 B/25, the Italian torpedo boat Castore sailed from Tripoli and joined the convoy at 1410 B/25, taking the lead to pilot it to Tripoli. At 1351 B/25, in position 33°13'N, 13°59'E, Climene sighted an oil slick and attacked it with depth charges, claiming to have sighted a submarine briefly breaking surface and claiming it sunk [bogus as there was no submarine in this area] before rejoining the convoy at 1424 B/25. The three liners screened by Freccia, Orsa, Castore, Generale Carlo Montanari and Climene arrived at Tripoli at 1642 B/25-1754 B/25.?

At 1315 B/25, a signal from the Naval Officer in Command, Malta was received which reported the following; Two 8" and one 6" cruiser in position 35°04'N, 15°33'E, at 1040 B/25 on course 010° at 25 knots. They altered courser to 020° at 1048 B/25.
It was estimated that these cruisers had passed about 20 miles to the southward of HMS Ursula during the forenoon. (40)

26 May 1941 At 2358 B/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) briefly grounded in position 030° - Khoms light - 1 mile. By blowing main ballast and going astern she quickly came off. At the time of the grounding was investigating a small tug at anchor close to the Khoms breakwater. As the target was very small it was decided it was not worth to bother this vessel. (36)
27 May 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) attacked a merchant vessel with two torpedoes in position in position 32°33'N, 14°47'E. No hits were obtained.

This was probably the transport Silvio Scaroni (Italian, 1394 GRT, built 1920) on passage from Benghazi to Tripoli. This vessel had sailed from Benghazi on 25 May (time unknown) for Tripoli where she arrived at 0700 B/28.
HMS Ursula also tried to intercept damaged ships from an enemy convoy but nothing was sighted.

These were from a convoy which consisted the transports Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6338 GRT, built 1939), Marco Foscarini (Italian, 6342 GRT, built 1940), Barbarigo (Italian, 5293 GRT, built 1930), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940), Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937), screened by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli and Saetta which sailed from Naples at 1530 B/25-2300 B/25-0203 B/26-0230 B/26 through the Straits of Messina. The Italian torpedo boat Cigno sailed from Messina at 1010 B/26 to relieve Saetta which turned back at 1323 B/26 and arrived at Naples at 2048 B/26. At 1556 B/26-1704 B/26, the Third Cruiser Division (made up of the Italian heavy cruisers Trieste and Gorizia screened by the Italian destroyers Ascari, Lanciere and Corazziere) sailed from Messina to cover them until they met the returning Conte Rosso convoy (actually the Victoria convoy as Conte Rosso had been sunk by HMS Upholder). At about 1300 B/27, the convoy came under air attack by six Blenheim bombers, five from 82 Squadron (RAF) and one from 139 Squadron (RAF), each armed with four 250-lb bombs which attacked eight merchant vessels, six destroyers and two cruisers and claimed seven direct hits. Marco Foscarini was indeed hit by two bombs (one killed) and set afire, the destroyer Antonio da Noli stood by her. The Italian torpedo boat Pleiadi sailed from Tripoli at 2320 B/27 to screen her. The tugs Salvatore Io and Pronta followed on 28-29 April to tow her. The Third Cruiser Squadron returned to Messina at 1335 B/28-1514 B/28. Pleiadi had returned to Tripoli at 1420 B/28, she was relieved by the Italian torpedo boat Pallade which sailed from Tripoli at 0808 B/29. Marco Foscarini was towed to Tripoli, arriving at 1500 B/30 where she was beached and became a total loss. Pleiadi was lost on 30 April when an Italian seaplane collided with her as it was taking off causing a serious fire which detonated ammunition. At 2030 B/28. Sebastiano Venier was also hit by a bomb which failed to explode. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 2325 B/27-0015 B/28, except Antonio da Noli which had screened Marco Foscarini until the danger appeared to have subsided and reached Tripoli at 2030 B/28.

1308 B/27 - Received a signal from the Cdr. S.1 timed 1227 B/27, ordering HMS Ursula to intercept an enemy convoy. Set course accordingly. This convoy had been sighted at 1107 B in position 34°20'N, 15°50'E. The convoy was made up of eight merchant ships escorted by two cruisers and six destroyers. Course of the convoy was 205° speed 13 knots.
1337 B/27 - Sighted smoke bearing 090°. Interception was only possible if Ursula proceeded on the surface when she was well clear of the coast.
1400 B/27 to 1600 B/27 - Heard several explosions as the RAF was attacking the convoy.

1615 B/27 - Well clear of the coast now so surfaced in position 32°33'N, 14°47'E to reach a position to intercept the convoy however upon surfacing a small merchant vessel of about 2000 tons was sighted about 5 nautical miles away. Ursula dived again. Decided to attack this vessel as it seemed likely that Ursula was sighted and it would now be impossible to intercept the convoy.
1706 B/27 - Fired two torpedoes from 3000 yards. No hits were obtained.
1715 B/27 - Prepared to surface and engage the target with gunfire.
1720 B/27 - When about to surface the vessel turned stern on and opened fire with the stern gun in the direction of the periscope. Decided not to surface and abandon the action.
1907 B/27 - Surfaced and proceeded as fast as possible towards position 'B' hoping to intercept any bomb-damaged ships following the convoy in.
2042 B/27 - Dived for two unidentified aircraft surfacing again at 2055 B/27 and continued the passage at full surfaced speed.

2109 B/27 - Received the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1930 B/27 which stated the following; Anticipate convoy arriving in position 'A' at 2330 B/27. At 1550 B/27, Place, course and speed of the convoy were 33°48'N, 14°44'E, 210°, 10 knots. The convoy was escorted by six destroyers. The cruiser had returned to the northward. If impossible to intercept in position 'A', try position 'B'. Expect the escort to be reinforced from Tripoli.

2233 B/27 - Received the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 2145 B/27 addressed to HMS Union (Lt. R.M. Galloway, RN) which started the following; Proceed to investigate two merchant ships successfully attacked by aircraft at 1550 B/27 in position 33°48'N, 14°44'E. Two escorting cruisers sighted retiring northwards may return to salvage. Possibility of RAF survivors from two Blenheims. On completion return to area 'O'.
The above signal confirmed the air attack on the convoy and that ships had been damaged.
Around 0215 B/28, HMS Ursula arrived in the vicinity of position 'B' but nothing was sighted of the enemy all day. (36)

28 May 1941 In a signal timed 2217 B/28 [unable to find the text of this signal], HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) was informed by the Cdr. S.1 that enemy cruisers were to the northward. Lt. McGeoch decided to continue proceeding westwards to cover the approaches to Tripoli. (36)

30 May 1941 At 0920 B/30, in approximate position 32°58'N, 12°12'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat and a Generale-class torpedo boat leaving Zuara (Zuwârah), Libya. They were proceeding in line ahead, two cables apart. They set a steady course 100° at 24 knots.
These were the Italian torpedo boats Antonio Mosto and Cigno which sailed from Sfax, Tunisia, at 2132 B/29-2150 B/29 and arrived at Tripoli at 1345 B/30-1350 B/30.

What were these ships doing in a French Port ?!; Antonio Mosto had sailed from Trapani at 1530 B/21 May to escort the Italian steamer Florida II (3313 GRT, built 1905) to Tripoli. The weather was very bad. At 0025 B/24, in heavy rain and low visibility, the torpedo boat ran aground near Kerkennah Buoy no.7. She managed to free herself, but with only 40 tons of fuel left, the torpedo boat was unable to proceed to her destination. The two vessels dropped anchor in the Sfax roads at 1918 B/24. They had barely done so when they were attacked by two Blenheim bombers of 139 Squadron, Florida II was hit and was rocked by a large explosion (two killed, five wounded) and was badly damaged. She was finished off by three Blenheim bombers of 82 Squadron (RAF) on 31 May. In the meantime, Admiral Farina of the CIAF (Italian Armistice Commission with France) requested from French Admiral Ollive (Amiral Sud – Commanding the Mediterranean Theatre) that the French Navy supply 50 tons of fuel to the torpedo boat. Ollive had accepted but the French Navy had no fuel stocks in Sfax, only fuel for civilian use was available and the French were reluctant to part from it. Only water and food supplies were provided. Finally, the Italian torpedo boat Cigno sailed from Tripoli at 1420 B/28 and arrived at Sfax at 1300 B/29 and went alongside Antonio Mosto to cede fuel to her. This was completed at 1730 B/29, and they sailed in company four hours later. (36)
31 May 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel off Zuara (Zuwârah), Libya in approximate position 33°00'N, 12°09'E with two torpedoes. No hits were obtained.
The target was probably the German transport Sparta (1724 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Pilo.
0540 B/31 - Sighted a laden merchant vessel of about 4000 tons escorted by a 'Generale-class' torpedo boat 5 cables ahead of her. Enemy course was 145°. Range was 4000 yards. Started attack.
0553 B/31 - In approximate position 33°00'N, 12°09'E fired two torpedoes from 1500 yards. No hits were obtained. It appeared the attack was not noticed. HMS Ursula resumed her patrol off Zuara. (36)

1 Jun 1941 At 0905 B/1, in approximate position 32°59'N, 12°10'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) sighted smoke and the upperworks of a small vessel leaving Zuara steering a course of approximately 100°.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 1500 B/1, a Generale-class torpedo boat was seen leaving Zuara. She was zigging on a base course of 340° at 20 knots. Later she changed course to a base course of 320°.

This was either the torpedo boat Climene [Spica-class though !] which sailed from Tripoli at 0740 B/1 and returned at 1752 B/1 or the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori [The lead ship of her class but these looked very similar to the Generale-class. Both classed had three funnels] which sailed from Tripoli for an A/S search at 1000 B/1 and returned at 0745 B/2. It seems thus more likely it was the latter if the identification was correct.

At 1634 B/1, sighted a convoy coming from the direction of point 'E' (32°52'N, 12°27'E) approaching point 'F' (33°00'N, 12°09'E). The convoy was made up of a seagoing tug, a coastal tanker of about 900 tons in ballast or lightly loaded, a coastal tanker of about 800 tons in ballast or lightly loaded. They were escorted by a naval trawler. The convoy was roughly in line ahead at four cables apart. It was considered that this convoy was not worth comprising the submarines position. The convoy altered course to 320° at point 'F' and steered a steady course at 7 knots.

This must have been the convoy made up of was made up of the Italian naval auxiliaries Arsia (736 GRT, built 1900) and Costanza (582 GRT, built 1902) escorted by the Italian auxiliary minesweeper (former tug) G.61/ Costante Neri (100 GRT, built 1914) and the Italian auxiliary gunboat (trawler) Grazioli Lante (295 GRT, built 1912). They sailed from Tripoli at 0525 B/1 and arrived at Lampedusa at 1910 B/2. Arsia was torpedoed and sunk by HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) on 3 June at the entrance of the harbour of Lampedusa. Grazioli Lante and probably the two other vessels made eventual passage to Pantelleria (8-9 June) and arrived at Trapani at 1855 B/9 June. (41)
3 Jun 1941 At 0330 B/3, the starboard generator of HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) became defective. It proved to be impossible to effect repairs at sea. (36)

3 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 1110 B/3, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN), HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) that at 0920 B/3 three cruisers and four destroyers had been spotted in position 36°20'N, 12°10'E proceeding on course 030° at 15 knots and at 0930 B/3 six merchant vessels and six destroyers had been sighted in position 36°15'N, 12°15'E proceeding on course 190° at 12 knots.

In a follow up signal timed 2125 B/3, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Ursula of the following; At 1930 B/3, convoy of seven ships sighted steering 105° speed 12 knots in position 34°50'N, 12°00'E. Swordfish are attacking. Anticipate ships will turn towards Tripoli.
The convoy was however not sighted by HMS Ursula.
This must have referred to a cover force made up of the Italian light cruisers Luigi di Savoia Duca Degli Abruzzi, Giuseppe Garibaldi and the destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere and Alpino.

They were covering a convoy made up of the Italian transports Aquitania (4971 GRT, built 1924), Beatrice C. (7015 GRT, built 1920), Caffaro (6476 GRT, built 1924), Montello (6117 GRT, built 1927), Nirvo (5270 GRT, built 1919) and the Italian tanker Poza Rica (7799 GRT, built 1940). The close escort of this convoy was made up of the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Geniere and Dardo. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori sailed from Tripoli at 1215 B/2 to meet the convoy and reinforce the escort.
The convoy was attacked in the afternoon by British aircraft during which the Montello was sunk and the Beatrice C. was heavily damaged and later scuttled as she could not be salvaged.

The convoy had sailed from Naples 1730 B/1 - 1930 B/1 and arrived at Tripoli at 1145 B/4 - 1237 B/4 (except the two transports which were sunk and Camicia Nera who returned to Naples after finishing off Beatrice C.. (42)

4 Jun 1941 At 0455 B/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) dived in position 34°03'N, 12°53'E, and proceeded direct towards position 35°31'N, 14°13'E and then on to Malta leaving patrol. HMS Ursula had been ordered to arrive at Malta around 0640 B/5 which, also given the defective generator, would not be possible. (36)

5 Jun 1941 At 2215 B/5, HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) received the signal timed 2016 B/5 from the Cdr. S.1 in which she was ordered to report her position. At 2350 B/5, HMS Ursula was able to pass a signal in which she reported her position. (36)
6 Jun 1941 Around 0920 B/6, HMS Ursula (Lt. I.L.M. McGeoch, RN) ended her 20th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (36)
8 Jun 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard during a short refit and repairs to her defective starboard generator. (43)
11 Jun 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) is undocked. She continued her refit / repairs alongside the Machinery Wharf. (43)
22 Jun 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) conducted trials with the starboard generator while alongside the wharf at Malta.
On the 27th it became apparent that more repairs were required. (43)
10 Jul 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) completed her generator repairs at Malta. (44)

11 Jul 1941 Around 1800 B/11, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta on what should have been her 21st war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). Shortly after departure problems were encountered with her port generator and at 2145 B/11 it was decided to return to Malta.
HMS Ursula entered harbour around 0655 B/12.

18 Jul 1941 Around 1830 B/18, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta to resume her 21st war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was now ordered to patrol in an area to the south of Lampedusa. (Area 'K' bounded by lines joining the following points; a) 34°15'N, 12°20'E, b) 35°10'N, 11°50'E, c) 35°30'N, 12°45'E and d) 34°35'N, 13°15'E.
On departure from harbour two practice attacks were made on HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). (36)
18 Jul 1941 For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during her 21st war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean), see the map below.

21 Jul 1941 At 1800 B/21, in position 34°34'N, 12°10'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted the masts of a schooner hull down to the southward. Lt. Mackenzie endeavoured to close but the schooner was proceeding under auxiliary engines and passed out of sight on a westerly course. According to the logbook of HMS Ursula a small tug was in company with the schooner but this is not mentioned in the patrol report.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (45)

22 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 1010 B/22, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) that an enemy convoy made up of four enemy transports escorted by four destroyers had been sighted at 0910 B/22 in position 37°18'N, 12°00'E proceeding on course 180° at 9 knots.

This referred to a convoy consisting of the transports Maddalena Odero (Italian, 5545 GRT, built 1921), [Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1925) was supposed to join them but was slightly damaged in an air attack on Naples on 20 July and stayed behind], Nicolò Odero (Italian, 6003 GRT, built 1925), Caffaro (Italian 6476 GRT, built 1924) and Preussen (German, 8224 GRT, built 1922) screened by the Italian destroyers Folgore (escort leader), Saetta and Euro sailed from Naples at 0200 B/21-0515 B/21 for Tripoli. The Italian destroyer Fulmine followed at 0950 B/21 to join up. The Italian destroyer Alpino sailed from Palermo at 1522 B/21 to join them at 2000 B/21, 20 miles west of Ustica. At 1848 B/22, the convoy was attacked by enemy bombers. At 1901 B/22, Preussen was hit by a bomb and blew up six minutes later. The destroyers Euro and Fulmine picked up 260 survivors and proceeded to Porto Empedocle where they arrived at 1050 B/23-1100 B/23.

More enemy shipping movements were going on though. The Italian destroyer Fuciliere sailed from Palermo at 1430 B/21 and arrived at Trapani at 1800 B/21 to escort the tanker Brarena (Italian, 6996 GRT, built 1930) which arrived at 2000 B/21 from Naples escorted by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori and had stopped off Trapani to wait for her. Brarena escorted by Fuciliere sailed at 2100 B/21 from Trapani but were then recalled and told to delay their departure to 2230 B/21. They were to join up with the above convoy at dawn on the 23rd at 20 miles north of 'Point 33' and overtaken by the Folgore convoy. At 1635 B/22, they were in position 35°45'N, 12°03'E (where they located an empty lifeboat, believed to be from Barbarigo). At 1904 B/22, a loud explosion was seen bearing 130° [this was Preussen blowing up]. At 1912 B/22, two bombers attacked Brarena, the second scored three hits in position 35°20'N, 12°12'E. Fuciliere picked up survivors and attempted to take her in tow, but at 2113 B/22, two Wellington torpedo bombers attacked, one scoring a hit on the merchant ship and the other narrowly missing astern the destroyer. Fuciliere decided to abandon the merchant ship which was still afloat and join up with the Folgore convoy which she did at 0205 B/23. Brarena drifted aimlessly and finally end up aground near Kerkennah Buoy No. 3 at the beginning of August. At 1323 B/23, Fuciliere was given freedom of action by Folgore and arrived at Tripoli at 1530 B/23 where she landed the Brarena survivors, including several wounded. The Folgore convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1858 B/23.

In a signal timed 1440 B/22, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Ursula that two transport ships had been sighted at 1230 B/22 in position 36°20'N, 12°10'E proceeding on course 170° at 8 knots.
This signal probably referred to the Brarena and Fuciliere.

In a signal timed 1655 B/22, Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Ursula that four medium transport ships had been sighted at 1530 B/22 in position 36°30'N, 11°55'E proceeding on course 193° at a speed of 9 knots. Two destroyers were ahead, two astern and one on the port beam. HMS Ursula was ordered to proceed to intercept.
In a signal timed 1940 B/22 the Vice Admiral Malta informed HMS Ursula that a single escorted northbound transport expects to pass west of Lampedusa at about 0330 B/23 at a speed about 15 knots.

This signal probably referred to the transport Barbaro (Italian, 6343 GRT, built 1940) escorted the Italian torpedo boat Clio which had sailed from Tripoli at 14 knots at 1221 B/22. They were however recalled as 'Force H' was known to be at sea. They returned to Tripoli at 1048 B/23.
The above signal was later cancelled in the Vice Admiral Malta's signal timed 0006 B/23.

In a signal timed 2025 B/22, the Vice Admiral Malta informed HMS Ursula that 2025 B/22 three merchant ships and four 4 destroyers were in position 35°39'N, 12°04'E proceeding on course 190° at 10 knots. (46)

26 Jul 1941 At 0055 B/26, well to the south of Lampedusa, in approximate position 34°50'N, 12°25'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a single torpedo boat which appeared to be searching. Avoiding action was taken.
The was no destroyer or torpedo boat in area (only on the following day). Perhaps a it was a MAS boat ?

In a signal timed 1455 B/26, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Ursula that probably an enemy convoy would be passing Kerkennah on 29 July. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal.]

This must have referred to a convoy that was to leave Naples on 27 July bound for Tripoli. It was made up of the following transports Amsterdam (Italian, 10873 GRT, built 1921), Bainsizza (Italian, 7933 GRT, built 1930), Col di Lana (Italian, 5891 GRT, built 1926) and Spezia (German, 1825 GRT, built 1924) and escorted by the destroyers Freccia, Dardo, Strale. On the 28th the destroyer Turbine joined, having sailed from Trapani at 0340 B/28. Distant cover for this convoy would be provided by the light cruisers Raimondo Montecuccoli, Giuseppe Garibaldi and the destroyers Granatiere and Bersagliere.

The convoy sailed from Naples at 0345 B/7-0400 B/27 (according to the sailing orders, it was supposed to sail at 1830 B/27, but apparently the convoy sailed ahead of schedule). It was to be joined by the tanker Poza Rica (Italian, 7599 GRT, built 1941) escorted by a torpedo boat from Palermo, but she had defects and could not sail in time. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1915 B/29-1956 B/29.
At 0500 B/29, Turbine collided with Col di Lana and suffered light damage. (36)
27 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 1331 B/27, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) that a probable northbound convoy was be passing Kerkennah on 30 July 1941.

The expected convoy (Tripoli - Naples) must have been the one which was made up of the Italian troop transports Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932), Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933) and Marco Polo (12567 GRT, built 1912) that were escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Camicia Nera, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti.

In a signal timed 2115 B/27, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Ursula that a northbound (slow) convoy of five merchant ships and four destroyers that had left Tripoli around 0930 B/27 was to be expected. It was anticipated the convoy would take the usual route. A small warship in tow also left today for repairs at Naples and should make good 13 knots.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transports Ernesto (7399 GRT, built 1914), Nita (6803 GRT, built 1913), Nirvo (5270 GRT, built 1919), Aquitania (4971 GRT, built 1924) and the Italian tanker Castelverde (6958 GRT, built 1921). The gunboat Palmaiola (562 GRT, built 1916) was being towed by the Ernesto. But as the Ernesto was proceeding too slow with the tow it was slipped and the gunboat returned to Tripoli assisted by a tug when they were just seven miles out (see DEFE3/832/726, 730, 732). Escorts were initially the Italian destroyers Folgore, Saetta, Fuciliere and Alpino. The destroyer Fulmine joined at sea having sailed from Pantelleria. The convoy sailed from Tripoli at 0545 B/27-0730 B/27 and arrived at Naples at 0158 B/3-0232 B/3, except Fuciliere and Alpino which were detached to escort the torpedoed light cruiser Giuseppe Garibaldi (see below).

The convoy was to be covered by the light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi (which was torpedoed by HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) at 1955 B/28) and Raimondo Montecuccoli (Eighth Cruiser Division), screened by the destroyers Granatiere and Bersagliere which sailed from Palermo at 2215-2300 B/27. After Garibaldi was torpedoed by HMS Upholder, Montecuccoli returned to Palermo at 0030 B/29. Garibaldi screened by Granatiere and Bersagliere and later joined by Fuciliere and Alpino proceeded to Palermo at 10 knots and arrived at 0530 B/29-0640 B/29.
The Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori later sailed from Naples to join the escort when it was approaching its destination.

28 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 0945 B/28, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) of the position of the convoy mentioned in his earlier signal timed 1455 B/26. Lt. Mackenzie decided to patrol in position 'G' [This must have been in approximately 34°48'N, 12°15'E]. (47)

29 Jul 1941 At 0400 B/29, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a darkened vessel. She turned to attack. HMS Ursula passed between two destroyers at range of 800 and 600 yards. A third destroyer was then seen coming towards at a range of 1000 yards. No merchant vessels were seen at this time as the visibility was poor.

At 0410 B/29, in position 34°48'N, 12°15'E, HMS Ursula was forced to dive by a destroyer which passed overhead shortly afterwards. No depth charges were dropped. From Asdic reports, two merchant vessels appeared to be present. These were heard passing over the fore-ends at 0418 B/29 and another destroyer, probably weaving astern of the convoy, passed close at 0422 B/29.

At 0426 B/29, HMS Ursula surfaved to endeavour to follow up and attack. The course of the convoy was estimated as being 170° at a speed of 11 knots. Shortly after surfacing the rear destroyer challenged or fired green Very lights. She then commenced dropping depth charges. It was not possible to get into a firing position before daylight and the chase had to be broken off and HMS Ursula had to dive when daylight approached. Aircraft were patrolling over HMS Ursula's position from 0515 B/29 to 0700 B/29.
[For the composition of the convoy encountered see the event for 26 July 1941.] (36)
30 Jul 1941 At 2115 B/30, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (47)
1 Aug 1941 Around 0700 B/1, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 21st war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (36)

14 Aug 1941 Around 1830 B/14, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta for her 22nd war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the Southern approaches to the Straits of Messina (Area 'N').
On departure two practice attacks were made on HMS Jade (Boatswain W.J.H. Fellows, RN). (36)
14 Aug 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ursula during her 22nd war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) see the map below.

16 Aug 1941 At 0900 B/16, east of Augusta, in approximate position 37°15'N, 15°36'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted an auxiliary four-masted schooner bearing 200° at a range of 6 miles. (45)

16 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1015 B/16, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) was informed by the Senior Officer Submarines Malta that a Vichy-French evacuation convoy (from Syria) was expected to pass through the Straits of Messina northbound around 1000 B/20.

This signal referred to convoy 'A', which was made up of the transports Champollion (12263 GRT, built 1925), Mariette Pacha (12239 GRT, built 1926) and Explorateur Grandidier (10268 GRT, built 1926). They sailed from Toulon on 9 August and arrived in Beirut on 15 August. They then left Beirut on 16 August and arrived at Marseille on 23 August. During the return journey the ships carried the following numbers of passengers; Champollion 2041 passengers, Mariette Pacha 1605 passengers and Explorateur Grandidier 1627 passengers. In total there were thus 5273 passengers on board the ships of the convoy of which 4732 were military personnel. (48)
18 Aug 1941 At 1800 B/18, south-south-east of Cape dell'Armi, in position 37°49'N, 15°44'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a northbound hospital ship.
This was the Italian hospital ship Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which sailed from Tripoli at 1500 B/17 and reached Naples at 0820 B/19. (36)

20 Aug 1941 At 1025 B/20, in position 37°55'N, 15°50'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted an Italian submarine, thought to be of the Adua-class, proceeding westwards very close inshore. The range could not be closed to less then 8000 yards and an attack was not possible.

This was most likely the Italian submarine Topazio. Topazio reported to be off Cape dell'Armi at 1515 B/20. She had sailed from Taranto at 1220 B/19 and arrived at Augusta at 1715 B/20. An outside possibility is Tricheco which sailed from Messina on that day for exercises but it is highly unlikely it was her.
At 1500 B/20, two small vessels, thought to be motor minesweepers, were seen sweeping inside the 100 fathom line from Cape dell'Armi to Cape Spartivento.
At 1535 B/20, in position 37°55'N, 15°51'E, sighted a naval vessel resembling a corvette proceeding westwards.

We have been unable to identify this vessel. We thought it may have been the Italian submarine chaser Albatros but most likely it was not her as she had sailed from Messina at 0759 B/20 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1511 B/20, unless there is an error in time somewhere.

At 1600 B/20, sighted a single unescorted medium sized merchant vessel coming down the Straits of Messina and towards HMS Ursula but 15 minutes later, when the range was still 8000 yards the ship turned round and went back. We have been unable to identify this vessel.

At 1800 B/20, in position 37°51'N, 15°43'E, a motor torpedo boat was sighted apparently on patrol. It looked like a German E-boat [These had yet to arrived in the Mediterranean at that time.]. We have been unable to identify this vessel. (45)

21 Aug 1941 At 1430 B/21, just to the east of Cape dell'Armi, in approximately 37°55'N, 15°43'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a small tug towing a lighter proceeding eastwards. A MAS-boat appeared to be in company.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (36)

21 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1415 B/21, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN), HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) that a convoy of three Vichy-French ships would pass through position 41°35'N, 10°00'E about 0001/21 and would then proceed towards the Messina Strait. They were expected to pass through position 37°03'N, 16°46'E around 2000 B/22. They were not to be attacked.

This referred to the Vichy-French evacuation convoy 'C' which was made up of the liners Marrakech (6288 GRT, built 1914), Sinaia (8567 GRT, built 1922) and Groix (9957 GRT, built 1922). This convoy had departed Toulon on 19 August and arrived at Beirut on 28 August. At Beirut Marrakech embarked 733 passengers, Sinaia embarked 1867 passengers and Groix embarked 1562 passengers. Total number of passengers was 4162 of which 3674 were military personnel. The convoy departed Beirut on 29 August and arrived at Marseille on 5 September. (48)
22 Aug 1941 At 1040 B/22, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) just south of the Straits of Messina, sighted a MAS boat patrolling in the area. She passed (almost) overhead when first sighted.

At 1150 B/22, in position 37°58'N, 15°31'E, the expected southbound Vichy-French evacuation convoy was sighted. This convoy (convoy 'C') was made up the liners Marrakech (6288 GRT, built 1914), Sinaia (8567 GRT, built 1922) and Groix (9957 GRT, built 1922). This convoy had departed Toulon on 19 August and arrived at Beirut on 28 August. (36)

23 Aug 1941 At 0205 B/23, south of the Straits of Messina, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) dived in position 37°47'N, 15°34'E to avoid two MAS boats which had been picked up on Asdic and were later seen coming towards.

At 1340 B/23 [time according to the log, the patrol report gives the time as 1555 B/23], HMS Ursula was bombed from the air while at periscope depth about 2 nautical miles east of Taormina, Sicily, Italy in position 37°51'N, 15°20'E. Two bombs were dropped but no damage was sustained.
At 2108 B/23, HMS Ursula surfaced in position 37°43'N, 15°32'E with the intention of landing commandos by folbot to blow up a tunnel near Capo dell’Armi but the bad weather prevented the operation.

At 2115 B/23, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1925 B/23 was received which cancelled her recall from patrol and ordered her to patrol off Cape dell'Armi in position 37°56'N, 15°36'E to intercept Italian heavy units (battleships) which had been operating to the south of Sardinia and were expected to return to Taranto via the Straits of Messina.
The enemy battleships were the Littorio and Vittorio Veneto escorted by the destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere, Alpino, Aviere and Camicia Nera.

They had sailed from Taranto at 1450 B/22-1610 B/22 in anticipation of an enemy squadron which sailed from Gibraltar and crossed the Straits of Messina at 0730 B/23-0800 B/23. They operated in the Tyrrhenian Sea. At 1635 B/23, they were reinforced by destroyers Nicoloso Da Recco, Lanzerotto Malocello and Ugolino Vivaldi. They had sailed from Naples at 1232 B/23-1405 B/23 (so did he destroyers Freccia, Folgore and Fulmine who sailed at 2030 B/23-2045 B/23 and then went to Palermo, arriving at 1000 B/25-1110 B/25). At 1845 B/23, they sighted the cruisers of the Third Cruiser Division (see below). During the early hours of the 24th, the minelayer HMS Manxman (Capt. R.K. Dickson, RN) successfully laid a minefield between Leghorn and Piombino but was not detected. At 2035 B/24-2216 B/24, the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Alpino, Fuciliere, Granatiere, Bersagliere and Maestrale reached Cagliari to refuel and sailed again at 0350 B/25-0530 B/25. The two battleships, screened by Camicia Nera, Antoniotto Usodimare, Alpino, Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio Da Noli, Nicoloso Da Recco and ? ), reached Naples at 0647 B/26-0834 B/26. Aviere reached Naples at 1230 B/26. Emanuele Pessagno arrived at Naples at 1617 B/26.

Other movements: The Third Cruiser Division departed from Messina at 0955 B/23-1010 B/23: heavy cruisers Trieste, Trento, Bolzano and Gorizia, screened by the destroyers Carabiniere and Corazziere. Ascari and Lanciere, sailed from Reggio (Calabria) at 0900 B/23-0940 B/23 to join them. We know they joined the Battle Squadron at 1845 B/23 but it is not clear when they were detached. They returned to Messina at 0728 B/26-0815 B/26, with the following exceptions: as they approached their destination, Bolzano was torpedoed by HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) at 0638 B/26 and reached Messina at 1306 B/26. Corazziere entered Messina at 1130 B/26 and Carabiniere at 1220 B/26, Ascari and Lanciere returned to Reggio at 1228 B/26-1259 B/26. The destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Emanuele Pessagno, Giuseppe Da Verazzano, Antoniotto Usodimare and Antonio Da Noli sailed from Trapani at 1915-2020 B/23. The destroyers Maestrale and Scirocco sailed from Palermo at 1240 B/23-1330 B/23. Maestrale and Scirocco returned to Palermo at 0130 B/26 and 1831 B/26 respectively. Da Verazzano, Pigafetta and Maloceloo arrived at La Spezia at 1400 B/26, 1345 B/26 and 1403 B/26 respectively.

The Eighth Cruiser Division, made up of the light cruisers Luigi Di Savoia Duca Degli Abruzzi, Muzio Attendolo and Raimondo Montecuccoli, screened by five destroyers, including the Fulmine, departed from Palermo at 0530 B/24-0630 B/24. They returned to Palermo at 0830 B/24-0940 B/25.

The Italian squadrons reported six submarine sightings: 1. 1039 B/24 by the destroyer Fulmine (318° - Marettimo – 30 miles) sighted two torpedo tracks and dropped depth charges (this was HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN)). 2. 1745 B/24 by the battleship Vittorio Veneto (off Cape Spartivento, Sardinia). 3. 1219 B/25 by the destroyer Nicoloso Da Recco (off Cape Spartivento, Sardinia). 4. 1238 B/25 by the destroyer Aviere (48 miles SSE of Cape Spartivento, Sardinia). 5. 1845 B/25 by the destroyer Granatiere and the battleship Littorio (85 miles east of Cape Carbonara, Sardinia). 6. 1900 B/25 by the battleship Vittorio Veneto (off Cape Carbonara, Sardinia). The last five sighting seems all to be bogus. (36)
25 Aug 1941 At 1630 B/25, in position 37°55'N, 15°39'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) sighted a northbound hospital ship.
This was Italian Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which sailed from Tripoli at 1500 B/24 and arrived at Naples at 0810 B/26. (45)
26 Aug 1941 At 0001 B/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) left patrol to return to Malta.
At 0201 B/26, east of Catania, in position 37°27'N, 15°31'E, a MAS boat was sighted laying stopped dead ahead. HMS Ursula dived to avoid surfacing again a little over an hour later.
This was most probably MAS 424. She had sailed from Messina at 2040 B/25 and returned at 1040 B/26. (36)
27 Aug 1941 Around 0650 B/27, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 22nd war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (36)

29 Aug 1941 Around 1930 B/29, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) departed Malta for her 23rd war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was to patrol south of Lampedusa in position 34°50'N, 12°37'E.
An important fast Italian troop convoy had departed Naples for Tripoli that day.

This convoy was made up of the Italian troop transports Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933), Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932) and Victoria (13098 GRT, built 1931) which were to proceed from Naples to Tripoli. The convoy was escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Vincenzo Gioberti, Antonio da Noli, Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno. The convoy had sailed from Naples at 0910 B/29-1115 B/29. The convoy was unsuccessfully attacked by HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) at 1217 B/29 and by HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) at 0710 B/31. It arrived at Tripoli at 0940 B/31-1045 B/31.

29 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 2155 B/29, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) to steer 180° and proceed southwards with all despatch.

In a follow up signal timed 2301 B/29, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta sent the following orders to these two submarines; HMS Upholder to patrol in position 32°53'N, 13°50'E. HMS Ursula in position 32°57'N, 14°00'E. The convoy was expected to pass through position 33°08'N, 16°25'E at 2230/30 either for Tripoli (Libya) direct or via Khoms to arrive 0730/31. Speed of the convoy will be 17.5 knots. Times quoted thought to be minus 2 hours but may be also be GMT. Tripoli (Libya) is providing air and will also reinforce the surface escort. Leave positions 1000 B/31 and do not come to periscope depth for routines after diving on 31st. (48)
31 Aug 1941 At 0456 B/31, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) dived in position 32°58'N, 14°02'E.
At 0510 B/31, HE was picked up on bearing 350°. HMS Ursula turned to the northward to investigate.
At 0520 B/31, HE was reported from 350° to 120°. HMS Ursula turned back to the east.

At 0521 B/31, a Navigatori-class destroyer was seen coming towards. The light at this time was too great to permit to surface. It was still very poor though and periscope visibility was low. This destroyer passed at a range of only 200 yards. HE was heard over a wide arc to the northward. It was obvious that the troopships were to the northward but they were never seen at this stage. At 0545 B/31, the light now having improved, an Oceania-class liner was seen to the north-west. Range was about 7000 yards. The convoy made a large alteration of course to the south-west at this time but a firing position could not be attained. By 0700 B/31, the convoy had passed. HMS Ursula altered course to 000° to return to Malta. [For the composition of the convoy see the event of 29 August 1941.] (36) 2 Sep 1941 Around 0700 B/2, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 23rd war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (36) 10 Sep 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) conducted practive attacks off Malta on HMS Gloxinia (Lt.Cdr. A.J.C. Pomeroy, RNVR) and HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). After the exercises, HMS Ursula went to the Malta Dockyard where she was docked in No.2 graving dock. (49) 12 Sep 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) is undocked. (49) 16 Sep 1941 Around 1315 B/16, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 24th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was to patrol off Tripoli, Libya in position 32°53'N, 13°45'E. She was part of a patrol line with HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) (in position 032°52'N, 15°03'E), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) (in position 32°57'N, 14°53'E) and HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wright, DSC, RN) (in position 33°01'N, 14°42'E). An important fast Italian troop convoy had departed Taranto for Tripoli that day. The convoy in question was made up of the Italian troop transports Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933), Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932) and Vulcania (24469 GRT, built 1928) which were on passage from Taranto to Tripoli. They were escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Antonio da Noli, Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani. On departure from Malta a practice attack was made on HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). (50) 16 Sep 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ursula during her 24th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) see the map below. 18 Sep 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) attacked the Italian troop transport / liner Vulcania (24469 GRT, built 1928) about 30 nautical miles east of Tripoli, Libya in approximate position 32°55'N, 13°52'E. Four torpedoes were fired but all missed their target although it was thought that one hit was obtained. [For the composition of the convoy Vulcania was part of see the event for 16 September 1941.] 0200 B/18 - Dived in position 32°58'N, 13°50'E, and patrolled at 70 feet listening for HE. 0610 B/18 - Received the contact report of HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN) timed 0419 B/18 in which she reported to have damaged to troop transports. 0625 B/18 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat with a MAS boat alongside. Stopped two miles to the southward. Later altered course to the eastward to get on the expected line of approach of the convoy. 0656 B/18 - Sighted a large transport with a Navigatori-class destroyer as escort. [This was most likely Antoniotto Usodimare.] 0705 B/18 - In approximate position 32°55'N, 13°52'E fired four torpedoes from 3500 yards at what is thought to be the troop transport / liner Vulcania. It was thought the one hit was obtained. [This was not the case though]. 0712 B/18 - Altered course to the eastwards. No counter attack had developed. 0725 B/18 - Came to periscope depth and sighted the target 6 miles to the westward with a list of 5° to starboard. Went deep to reload the torpedo tubes. 0751 B/18 - Suspended reloading the torpedo tubes. Two destroyers were heard approaching from the west at 24 knots. Two depth charges were dropped astern. The destroyers passed close up the starboard side and continued to the eastwards at high speed. 0824 B/18 - Reloading continued. 0914 B/18 - Completed reloading. 1040 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth and sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat hunting 5 miles to the westward in the position of the attack. Went deep. 1100 B/18 - The torpedo boat passed overhead. 1040 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth. The torpedo boat was seen still hunting to the south-eastward. 1200 B/18 - An Oriani-class destroyer was sighted proceeding along the coast towards Tripoli at 20 knots. 1225 B/18 - An armed yacht was seen hunting to the south-westward. 1235 B/18 - This yacht was seen approaching. Went deep to evade. 1235 B/18 to 1405 B/18 - The yacht and another vessel, thought to be a torpedo boat, hunted the submarine. No depth charges were dropped. 1430 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. 1540 B/18 - Heard four loud explosions fairly close. 1710 B/18 - Sighted the yacht hunting inshore near Khoms. 2045 B/18 - Surfaced in position 33°02'N, 14°12'E. Set course for position 32°53'N, 14°12'E. 2100 B/18 - Sighted signalling ahead. Stopped. 2125 B/18 - Again sighted signalling ahead. Considered it to be a light on shore. Proceeded. 2322 B/18 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2140 B/18, recalling HMS Ursula. Set course to return to Malta. (50) 20 Sep 1941 Around 0800 B/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 24th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (50) 23 Sep 1941 Around 1935 B/23, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 25th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the Southern approaches to the Strait of Messina. At 2112 B/23, a possible submarine was sighted for which the rudder was put hard to starboard. At 2118 B/23, HMS Ursula proceeded on her previous course of 072°. HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) had departed Malta half an hour before HMS Ursula and may thus have been the 'submarine' sighted. [No Italian submarines were in the area.] [No patrol report is available for this patrol. The log for September list no attacks but for October no log is available. As far as is known to us no attacks were carried out.] For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during this patrol see the map below. [Map is incomplete, only the positions for September can be shown.] (49) 25 Sep 1941 At 2155 B/25, in position 37°49'N, 15°34'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) heard turbine HE on bearing 300°. HMS Ursula dived to avoid being detected. At 2232 B/25, HMS Ursula surfaced. Nothing was sighted though. (49) 26 Sep 1941 At 0235 B/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) heard turbine HE on bearing 140°. Nothing was sighted though. At 1010 B/25, south-south-west of Cape dell'Armi, in approximate position 37°54'N, 15°39'E, a sailing vessel was sighted bearing 090° at a range of three miles. Course of the vessel was 285°. At 1230 B/25, smoke was sighted and five minutes later a cruiser and seven destroyers were sighted. At 1300 B/25, the cruiser, thought to be a heavy cruiser of the Trento-class, and seven destroyers were seen to proceed up the Straits of Messina. They passed out of range. These were the heavy cruiser Trieste (Third Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Corazziere, Carabiniere, Ascari, Lanciere, Aviere and Camicia Nera which sailed from Taranto at 2200 B/25-2300 B/25 for La Spezia but were diverted to counter Operation HALBERD, they were joined by the heavy cruisers Trento and Gorizia which sailed from Messina at 1410 B/26 while Aviere and Camicia Nera were detached to Messina and arrived there at 1818 B/26-1830 B/26. The three cruisers and four remaining destroyers arrived at La Maddalena at 0015 B/29-0416 B/29 At 1400 B/26, a destroyer / torpedo boat was sighted bearing 297°. enemy course was 010°. This was thought to be possibly the Italian submarine chaser Albatros which is possible as she had sailed from Messina at 1900 B/25 for an A/S search and returned at 1500 B/26. It is however not known to us if she carried out the A/S search to the south or north of the Straits. Obviously, her sortie on the next day was north of the Straits. (49) 27 Sep 1941 At 1305 B/27, south of the Straits of Messina, in approximate position 37°58'N, 15°32'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) a schooner was sighted bearing 150°. Enemy course was 360°. At 1455 B/27, a hospital ship was seen proceeding on course 025° northwards along the Sicilian coast. This was most probably the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) which had sailed from Augusta at 1045 B/27 for Palermo (arrival time not known to us). At 1945 B/27, a hospital ship was seen proceeding up the Calabrian coast. This was most probably the Italian hospital ship Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which had sailed from Benghazi at 1500 B/25 and arrived at Naples at 0915 B/28. At 2315 B/27, HE was picked up. At 2355 B/27, in position 37°44'N, 15°34'E, HMS Ursula dived to evade a small torpedo boat. HMS Ursula surfaced at 0042 B/28. (49) 29 Sep 1941 At 0445 B/29, south of the Straits of Messina, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) sighted a hospital ship proceeding down the Sicilian coast. It was thought to be the Italian California (12767 GRT, built 1920). This must have been Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, 1928) which arrived at Augusta at 0920 B/29 from Tripoli (after searching for the survivors of the Albatros, north of the Straits (so she was indeed moving southwards). At 1300 B/29, a two-funnelled ship was seen proceeding from Catania towards Cape Spartivento. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (49) 30 Sep 1941 At 0630 B/30, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) sighted a schooner proceeding down the middle of the Straits of Messina. At 0655 B/30, a submarine was seen proceeding up the Straits of Messina. An attack was not possible and the enemy passed out of range. [We have been unable to identify this 'submarine'. None should have been in the area.] At 1500 B/30, a schooner, proceeding under sail, was seen bearing 270° at a range of 3 miles. [No further details for this patrol are available as there is neither a logbook or patrol report.] (49) 6 Oct 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 25th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (51) 17 Oct 1941 Around 1230 A/17, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 26th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the east coast of Tunisia off the Gulf of Hammamet in area 'G' joining positions 37°24'N, 15°52'E, 37°41'N, 16°51'E, 38°33'N, 16°28'E and 38°16'N, 15°29'E. [No logs are available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (36) 18 Oct 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Beppe (4859 GRT, built 1912) some 45 nautical miles west of Lampedusa Island in position 35°25'N, 11°39'E. The damaged Beppe is towed to Tripoli, Libya. Beppe was in a convoy with was further made up of the Italian transports Caterina (4838 GRT, built 1920), Marin Sanudo (5081 GRT, built 1926), Paolina (4894 GRT, built 1911), Probitas (5084 GRT, built 1918) and Amba Aradam (405 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the Italian destroyers Folgore, Fulmine, Antoniotto Usodimare, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani sailed from Naples at 0955 B/16-1335 B/16. At 1500 B/16, Probitas which had developed engine defects turned back, escorted by Fulmine. They arrived at Naples at 1648 B/16-1652 B/16). At 0910 B/18, Beppe was hit in the forepeak, her rudder was out of the water. Vincenzo Gioberti picked up some of the crew and her military passengers, in all 68 persons. With some difficulty, Beppe managed to do 4 knots. Soon, it was realised that she needed towing and the destroyer attempted to do so but the cable quickly broke. At 0400 B/19, Beppe and Vincenzo Gioberti were joined by the destroyer Nicoloso da Recco which had sailed from Tripoli with the destroyer Sebenico at 1900 B/18 to assist them. Vincenzo Gioberti was short of water and Nicoloso da Recco signalled her to proceed to Tripoli where she arrived at 1707 B/19. At 2030 B/18, the Italian tug Ciclope sailed from Tripoli and joined them at 0900 B/19. In the meantime, Sebenico proceeded to reinforce the escort of the convoy which had come under air attack from Swordfish aircraft of 830 Squadron (FAA) at 2210 B/18. The steamer Caterina had been hit by a torpedo; the destroyer Alfredo Oriani stood by her. The German tug Max Berendt sailed from Tripoli at 0430 B/19 to tow her, followed by the Italian tug Marsigli at 0500 B/19. The torpedo boats Generale Antonino Cascino and Calliope sailed from Zuara and Sidi Bilal respectively at 0445 B/19 to assist the stricken vessel, but Caterina sank at 1720 B/19 in position 350° - Tripoli Light - 62 miles. Generale Antonino Cascino picked up 195 survivors, there were four victims. The convoy which now consisted of Marin Sanudo, Paolina and Amba Aradam, screened by the destroyers Folgore and Antoniotto Usodimare arrived at Tripoli at 1130 B/19-1155 B/19, followed by Generale Antonino Cascino at 1525 B/19, Alfredo Oriani and Sebenico at 2205 B/19-2230 B/19, Max Berendt at 0525 B/20 and sailed again to assist Beppe. Nicoloso da Recco arrived at Tripoli at 1445 B/20. Beppe towed by Ciclope, Marsigli and Max Berendt and escorted by Calliope arrived at 0610 B/21-0700 B/21. 0706 A/18 - In position 35°27'N, 11°45'E sighted smoke bearing 306°. Started attack. 0802 A/18 - In position 35°25'N, 11°39'E fired four torpedoes at two merchant vessels of about 6000 tons. Ranges were 6000 and 7000 yards. It was thought that two hits were obtained, one on each target. 0825 A/18 to 0900 A/18 - One of the escorts carried out counter-attacks in which nine depth charges were dropped. No damage was done to Ursula. 0912 A/18 - Sighted a merchant vessel stopped in the position of the attack. Altered course towards and started to reload No.3 torpedo tube. 1005 A/18 - The vessel was seen to be lower in the water. 1100 A/18 - Nothing in sight. It was thought the vessel had sunk. 1830 A/18 - Surfaced and set course for Malta. (36) 19 Oct 1941 Around 1205 A/19, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) arrived at Malta to embark four new torpedoes. At 2030 A/19, she departed again to resume her 26th war patrol in her original patrol area (area 'G'). (36) 20 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1041 A/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN); If possible establish patrols as follows from dusk tonight 20/10. HMS Unbeaten in position 073° - Cape Murro di Porco - 7.5 miles. HMS Ursula in position 118° - Cape Murro di Proco - 11.5 miles. Large destroyers might pass to the south-east during night. After attack or at 0430 A/21 proceed in execution of previous orders. This referred to a troop transport run by the Italian destroyers Antonio da Noli, Nicolò Zeno and Emanuele Pessagno from Augusta to Benghazi. They were transporting German troops; Antonio da Noli: 9 officers and 258 other ranks. Nicolò Zeno: 7 officers and 263 other ranks. Emanuele Pessagno: 4 officers and 262 other ranks. They sailed from Augusta at 1950 B/20-2025 B/20 and arrived at Benghazi at 1600 B/21-1840 B/21. (52) 22 Oct 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) carried out a reconnaissance of the coast North of Cape Spartivento to select a suitable bridge to bombard. At 0900 A/22, a suitable target was seen near position 38°06'N, 16°14'E. [Italian sources show that the bridge and telegraph line sustained some damage but seven unexploded shells were found near the bridge.] 1419 A/22 - Ursula surfaced 1500 yards from the bridge. 77 Rounds were fired and the bridge was damaged by 22 direct hits. 1439 A/22 the action was broken off when the gun jammed and an aircraft that was seen to the seaward. (1) 26 Oct 1941 At 1830 A/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1047 A/26 in which she had been ordered to arrive off Malta at 0630 A/28. She was to proceed through positions 36°57'N, 16°15'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E on the way back. (52) 28 Oct 1941 Around 0830 A/28, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 26th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (36) 6 Nov 1941 Around 1500 B/6, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 27th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Misrata, Libya in area 'D 1' enclosed by lines joining the following positions; 32°45'N, 15°21'E, 32°45'N, 15°00'E, 32°45'N, 16°30'E, 32°00'N, 16°30'E. En-route to her patrol area, HMS Ursula was to proceed through position 35°30'N, 14°14'E and position 34°10'N, 14°14'E. [No logs are available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (53) 8 Nov 1941 At 1430 B/8, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) entered her patrol area. (50) 11 Nov 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) attacked a convoy with three torpedoes about 20 nautical miles south-east of Misrata, Libya in position 32°07'N, 15°26'E. No hits were obtained and HMS Ursula was depth charged by the escorts. This convoy was made up of the transport Pertusola (Italian, 1502 GRT, built 1882), which was towing the Italian auxiliary schooner V 236 / Imperia (222 GRT, built 1912), and the transport Ascianghi (Italian, 610 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the torpedo boats Generale Antonio Cascino and Calliope). The convoy had sailed from Benghazi at 1700 B/9-1800 B/9 and arrived at Tripoli at 1400 B/12-1510 B/12. A torpedo passed under the bridge of Pertusola without exploding. Calliope attacked the submarine with two depth charge patterns, recording three explosions and a large oil slick and believed the submarine sunk. 1350 B/11 - Sighted smoke. 1400 B/11 - Sighted three schooners escorted by RD-type minesweepers proceeding northbound close inshore of Ras Khara. These were the schooners Galileo Galilei (?) and Luigi Razza (113 GRT, built 1938) escorted by the minesweepers RD 1 and RD 16, which sailed from Benghazi on 8 or 9 November and arrived at Tripoli at 0900 B/12. --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 1615 B/11 - Sighted smoke to the south. 1730 B/11 - Sighted a convoy of two merchant vessels (one of 2000 tons, one of 1000 tons that was towing a sailing vessel). Escort was provided by two torpedo boats, one was seen to be the Generale Antonio Cascino and the other was a more modern one of the Spica class. 1752 B/11 - In position 32°07'N, 15°26'E fired three torpedoes at the 2000 tons merchant vessel from . Both missed as the tracks were sighted and evaded by the target. 1755 B/11 - Both torpedo boats altered towards. Ursula went deep and rigged for depth charging. 1800 B/11 to 1810 B/11 - Fourteen depth charges were dropped. All rather close but Ursula sustained no damage. (50) 14 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1149 B/14, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) to leave patrol P.M./l6 through position 33°00'N, 15°00'E, 35°20'N, 14°00'E and 35°30'N, 14°10'E to arrive Marsamxett (Malta) during daylight/18. (54) 16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1745 B/16, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) to remain on patrol in area D until dusk on the 18th and thence to patrol within 10 miles of position 33°00'N, 14°50'E by dawn/19. (54) 16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1635 B/16, the Capt. S.10 send the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) proceed as ordered in the signal timed 1221 B/16. HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) proceed towards Cephalonia. HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) are to remain on patrol. Further instructions will follow. (54) 18 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1118 B/18, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) to return to Malta through positions 35°00'N, 15°00'E, 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 36°00'N, 14°53'E to arrive before 1800 B/21. In a signal timed 1545 B/18, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Ursula to leave patrol forthwith and return to Malta passing through positions 33°00'N, 14°50'N, 35°20'N, 14°14'E thence as ordered by Capt. S.10. In a signal timed 2305 B/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula; Owing to own force movements follow route given as given in my signal timed 1118 B/18 to arrive before 1800 B/20 or at 0730 B/21. (54) 20 Nov 1941 Around 0945 B/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 27th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. HMS Ursula returned from patrol suffering from fuel leaks. Before she was to proceed to Gibraltar she was docked to effect repairs. [Date of docking and undocking is not know to us.] (53) 30 Nov 1941 P.M., HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. She is to proceed to the U.K. for a refit. On leaving Malta she was to proceed through position 38° 00'N, 11°44'E and then through 38°05'N, 09°00'E around 2300 B/2. Further routing would be signalled by the Capt. S.8. [No logbook or passage report is available so details may be missing.] (51) 1 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2208 A/1, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS Regent (Lt. W.N.R. Knox, DSC, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN); Onward route. Regent through the following positions. K) 37°20'N, 00°00'E, L) 35°40'N, 02°45'W, M) 35°40'N, 05°00'W. Adjusting speed to pass through position K at 0200 A/6 and L 0700 A/7 to arrive Gibraltar 0900 A/8. URSULA through the following positions; N) 38°05'N, 06°00'E, P) 36°55'N, 00°00'E, L) see above, M) see above. Adjusting speed to pass through position P at 0200 A/7 and L at 0700 A/8 to arrive at Gibraltar at 0900 A/9. Both submarines are to report on crossing 03°00'E if unable to comply with above time within one hour. S.B.R. arranged. Submarines were to change wireless watch at 0001 A/6 to normal watch on Gibraltar broadcast shifting to read M.S. routines. (55) 4 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1853 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) informed the Capt. S.8 that her position at 1900 A/4 was 37°42'N, 04°00'E. Permission is requested to pass points 'P' and 'L' 24 hours early to arrive at Gibraltar at 0900 A/8. No reply came to this signal. (55) 5 Dec 1941 Not having received an answer to her signal timed 1853 A/4 which had been passed via Rosyth Radio at 1954 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) sent a new signal timed 1746 A/5 to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.). HMS Ursula gave her position at 1800 A/5 as 37°16'N, 01°36'E. Permission was requested to pass points 'P' and 'L' twenty-fours early to arrive at Gibraltar 0900 A/8. A weather report was also included. In a signal timed 2310 A/5, F.O.C.N.A. replied as follows; Reply to your 1746 A/5. Your 1954 A/4 not received. Remain on patrol within 15 miles of position 'P' leaving to conform with my 2208 A/1. U-Boats patrol your vicinity. HMS Clyde on patrol within 30 miles from the African coast (to the east of Oran). Own aircraft on A/S patrols up to longitude 00°45'W. Shift W/T watch forthwith. (55) 6 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1108 A/6, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) of the following; U-boats believed to be patrolling in the vicinity Alboran Island. To avoid own night patrols do not cross 04°40'W before 0730 A/9 thence proceed on surface direct to Gibraltar. Bombing restrictions are arranged. (55) 8 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1208 A/8, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) of the following; Two destroyers on north and south patrol 20 miles to the east of Gibraltar, aircraft may co-operate. Comply strictly with the instructions in my signal timed 1108 A/6. In a signal timed 1610 A/8, F.O.C.N.A. informed HMS Ursula of the following; HMS Hermione and six destroyers (these were HMS Maori, HMS Blankney and HMS Exmoor which were to join HMS Laforey, HMS Arrow and HrMs Isaac Sweers already on A/S patrol to the east of Gibraltar) left Gibraltar 1600 A/8 sweeping east at 17 knots returning to Gibraltar at 0800 A/9. Hezlet, RN) arrived at Gibraltar. (56) 14 Dec 1941 As her refit in the U.K. can start only around mid-January 1942, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. (55) 16 Dec 1941 Around 1755 A/16, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 28th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (56) 17 Dec 1941 Around 0800 A/17, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 29th war patrol. She is ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay and end this patrol in the U.K. It was thought the German battlecruisers Scharnhorst, Gneisenau and the heavy cruiser Prinz Eugen were ready to leave Brest for operations in the North Atlantic and / or an attempt to return to Germany. HMS Ursula was escorted out by HMS Imperialist (T/Lt. P.E. Kitto, RNR) until around 1815 A/18 when they parted company to the west-south-west of Lisbon in position 38°30'N, 10°00'W. Hezlet, RN) dived in the vicinity of her assigned patrol position [position '13']. (1) 21 Dec 1941 At 2359 A/21, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) left patrol for Portsmouth proceeding through position '14' in accordance with the orders in the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2139 A/20. (58) 23 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1129 A/23, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN); After passing latitude 49°N proceed direct to Falmouth to rendezvous as ordered by the Flag Officer in Command Falmouth on 25/12. In a signal timed 1514 A/23, the Flag Officer in Command Falmouth sent the following orders to HMS Ursula; Rendezvous in position 180° - Lizard - 5 miles at daylight 25th. (66) 7 Jul 1942 At 1200 B/7, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) left patrol in accordance with the Capt. S.9's signal timed 0645 B/7. (1) 11 Jul 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) had an encounter with each other in approximate position 64°01'N, 02°43'E. HMS Ursula reported the following; At 1651 A/11, HMS Ursula sighted a submarine astern bearing 030° at a range of 5 miles. At 1652 A/11, HMS Ursula dived and altered course to 020° to close the submarine at 1.5 knots. The other submarine had also dived. At 1712 A/11, a challenge was sent by S.S./T. but there came no reply. At 1808 A/11, HE was picked up ahead (220 revs). Course was altered to keep the bearing ahead. At 1818 A/11, another challenge was sent by S.S./T. and two minutes later HMS P 54 replied with her pendants. HMS Ursula altered course to clear HMS P 54 astern. At 1829 A/11, HMS Ursula surfaced and proceeded at reduced speed until HMS P 54 was 5 miles ahead. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- HMS P 54 reported the following; At 1650 A/11, in position 64°04'N, 02°42'E, sighted the conning tower of a submarine bearing 210° at a range of 8 miles. At 1655 A/11, the other submarine was seen to dive. HMS P 54 then also dived and proceeded towards the other submarines position while sweeping on Asdics. No contact was gained and no HE was picked up. At 1812 A/11, Asdic impulses were reported and the other submarine was identified through S.S./T. as being HMS Ursula. At 1828 A/11, HMS Ursula was seen on the surface, 3500 yards astern. At 1831 A/11, HMS P 54 surfaced. It was agreed that HMS P 54 would proceed ahead at 9.5 knots and HMS Ursula would follow at 8.5 knots. (67) 13 Jul 1942 Around 0700 B/13, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B.
