Also parted of the Oran Attack Force were the ships in convoys KMS O1 and KMF O2. Around 1700 Z/6, convoy KMS O1 passed through the Straits of Gibraltar followed by convoy KMF O1 around 1830 Z/6. These convoys then merged to the east of the Straits of Gibraltar. These convoys came direct from the U.K. and were made up of the following (troop) transports; Alcinous (Dutch, 6189 GRT, built 1925), Alphard (British, 5483 GRT, built 1937), Batory (Polish, 14287 GRT, built 1936), Benalbanach (British, 7153 GRT, built 1940), Charles H. Cramp (American, 6220 GRT, built 1920), Chattanooga City (American, 5687 GRT, built 1921), Clan Mactaggart (British, 7622 GRT, built 1920), Delinlian (British, 6423 GRT, built 1923), Duchess of Bedford (British, 20123 GRT, built 1928), Durban Castle (British, 17388 GRT, built 1938), Edward Rutledge (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Empire Confidence (British, 5023 GRT, built 1925), Empire Mordred (British, 7024 GRT, built 1942), Havildar (British, 5401 GRT, built 1940), Letitia (British, 13595 GRT, built 1925), Llangibby Castle (British, 11951 GRT, built 1929), Lycaon (British, 7350 GRT, built 1913), Mark Twain (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Mary Slessor (British, 5027 GRT, built 1930), Monarch of Bermuda (British, 22424 GRT, built 1931), Mooltan (British, 20952 GRT, built 1923), Nieuw Zeeland (Dutch, 11069 GRT, built 1928)), Orbita (British, 15495 GRT, built 1915), Pacific Exporter (British, 6734 GRT, built 1928), Recorder (British, 5981 GRT, built 1930), Reina del Pacifico (British, 17702 GRT, built 1931), Salacia (British, 5495 GRT, built 1937), St. Essylt (British, 5634 GRT, built 1941), Tegelberg (Dutch, 14150 GRT, built 1937), Thesues (British, 6527 GRT, built 1908), Walt Whitman (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Warwick Castle (British, 20107 GRT, built 1930), William Floyd (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), William Wirt (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942) and Zebulon B. Vance (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942). Also part of these convoys was the headquarters ship HMS Largs and the landing ships infantery Glengyle, Queen Emma, Queen Beatrix, Royal Scotsman, Royal Ulsterman, Ulster Monarch and RFA Derwentdale. From the above warships the following had come direct from the U.K. with convoys KMS 1 and KMF 1; escort carrier HMS Biter, AA ship HMS Alynbank, destroyer HMS Vansittart, sloops HMS Aberdeen, HMS Deptford, cutters HMS Hartland, HMS Walney, frigates HMS Exe, HMS Swale and the corvettes HMS Gardenia, HMS Rhododendron, HMS Vetch and HMS Violet. On 2 November 1942, the minesweepers HMS Rhyl, HMS Clacton, HMS Polruan, HMS Rothesay and HMS Stornoway departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS 1. Around 0300 A/3, the light cruiser HMS Jamaica departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMF 1 coming from the U.K. Around 1950 A/4, the light cruiser HMS Aurora departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMF O1. Around 0430 A/6, the battleship HMS Rodney and the destroyers HMS Bulldog, HMS Boreas and HMS Beagle departed Gibraltar as part of Force H. Around 1500 A/6, the destroyer HMS Vansittart, which had arrived at Gibraltar on the 5th, departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS O1 with her were also the minesweepers HMS Bude, HMS Brixham and HMS Felixtowe. A few hours later, shortly after dark on the 6th, the landing ships tank HMS Bachaquero, HMS Misoa and HMS Tasajera departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS O1. They were escorted by the M/S trawlers HMS Fluellen, HMS Horatio, HMS Ronaldsay, HMS Shiant, HMS Coriolanus, HMS Eday, HMS Inchmarnock, HMS Kerrera, the motor launches ML 280, ML 458, ML 463, ML 469, ML 471, ML 480, ML 483 and the harbour defence motor launches HDML 1127, HDML 1128 and HDML 1139. Around 2200 A/6, the escort carrier HMS Dasher and the destroyers HMS Wivern, HMS Amazon, HMS Achates, HMS Antelope, HMS Boadicea and HMS Brilliant departed Gibraltar to join the convoys. Around 0300 A/7, the aircraft carrier HMS Furious, AA cruiser HMS Delhi and the escort destroyers HMS Farndale, HMS Avon Vale, HMS Calpe and HMS Puckeridge departed Gibraltar for the operation. Around 0300 A/7, the destroyers HMS Westcott and HMS Verity departed Gibraltar for the operation. Landing beaches, Oran The port of Oran, is situated at the head of a large bight between Cape Falcon, the rocky north-eastern end of the promontory of which Pointe Corales is the north-western extremity, to the west and Cap d'Aguille, to the east. This bight is divided into three bays of nearly equal size by Point Canastel and by Point Mers-el-Kebir which projects in an easterly direction from the foot of Jebel Santon and is surmounted by a powerful fortress. The heights of Jebel Murjajo lie westward of Oran with the conspicuous Fort Santa Cruz at their eastern end. The artificial harbour at Oran consists of six basins. It is protected on its northern side by a stone breakwater extending eastwards about a mile and a quarter from Pointe Mona on which, at the eastern end of the cliffs under Jebel Murjajo, lies Fort Lambourne. The arrangement of landing beaches at Oran was comparatively simple and consisted of three main beaches; 'X', 'Y' and 'Z' and one subsidiary beach 'R'. An armoured column from Combat Command 'B' would land at 'X' beach which lay at El Mrairia close to Cape Figalo, with orders to capture La Lourmel airfield, block the main roads north-east and south of La Lourmel, advance south of Debkra, and assist in the capture of the airfields at Tafraoui and Es Senia. The 26th Regimental Combat Team would land at 'Y' beach near Les Andalouses with orders to take control of the Jebel Murjajo heights and capture Oran from the west. The 18th Regimental Combat Team would land at 'Z-Green' beach near Arzew with the orders to capture the coastal defence, the port of Arzew and the port of Oran from the east. The 16th Regimental Combat Team would land at 'Z-White' beach to secure a bridgehead for Armoured Combat Command 'B', cover the east flank of the Oran Force and then take part in the capture of Oran. Combat team 'B' would land at 'Z-Red' beach to capture Tafaraoui, Es Senia and Oggaz airfields and assist in capturing Oran from the south. A portion of the First Ranger Battalion would made a subsidiary landing at 'R' beach southeast of Cape Carbon to capture the batteries at Fort la Ponte. The remainder of the battalion landed in Arzew harbour. Meanwhile a parachute battalion was flown from the U.K. and dropped at H-hour near Tafaraoui airfield with orders to neutralise all aircraft on Es Senia airfield and capture Tafaraoui landing ground. Instructions for Assault, Oran Beaches The fast assault convoy, KMF O1, after proceeding through the Mediterranean at 11.5 knots under the orders of Commodore Troubridge, would meet the slow convoy, KMS O1, in position 36°26'N, 01°15'W around 1600 A on 7 November 1942. Here the convoys are split into nine groups; For ' X ' beach Group I, 1st Division; Batory, HMS Princess Beatrix, Queen Emma, 2nd Division; Benalbenach, Mark Twain, Mary Slessor and Walt Whitman. They were escorted by the light cruiser HMS Aurora, destroyer HMS Wivern, corvettes HMS Gardenia, HMS Vetch and the motor launch HMS HDML 1139. Group VIII, LST HMS Bachaquero escorted by the M/S trawler HMS Horatio. For ' Y ' beach Group II; HMS Glengyle, Monarch of Bermuda, Llangibby Castle, Clan Mactaggart and Salacia. They were escorted by the destroyers Brilliant, HMS Verity, M/S trawlers HMS Coriolanus, HMS Eday, HMS Inchmarnock, HMS Kerrera and the motor launches HMS ML 458, HMS ML 463, HMS ML 469, HMS ML 471 and HMS HDML 1128. For ' Z ' beach Group III, 1st Division; Duchess of Bedford, Durban Castle, Ettrick, Warwick Castle. 2nd Division; Derwentdale, Reina del Pacifico and Tegelberg. They were escorted by the light cruiser HMS Jamaica, escort destroyers HMS Calpe, HMS Farndale, minesweepers HMS Brixham, HMS Bude, HMS Clacton, HMS Felixtowe, HMS Polruan, HMS Rothesay, HMS Rhyl, HMS Stornoway and the motor launches HMS ML 280, HMS HDML 1127. Group V; Alcinous, Alphard, Charles H. Cramp, Chatanooga City, Delilian, Recorder and Zebulon B. Vance. They were escorted by the sloop HMS Deptford, cutters HMS Hartland, HMS Walney, corvettes HMS Rhododendron, HMS Violet and the motor launches HMS ML 480 and HMS ML 483. Group VI, 1st division; Derbyshire, Letitia, Mooltan and Nieuw Zeeland. 2nd division, Empire Confidence, Lycaon and Theseus. Group VII, 1st division, Empire Mordred, Havildar, Pacific Exporter and St. Essylt. 2nd division; Edward Rutledge, William Floyd and William Wirt. Groups VI and VII were escorted by the AA cruiser HMS Delhi, destroyer HMS Vansittart, sloop HMS Aberdeen and the frigates HMS Exe and HMS Swale. Group IX; LST's HMS Misoa and HMS Tasajera. They were escorted by the M/S trawlers HMS Fluellen, HMS Ronaldsay and HMS Shiant. For ' R ' beach Group IV; HMS Royal Scotsman, HMS Royal Ulsterman and HMS Ulster Monarch. They had the same escort as Group III. Two submarines were stationed off the beaches as beacons, these were HMS Ursula and HMS P 54. HMS Aurora would be stationed ahead of Group II, HMS Jamaica ahead of Group III and HMS Delhi ahead of Group IV. On reaching position 35°52'N, 00°10'5"W, in the Gulf of Arzew, HMS Jamaica would proceed independently for fire support duty off 'Z' beach. On reaching position 35°48'N, 00°54'W, HMS Aurora was also detached from her group to patrol to the north of Mers-el-Kebir to engage, if needed reinforced by HMS Jamaica, any escaping vessels from Mers-el-Kebir during the darkness. During daylight HMS Aurora was to remain outside the range of the shore batteries. Meanwhile around 0400 A/8, HMS Delhi, would leave her group to meet the aircraft carriers at 0530 A/8 in position 36°07'N, 00°45'W so as to provide additional AA protection. The battleship HMS Rodney, escorted by HMS Bulldog, HMS Boreas and HMS Beagle, detached from Force H, was to protect the anchorage from attack by heavy enemy units. She too was to join the carriers around 0530 A/8. Initial landings were to be made at all beaches simultaneously at H-hour with the follow up forces being landed as soon as possible. Oran attack opens, 8 November 1942. The attack opened, after some delay at 0116 A/8. The assault on 'X' beach was delayed by around 35 minutes due to the strong current. The appearance of a small Vichy-French convoy of four ships escorted by a trawler had also caused some delay. One of the merchant vessels, the Eridan (9928 GRT, built 1929) had sabotaged her engines. She was boarded by an armed guard. The other three merchant vessels, the Agen (4186 GRT, built 1921), Carthage (2440 GRT, built 1897) and Montaigne (2770 GRT, built 1920) initially escaped to the north-east but then ran into Allied forces. They then beached themselves south of Cape Figalo. The appearance of the convoy had the effect that the minesweepers were badly delayed and were almost run over by the ships proceeding to 'X' beach. The minesweepers were then ordered to keep clear and no sweep was carried out ahead of the ships proceeding to 'X' beach. Despite some delays and other difficulties [it would go to far to give a full account of the landings here] that landings were successful. HMS Walney's assault on Oran harbour. It had been decided, that the cutters HMS Walney and HMS Hartland were to attack Oran harbour to prevent the French from performing demolition operations to destroy the ports facilities. They were to attack shortly after 0300 A/8. The cutters were to land American troops and anti-sabotage parties with orders to seize the shore batteries covering the harbour, prevent the scuttling of the dock and harbour works and prevent the blockage of the harbour. The motor launches ML 480 and ML 483 were to provide a smoke screen. HMS Aurora was to provide cover fire if needed and also to distract and confuse the shore batteries. The attack was carried out under the command of A/Capt. (Retd.) F.T. Peters, DSO, DSC, RN, which was embarked on board HMS Walney. The attack commenced at 0245 A/8, when HMS Walney and HMS Hartland with ML 480 and ML 483 in company, approached the harbour entrance. Sirens were heard sounding ashore and all lights in the town had been extinguished. For a time the force circled off the entrance while an announcement was made in French by loud hailer from HMS Walney. The reply was hostile. A searchlight was quickly trained on the Walney and she came under a heavy, though inaccurate, machine gun fire. Followed by the Hartland, she at once turned away northwards coming round in a full circle to charge the booms. At the same time ML 480 went on ahead to full speed, laying a smoke screen, but hit the outer boom, coming to rest under heavy fire half way across it. She got off again at 0310 A/8, just as HMS Walney, after completing her circle, charged the two booms at 15 knots, through the smoke and entered the harbour. On reaching it, A/Capt. Peters, slipped three canoes, manned with special parties, but at least one of the was sunk almost immediately by gunfire. HMS Walney herself was steaming up the harbour to westwards at slow speed was hit by pompom fire from the Ravin Blanc battery. Half way up the harbour she narrowly missed ramming a French destroyer proceeding towards the harbour entrance. It raked her with two broadsides from point blank range, wrecking her main engines, and a few minutes later she came under heavy cross fire from the destroyer Epervier laying alongside to the south and from submarines and destroyers moored to the northward. At last, blazing forward and amidships, she drifted out of control bows on to the jetty ahead of the Epervier. An attempt to get heaving lines ashore failed and she drifted once more slowly out into the harbour. Her guns were out of action, their crew virtually wiped out. Sixteen officers and men were lying dead on her bridge where A/Capt. Peters was the sole survivor. Below only five officers and men of the landing parties remained alive amidst scenes of undescridale carnage. Nothing further could be done and the ship was abandoned, her few survivors, including A/Capt. Peters were taken prisoner. The abandoned wreck rolled over shortly afterwards. HMS Hartland's assault on Oran harbour. When HMS Walney entered Oran harbour at 0310 A/8, Lt.Cdr. Billot waited five minutes, in compliance with his orders, before heading towards the harbour entrance. As she approached the smoke screen laid by ML 480, HMS Walney was picked up by a searchlight, and came under a devastating fire from the Ravin Blanc battery which killed nearly all her guns crews and temporarily blinded Lt.Cdr. Billot with a splinter. Before he recovered his sight the ship struck the breakwater, but eventually she entered the harbour and made for her objective, the Quai de Dunkerque, under gunfire from point blank range from the destroyer Typhon laying alongside it. Shells bursting inside her hull brought her to a standstill turning her mess decks, on which the troops were waiting, into shambles. Then with fires raging fore and aft, she drifted alongside the mole but she almost immediately was blown back into the harbour where she anchored while being under heavy fire from the French but these then ceased fire so that the ship could be abandoned. By 0410 A/8, all survivors had left and at 0525 A/8 there was an explosion on board but she remained afloat buring furiously. She later blew up and sank after a huge explosion which caused damage to buildings in a large area around the mole. Oran captured. While HMS Walney and HMS Hartland were gate-crashing into Oran Harbour, HMS Aurora was patrolling five miles away to the northward. At 0527 A/8, she opened fire on the searchlight illuminating HMS Hartland, successfully extinguishing its beam. Then shortly before first light she engaged and disabled a Vichy-French destroyer of the Simoun-class from a range of 6000 yards. This destroyer later sank and appears to have been the Tramontaine. Around 0600 A/8, two more destroyers of the Simoun-class were seen leaving Oran, but seeing that they were standing by their sinking consort, HMS Aurora for a time withheld her fire. These destroyers were however soon in action with HMS Boadicea and HMS Calpe, and when they fired torpedoes at HMS Aurora she too opened fire from 13600 yards. In the ensuing fight HMS Boadicea was hit forward, and one of the French destroyers, being severely damaged and set on fire by a broadside, was driven inshore [this was the Tornade]. The action ended abruptly at 0727 A/8, when the other destroyer, on being hit aft, turned and fled to the direction of Oran [this was the Typhon]. Meanwhile the sloop / minelayer La Surprise was sunk by HMS Brilliant around 0630 A/8. Aircraft from HMS Furious, HMS Biter and HMS Dasher had meanwhile attacked airfield destroying enemy aircraft on the ground and also in the air with only small losses to themselves. Shore batteries managed to hit the Reina del Pacifico and Llangibby Castle. HMS Rodney proceeded closer inshore and opened fire at 1010 A/8 on the Jebel Santon battery from a range of over 20000 yards. The battery was engaged several times during the day before HMS Rodney retired to seaward again. Fighting continued on 9 November 1942. At 1024 A/9, HMS Jamaica and HMS Aurora engaged the Epervier and Typhon. The Epervier was driven ashore in flames south of Cape de l'Aiguille while the Typhon retired to Oran where she too was beached. The Vichy-French at Oran capitulated around noon on the 10th. (74) 9 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 2158 Z/9, the Capt. S.8. ordered HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) to leave patrol forthwith diving by day to pass position 135° - Cape Palos - 10 miles at 2200 A/10 and 0001 A/ll respectively. Further orders would follow. In a signal 2206 Z/9, the Capt. S.8, referring to his signal timed 2158 Z/9, ordered both submarines that from position 135° - Cape Palos - 10 miles, HMS P 54 and HMS Ursula were to proceed Gibraltar dived by day on the 11th and on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion on the 12th. They were to keep 5 miles to the north of route SS as was given in 'TON 4, appendix 3' [operation orders for Operation Torch] to arrive at Gibraltar at 1430 A/12 and 1630 A/12 respectively. (73) 11 Nov 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) attacked but missed German submarine U-73 about 25 nautical miles south-south-east of Carboneras, Spain in position 36°44'N, 01°48'W. The British claim to have sunk the Uboat but this was not the case. The Germans noted at 1040 hours the boat was badly shaken. Cause not known. 1003 A/11 - Sighted a ship bearing 180°. Altered course to close. 1014 A/11 - Identified the target as the conning tower of a Uboat. Range 7000 yards. Brought all torpedo tubes to the ready. 1037 A/11 - In position 36°44'N, 01°48W fired a full salvo of six torpedoes from 5200 yards. 1041 A/11 - Heard one loud explosion. The HE of the target ceased. 1044 A/11 - The Uboat was seen to sink by the stern. 1046 A/11 - Fast HE was heard. There was nothing in sight except the sinking Uboat. It was thought another Uboat was in the area. 1100 A/11 - No HE could be heard. Started to close the Uboat's last position. 1130 A/11 - Sighted a large liner bearing 070°. Course was 220°. Range 7 nautical miles. Speed 14 knots. 1143 A/11 - The liner was seen to come towards. Ursula was now in the position where the Uboat was during the attack. 1148 A/11 - Heard one explosion. The liner was coming straight towards. Range was 1500 yards. 1228 A/11 - The liner disappeared on course 230°. [The liner was most likely the empty troop transport Otranto (British, 20026 GRT, built 1925) which reported being attacked but missed around this time in roughly the same position.] 1232 A/11 - Sighted a Uboat following the liner. Unfortunately no more torpedoes were on board (Ursula carried no reloads, the Folbots for the special operation had been carried instead) so the enemy could not be attacked with torpedoes. Closed up for gun action. But the Uboat dived shortly afterwards. It was thought it was a diffent Uboat than the one previously attacked. HMS Ursula remained patrolling in the vicinity hoping the enemy would surface again. At 2100 A/11, having seen nothing further of the enemy, HMS Ursula set course to continue return to Gibraltar. An enemy report on the enemy submarine was passed at 2336 A/11. [Note: in both cases however the Uboat was U-73 which had not been sunk in the torpedo attack.] (75) 12 Nov 1942 Around 1630 A/12, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) ended her 32nd war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (1) 20 Nov 1942 At 1125 Z/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 33rd war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Toulon and Genoa. HMS Ursula also carried two Folbots and a party of two that could be employed for train wrecking if a favourable opportunity was to occur. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ursula during this patrol see the map below. (1) 25 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 0855 A/25, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) informed the Capt. S.8 of the following; My position 41°20'N, 04°22'E. Delayed by weather and engine defect. Intend proceed on surface today and tomorrow. A weather report was also included in the signal. [Though not mentioned in the signal, the high power periscope was also out of action due to flooding. This periscope was out of action during the remainder of the patrol.] (76) 25 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 2123 A/25 the Capt. S.8 informed HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) of the following; P 54 in position 41°35'N, 04°50'E at 0601 A/25. Ursula in position 41°20'N, 04°22'E at 0835 A/25. Estimated speed of advance 5 knots until daylight 26th when both submarines are to proceed diving by day. Submerged bombing restrictions arranged accordingly, and also now in force north of 43°N. (76) 27 Nov 1942 At 1004 A/27, south-east of Ile du Levant (Levant Island) in position 42°56'N, 06°34'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sighted a 7000 tons tanker in ballast proceeding on course 050° at 8.5 knots. At 1130 A/27, a 5000 tons merchant vessel in ballast was seen proceeding on course 050° at 8.5 knots. At 1600 A/27, a 3000 tons merchant vessel in ballast was seen proceeding on course 045° at 8.5 knots. None were attacks as all were in ballast and there was too much sweel to set the torpedoes at less than 12 feet. Gunnery action was deemed to risky so close to the coast. At 1830 A/27, HMS Ursula surfaced in position 42°53'N, 06°33'E. Lt. Lakin had decided to coast crawl up the Italian Riviera. At 2157 A/27, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1558 A/27 was received which stated that HMS Ursula now also had freedom of action in the patrol area that had originally been assigned to HMS P 54. (77) 28 Nov 1942 In the evening, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) closed the coast to examine the coastal railway. A fairly deserted place with a tunnel was noted in the neighbourhood of San Lorenzo. (1) 28 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1540 A/28, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) of the following; A/S schooners may be encountered about 9 miles apart on line 110° from Cape Mele to Gorgona Island and one half way Gorgona and Caprata. These schooners are fitted with hydrophones, 20 depth charges, but apparently no Asdics, also aircraft sound locators, and they are in R/T touch with Genoa, Spezia, Liverno, or Porto Ferranto. (76) 29 Nov 1942 At 0900 A/29, west of Imperia, in approximate position 43°49'N, 07°55'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sighted a tug and two trawlers proceeding on course 250° in line abreast one mile from the shore. It was presumed they were sweeping the channel. At 1430 A/29, a 2000 tons merchant vessel in ballast flying the Spanish colours was sighted proceeding on course 230° 1 mile from the shore. At 1815 A/29, HMS Ursula surfaced and closed the coast near Alassio to examine a railway tunnel and the train service. At 2100 A/29, course was set to proceed to the north-east towards Savona. (1) 30 Nov 1942 Druring the night of 30 November / 1 December 1942, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) landed a party of two to sabotage a railway tunnel near Laigueglia. The party was succesful and was re-embarked later that night. 0630 A/30 - Dived in position 44°19'N, 08°31'E and approached Savona to within 1 mile. No shipping of interest was seen in the harbour. 0830 A/30 - Three A/S schooners left the harbour and proceeded to the south-east and north-west. 0900 A/30 - Two vessels, thought to be minesweepers, left and proceeded to the south-west. 1130 A/30 - Proceeded towards Varazze, no shipping of interest was seen there either. Set course to the south-west to proceed towards Cape Noli. 1415 A/30 - Sighted a small 800 tons coaster making for Savona about 1.5 miles from the shore. Enemy course was 025°. 1500 A/30 - A 1000 tons merchant vessel displaying Spanish colours passed steering 025° for Savona. Considered it too small for a torpedo and gunnery action was out of the question with Ursula being only one mile from the shore. 1818 A/30 - Surfaced in position 44°06'N, 08°22'E and commenced to close the coast near Cape Mele to 500 yards. 2015 A/30 - Launched a Folbot with a 2 men party. They were to wreck a train in a tunnel near the village of Laigueglia. The party consisted of Captain Richard Percy Livingstone (SBS) and Sergeant S. Wetherall. Due to the presence of a sentry, the demolition charges had to be laid 150 yards from the tunnel. 2115 A/30 - Ursula retired to seaward. 2335 A/30 - Returned to the rendez-vouz position and re-embarked the folbot party. They had managed to place their charges. 0008 A/1 - Ursula proceeded to seaward. A large flash and an explosion came from the direction of Laigueglia. These were followed by three smaller flashes. It was thought that a train had set off the charges. [See the event of 1 December 1942 for the continuation of the events.] (78) 1 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sank the Italian auxiliary A/S schooner V 135 / Togo (108 GRT) with scuttling charges about 5 miles south of Capo Mele, Italy (Approximate position 43°54'N, 08°17'E). According to Italian documents the crew of 16 abandoned the vessel, three of them were wounded. 0030 A/1 - While proceeding away from the coast after the train wrecking mission sighted a darkened ship ahead. Position was Cape Mele 120°, 4 nautical miles. Manned the gun and closed to 750 yards. 0100 A/1 - Opened fire with the deck gun from 500 yards. The 6th round was a hit. This hit was followed by more hits. The crew was seen to abandon ship. 0130 A/1 - Ursula secured alongside the schooner. It was the 'Togo' of about 100 tons. She was fitted with 2 .3" Breda machine guns, 12 small depth charges. All documents that could be found were taken on board Ursula and scuttling charges were placed. 0215 A/1 - Slipped and proceeded clear. 0240 A/1 - A magnificent explosion extending 200 feet into the air. The schooner was completely destroyed. 0630 A/1 - Dived in position 44°04'N, 08°24'E. Closed the coast near Albenga and examined the coastal railway. 1028 A/1 - Sighted an armed tug and two schooners in line abreast passing to the northward. They were presumably sweeping the channel as they were about 1.5 miles from the shore. 1100 A/1 - Proceeded towards Alassio to observe the results of last nights train wrecking. Unfortunately visibility was poor and the site of the wreck was behind the town and nothing could be seen. There was however a large goods train at Alassio station which appeared to be unable to proceed any further. 1928 A/1 - Surfaced in position 43°58'N, 08°20'E and closed the railway viaduct at Albenga. The railway runs across a massive stone viaduct for about 2.5 miles before entering the town from the north and these are no houses in the vicinity and the road runs about a mile clear. It was decided that this was an excellent place to commence a 'train shoot' - particularly as a train squarely hit would topple off the viaduct into the sea. 2100 A/1 - Stopped 400 yards off the centre of the viaduct in 3 fathoms of water, and prepared to shoot at the next train. It was a particularly dark night with no moon or stars and cloudy. 2145 A/1 - Two white rocket flares were suddenly fired from a position about 350 yards on the starboard bow. The surrounding country and HMS Ursula were lit up with disconcerting clarity. Lt. Lakin decided to retire immediately before the next flare would be accompanied by something more solid. Ursula dived and made of at full submerged speed. 2146 A/1 - Hardly had Ursula turned to seaward and there was a rumble when a train crossed the viaduct. 2200 A/1 - No further flares had been fired so it was decided to return to the viaduct in a different spot. These was some activity at the Albenga end and eventually after someone had inspected the entire track with a torch a single engine crossed bravely and slowly but after that no more trains were sighted. At 2240 A/1, HMS Ursula retired to seaward to charge the battery. (1) 1 Dec 1942 In a signal 1546 A/1, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN); Leave patrol P.M. on 4 December and return to Gibraltar, diving by day by reverse of outward route. Passing position 'U' at 1800 A/4, position 'T' at 2230 A/7, position 'S' at 0130 A/9, position 'R' at 2130 A/9 and arriving at Gibraltar 1600 A/10 proceeding on the surface at discretion of the Commanding Officer on the 10th. [We have been unable to identify these positions HMS Ursula was to pass through.] (79) 2 Dec 1942 At 0645 A/2, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) dived in position 43°49'N, 08°01'E and proceeded to examine Oneglia where there should be a large olive oil producing factory. On closing the massive cluster of oil storing tanks were seen in the centre of the town. HMS Ursula then proceeded towards San Lorenzo where an ideal deserted place for a train shoot was discovered. At 1740 A/2, retired to seaward and surfaced early to charge the battery. At 2230 A/2, when in position 43°50'N, 07°56'E, HMS Ursula commenced to close the coast at San Lorenzo to 400 yards. The coast was unlit and quite deserted, situated between two villages. At 2300 A/2, a westbound goods train appeared. Fire was opened from a range of 500 yards. The first round was a very near miss just above the engine, which burst on the rock face of the rainly immediately behind the cab. The driver of the train immediately switched out all lights. Ursula continued to fire allowing for the trains advance. One, possibly two hits were obtained on the train, and several more on the posts supporting the overhead wires, which all came down with a run causing a series of magnificent 4000 volt sparks and electric blue flashes along the whole length of the train. 13 Rounds in all were fired, and then the gin showed signs of its old complaint - jambing. By this time the train had rolled round the corner of the next cape into the village of San Stefano. [Italian sources show that one wagon was hit and electric cable was down on a length of 500 metres.] At 2330 A/2, HMS Ursula made a detour out to sea and approached Oneglia which was five miles to the northward. (1) 3 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) bombarded olive oil storage tanks at Oneglia, Italy. Later on this day HMS Ursula sank the German transport Sainte Marguerite II (1855 GRT, built 1921) about 30 nautical miles south-east of Cannes, France in position 43°11'N, 07°19'E. This vessel was bound from Marseilles, France to Naples, Italy. The German survivors were left with the impression that the submarine was American but no US submarines operated in the Mediterranean during the war. Closed to within 150 yards of Oneglia pier. Nothing ashore stirred. There were a few lights on in houses and a car or two, whose headlights assisted Ursula in identifying the target. 0125 A/3 - Opened fire from 700 yards. Round 1 was a bit low and round 2 was over. Round 3 felled a 250 feet tall chimney with a resounding clatter of bricks and a cloud of dust. The ensuing 8 to 10 rounds surpassed the expectations and all were direct hits on the olive oil tanks, each shell bursting with an unusually large red flash, and soon a cloud of dense black smoke arose. But then the gun again jambed. After five minutes of struggling the shell was extracted and Ursula carried on firing. After firing three more rounds, which all hit, a starshell burst about 7000 yards out to sea illuminating both the town and Ursula. Intermittent popping of guns began and Lt. Lakin decided the party was over and gave order to retire at full speed on the surface while throwing overboard six smoke candles which formed a protective smoke screen. Another star shell was fired further out to sea and some shells started to fall about three miles away. Then a single search light opened up about 1000 yards on the port beam. This swept over Ursula three times. A little while later this searchlight found Ursula and two guns situated on the top of Cape Berta opened fire which was rather accurate. So at 0150 A/3, HMS Ursula dived in position 43°52'N, 08°02'E. 0322 A/3 - Surfaced in position 43°48'N, 07°59'E. All was quiet. It was disappointing that olive oil was not sufficiently inflammable to cause a spectacular fire but even so it was felt that some damage was done to the 'Italian stomach'. 0630 A/3 - Dived in position 43°26'N, 07°42'E. 1805 A/3 - Surfaced in position 43°11'N, 07°19'E and almost immediately sighted a darkened ship bearing 270°. Range was 3.5 nautical miles. Enemy course was 050°. Closed at full speed. Brought the tubes to the ready and manned the gun. The ship appeared to be in ballast and was of about 2000 tons. 1830 A/3 - Turned to starboard and brought the gun to bear. Range was 750 yards. 1831 A/3 - Opened fire. Several hits were obtained but after a few minutes the gun again jammed. 1835 A/3 - Fired one torpedo from about 500 yards. The torpedo ran under. 1840 A/3 - The ship was now stopped and the gun was in working order again so three more rounds were fired. 1850 A/3 - Closed the ship and noticed that the crew was abandoning her. The master and chief engineer were taken from the boat. 1900 A/3 - Went alongside the target and boarded her. Some provisions were taken from her as well as documents. 1940 A/3 - The seacocks and condeser inlets to the engine room were opened as well as the flood connection to both holds. 2040 A/3 - Cast off and waited for the ship to sink. 0030 A/4 - The ship had still not sunk. She was boarded again and scuttling charges were placed. Half an hour later the charges went off and the ship sank almost immediately by the bows. (1) 4 Dec 1942 At 0105 A/4, in position 43°10'N, 07°14'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted ship bearing 270°. Ursula closed to investigate. At 0130 A/4, the ship was identified as the Swiss chartered ship Nuestra Senora del Carmen (Spanish, 3482 GRT, built 1895). (1) 5 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2110 A/5, the following orders were issued to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) by the Capt. S.8; Proceed by previous route adjusting speed to pass 39°27'N, 01°26'E at 0001 A/7, Gape Palos at 0600 A/, Gape de Gata at 0600 A/9 to arrive at Gibraltar at 1100 A/10. Do not pass 04°55 W before 0600 A/10. (79) 6 Dec 1942 At 1115 A/6, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0942 A/6 which stated the following; German controlled Danish steamship Grete (1563 GRT, built 1931) expected to leave Cartagena P.M. on 6th December for Marseilles. At 1300 A/6, HMS Ursula altered course to close the expected line of advance to Marseilles of the Grete. The German controlled was however not seen. (80) 7 Dec 1942 At 0206 A/7, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2230 A/6 of which the second paragraph contained the following orders for her; HMS Ursula is to pass 38°45'N, 00°41'E at 0800 A/7. 38° 30'N, 01°26'E at 2030 A/7. 38°42'N, 03°02'E at 0800 A/8 thence direct to 37°01'N, 03°06'E to made rendezvous with escort at 0900 A/9 for onward passage to Algiers. At 0400 A/7, course was altered to comply with the above orders. At 1700 A/7, a schooner was sighted which was proceeding towards Ibiza. Decided to try a little mild interference in the name of the enemy. At 1750 A/7, in position 38.41'N, 01.02'E, HMS Ursula surfaced and hoised the Nazi flag taken from the Sainte Marguerite II. Proceeded to close the schooner hoping to obtain some bread and fish. At 1830 A/7, the schooner was brought to with a few bursts of Vickers over the mast. They were induced to lower a boat which came alongside and took over the boarding party, which included Lt. Tweedie who speaks Spanish. It was now rather dark, so the 6" Aldis was 'accidentally' shone on the flag, to make quite certain of our 'identity'. The boarding party soon retirned with the news that the schooner was carrying a cargo of rough salt and that there seemed to be no quantities of food of any kind on board. A bottle of wine, found out later to be of bad quality, was passed and shortly afterwards company was parted with a great deal of Heil-ing. They had no info on German submarines in the vicinity. It is feared that as an attempt to prejustice the Axis cause, this episode had failed. (80) 9 Dec 1942 Around 1130 A/9, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) ended her 33rd war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers. She was escorted in. Identity of the escort, which had joined at 0830 A/9, is not known. (1) 21 Dec 1942 At 1600 A/21, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for her 34th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Marettimo Island proceeding through position 38°01'N, 04°01'E at 0200 A/22 thence dived by day to pass through position 38°20'N, 08°00'E at 1800/23. Further routing would be signalled by Capt. S.10. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ursula during this patrol see the map below. (1) 22 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1147 A/22, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN); From position 38°20'N, 08°00'E at 1800 A/23 direct to patrol within 10 miles of position 38°12'N, 11°50'E by 0300 A/25. (79) 25 Dec 1942 At 2130 A/25, north-west of Marettimo Island in position 38°08'N, 11°40'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) heard fast turbine HE to the south-west. At 2134 A/25, a single ship was sighted at extreme range. This appeared to be a destroyer going from Cagliari towards Marittimo or it was on patrol. Enemy course was 110° at 15 knots. The enemy vessel was lost from sight after three minutes. At 2155 A/25, the HE faded. This was possibly the Italian destroyer Grecale carrying PoW's which sailed from Tunis at 0825 B/25 and arrived at Palermo at 0015 B/26. (1) 26 Dec 1942 At 2135 A/26, north-west of Marettimo Island in position 38°08'N, 11°40'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sighted two destroyers zigging in quarter line at a range of 4000 yards. Enemy base course was 160°, speed 12 to 15 knots. They disappeared in the direction of Marettimo. The place and time of sighting was (almost) identical with the previous night. There is nothing that matches unless there is an error somewhere: The Italian destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Antonio Pigafetta and Legionario, carrying troops, sailed from Palermo at 2000 B/26-2030 B/26 and arrived at Tunis and La Goulette at 0730 B/27-0852 B/27. The Italian torpedo boat Orione sailed from Palermo at 1920 B/26 escorting the steamer Zenobia Martini (Italian 1454 GRT, built 1911) to Pantelleria. The Italian destroyer Saetta sailed from Palermo at 0726 B/26 and arrived at Trapani at 1038 B/26. (1) 26 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0801 A/26, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) of the following; At 0615 A/26 two small merchant ships and one escort to the north-west of Cape San Vito in position 38°26'N, 12°28'E on course 240° at 5 knots. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (79) 28 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German transport Gran (4140 GRT, built 1938, former Norwegian Gran offsite link) about 12 nautical miles north-west of Marettimo Island, Italy in position 38°09'N, 11°54'E. Gran was en-route from Napels to Bizerte and was escorted by the Italian destroyer Lampo and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade. 0530 A/28 - In position 38°09'N, 11°51'E sighted a large merchant ship escorted by two destroyers. Range 10000 yards. Started attack. 0542 A/28 - Dived. 0553 A/28 - In position 38°09'N, 11°54'E fired three torpedoes from 750 yards. Loud explosions were heard after 32, 38 and 45 seconds. These were followed by breaking up noises. Meanwhile Ursula went to 150 feet and retired from the scene. 0606 A/28 - Depth charging started. 0623 A/28 - A destroyer dropped a number of small acoustic charges. Intermittent HE was heard from the other destroyer but no hunt followed. 0715 A/28 - Started to slowly come to periscope depth as it was now daylight. 0750 A/28 - Observed two destroyers stopped in the firing position. There was no sign of the merchant ship. A large number of assorted aircraft were circling overhead. 0850 A/28 - Both destroyers, which were seen to be rather smale so were probably Spica-class torpedo boats, passed at 3000 yards, steering a course of 021° at 21 knots. Both were painted grey and brown camouflage. Their decks were covered with survivors, who were sitting on the boats, gun shields, forecastle and quarter deck. They had a close air escort of Cant 506B's and an umbrella of six Ju 88's, 2 Savoia 85's and several other unknown aircraft. 1012 A/28 - Observed a Cant 506B circling the firing position, now 4 to 5 miles astern. 1013 A/28 - The Cant aircraft was seen to drop two bombs. The splashes were seen and explosions were heard. Two more aircraft were now seen patrolling in the area. 1100 A/28 - All was quiet now. Went to 50 feet and commenced reload. 1215 A/28 - Reload completed. 1613 A/28 - Received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1226 A/28 which stated the following; Force Q leaves Bone dusk 28th through position 38°10'N, 10°50'E at 0001 A/29 to operate to eastward returning to Bone passing 09°00'E at 0700 A/29. 1659 A/28 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1621 A/28 which stated the following; Your vicinity tonight. Clear present area to be in position 38°40'N, 12°10'E by 0400 A/29. If a northbound target is sighted it may be attacked. 1815 A/28 - Surfaced in position 38°12'N, 11°46'E. Proceeded 35 miles to the north-east in accordance with the orders received. (1) 29 Dec 1942 At 0045 A/29, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) arrived in her new patrol area (near 38°40'N, 12°10'E). (1) 30 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) was in rammed and damaged while she was in the process of attacking a southbound convoy of three large transports escorted by four destroyers North of Cape St. Vito, Sicily, near position 38°43'N, 12°40'E. Ursula had penetrated the destroyer screen and was on the point of firing torpedoes from close range when the convoy as a whole changed course and overran Ursula destroying both periscopes and standards, as well as damage to the conning tower. The attack had to be broken off. The convoy attacked was on passage to Bizerta and made up of the transports Alfredo Oriani (3152 GRT, built 1942), Manzoni (4550 GRT, built 1942) and Mario Roselli (6835 GRT, built 1942), escorted by the destroyers Maestrale, Corsaro and Lampo and the torpedo boats Sirio and Pallade. This convoy had sailed from Palermo at 0050 B/30-0200 B/30 and arrived at Bizerte at 1637 B/30-1800 B/30. In the reports of the Maestrale and Lampo, which are available to us, there is no mention of any ship of the convoy ramming a submarine / submerged object. It is thus not known which ship of the convoy struck HMS Ursula. 0023 A/30 - Received a signal, timed 2325 A/29, from Capt. S.10 ordering Ursula to proceed to a new patrol position in 360° - Cape San Vito - 12 nautical miles. Immediately set new course and proceed at full speed for this new patrol position. 0504 A/30 - Shortly after arriving in this patrol area sighted a convoy of three merchant ships escorted by four destroyers. Range was 9000 yards. Course 240°. Speed 15 knots. Started attack. 0509 A/30 - Dived and closed at full submerged speed. Started an attack on the leading merchant ship, the biggest, thought to be of about 5000 tons. 0513 A/30 - The leading destroyer passed ahead. Ursula went a bit deeper then periscope depth when the destroyer passed. 0515 A/30 - Returned to periscope depth. Found that the situation had changed. The convoy had made a zig towards of about 35°. 0520 A/30 - The target was now 600 yards off and came towards. Ursula had to be taken deep but she hang for over a minute at 25 feet. 0522 A/30 - When at 29 feet Ursula was hit by the ship on the periscope standards and bridge. 0525 A/30 - The rear destroyer of the convoy passed overhead. 0600 A/30 - The HE of the convoy faded away. 0601 A/30 - Surfaced and proceeded at full speed to clear the area. The damage was; both periscopes and periscope standards bent and useless. The upper telegraphs were broken, centre section of jumping wire carried away, night light smashed and superficial damage to the bridge. 0621 A/30 - Passed enemy report, timed 0500 A/30, to Capt. S.10. 0643 A/30 - Dived in position 38°43'N, 12°40'E. 0838 A/30 to 1100 A/30 - Heard many underwater explosions. 1150 A/30 - Heard fast HE crossing ahead. This sounded like two destroyers on an easterly course. 1828 A/30 - Surfaced in position 38°53'N, 12°38'E and proceeded to the northward. 2100 A/30 - Passed situation report, timed 2000 A/30, to Capt. S.10. Altered course to return to Algiers. Due to the damage sustained the patrol had to be broken off. (1) 30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2100 A/30, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) to be in position 38°16'N, 12°20'E by 2100 A/30. Enemy torpedo boat patrols were to be expected as was enemy shipping around midnight. In a signal timed 2000 A/30, HMS Ursula signalled the following to the Capt. S.10. This signal was also repeated to the Capt. S.8.; At 0522 A/30 was rammed by merchant ship during diving moonlight attack on three medium merchant ships escorted by four destroyers in position 360° - Cape San Vito - 12 miles. Both periscopes out of action and standards broken also superficial damage to bridge. Received routings, dived satisfactorily. Present position 39°11'N, 12°34'E. Course 245°. Speed of advance 120 miles per day. At 0553 A/28 sank southbound 6000 ton troop transport during similar attack in position 38°09'N, 11°51'E. three hits. five torpedoes remaining. A weather report was also included in the signal. (79) 30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2209 A/30, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); Aircraft are patrolling your area tonight after moonrise in endeavour to bomb enemy torpedo boats but enemy aircraft must be expected hunting you. (79) 31 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0029 A/31, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN); Your 2000 A/30. Fine work. Proceed through position 38°31'N, 10°44'E, thence course 270° to cross 08°00'E at 2001 A/l, thence as ordered by Capt. S.8. In a signal timed 1206 A/31, the Capt. S.8, sent the following orders to HMS Ursula; Proceed diving by day. Pass through positions 38°31'N, 08°00'E at 2001 A/1, 38°01'N, 04°48'E at 0001 A/3, thence on surface at your discretion to rendezvous with escort for Algiers in position 37°01'N, 03°17'E at 1100 A/3. (79) 2 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1432 A/2, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) of the following; 1) Your escort will be a motor launch. 2) Large fast convoy and Force H will arrive at Algiers at 0700 A/3 from the westward. 3) Three tank landing ships escorted by four minesweeepers will arrive at Algiers at 1100 A/3. Also a slow convoy will arrive at Algiers at 1400 A/3. All from westwards. 4) HMS Welshman (Capt. W.H.D. Friedberger, DSO, RN)leaves Algiers before daylight on 3 January proceeding coastwise to the east. In a signal timed 1800 A/2, HMS Ursula informed the Capt. S.8 of the following; Your signal timed 1206 A/31. Delayed by weather. Expect to arrive at rendezvous position at 0300 A/4. In a signal timed 2104 A/2, the Capt. S.8, sent the following to HMS Ursula; Your signal timed 1800 A/2 acknowledged. Adjust speed so as to pass through position 38°01'N, 04°48'E at 1800 A/3 to arrive at rendezvous not before 0730 A/4. (81) 3 Jan 1943 In a singal timed 1614 A/3, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) of the following; 1) Your escort will be HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. T.E. Williams, RNR). 2) Four H.S.L. leaves Algiers dawn 4th to eastwards. French convoy sails 0500 A/4th to westward. 3) HMS P 54 (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) leaves Algiers 1600 A/3 course 037°. HMS Sturgeon (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) leaves Algiers 0830 A/4 for exercise off Carbon. (81) 4 Jan 1943 Around 1100 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) ended her 34th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers. The damage Ursula had sustained required extensive repairs. Also she suffered from generator problems yet again. She was escorted in by HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. T.E. Williams, RNR) which had joined around 0740 A/4. (82) 20 Feb 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) carried out independent exercises off Algiers. She was escorted by two M/L's. (83) 21 Feb 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Algiers with HMS Lightning (Cdr. H.G. Walters, DSC, RN) and HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN). (83) 22 Feb 1943 Around 1800 A/22, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for Gibraltar. She is to return to the U.K. She was routed through positions 'A' (38°05'N, 01°35'E. at 0800 A/23. 'B' (135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles) at 0800 A/24. 'C' (180° - Cape De Gata - 5 miles at 0500 A/25. 'D' (180° - Cape Calaburras - 5 miles at 0600 A/26th to arrive at Gibraltar at 1030 A/26. Position 'D' was not to be passed before 0600 A/26. For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during this passage see the map below. (1) 26 Feb 1943 Around 1230 A/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Algiers. On arrival D/G trials were carried out. (1) 1 Mar 1943 At 1230 A/1, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K. At 1340 A/1, a collision with an unidentified Spanish trawler was narrowly avoided. HMS Ursula was to pass through the following positions; 'T' (35°49'N, 06°06'E) at 1845 A/1. 'U' (35°53'N, 06°15'E) at 2000 A/1. 'V' (36°37'N, 07°24'E) at 0700 A/2. 'W' (36°51'N, 09°13'E) at 0500 A/3. 'C' (42°59'N, 12°26'E) at 0330 A/7 shifting W/T watch at 0330/7 thence as ordered by F.O.S. (Flag Officer Submarines). In signals timed 1126 A/4 and 1529 A/4, HMS Ursula was ordered by F.O.S. to proceed on route 'Red' diving by day to make rendezvous with the escort in position 'X' (180° - Bishops Rock - 10 miles) at 0700 A/13. [No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (1) 6 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1216 A/6, the Flag Officer Gibraltar informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) that four or five Uboats may be crossing your course between 42°N and 45°N. (84) 8 Mar 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) attacked a German Uboat with four torpedoes in position 44°48'N, 12°13'W. No hits were obtained. The Uboat attacked was most likely the eastbound U-462 which was in approximate position 45°09'N, 12°25'W (Grid BE 9354) at 1200 B/8 and 45°09'N, 11°55'W (Grid BE 9364) at 1600 B/8. She reported nothing though. 1430 A/8 - Heard HE bearing Red 150°. The Officer of the Watch sighted the conning tower of a Uboat bearing Red 165°, course uncertain. 14450 A/8 - Lt. Profit now also sighted the Uboat throught the Low Power periscope (The attack periscope was defective). Bearing Green 25°. Enemy course 040°. Range 2300 yards. Started attack. 1451 A/8 - In position 44°48'N, 12°13'W fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards. No hits were obtained. 1647 A/8 - Surfaced and passed a contact report timed 1451 A/8. (1) 13 Mar 1943 At 1030 A/13, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN), makes rendezvous off Bishops Rock with her escort, the ASW trawler HMS Asie (Skr. E.A. Mutten, RNR). (1) 14 Mar 1943 Around 1930 A/14, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Fort Blockhouse - Gosport) from Gibraltar. [As no logs are available for the months of March and April 1943 some details for these months will be missing.] (1) 20 Mar 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) is docked at Portsmouth. At 0545 B/31, the light now having improved, an Oceania-class liner was seen to the north-west. Range was about 7000 yards. The convoy made a large alteration of course to the south-west at this time but a firing position could not be attained.
By 0700 B/31, the convoy had passed. HMS Ursula altered course to 000° to return to Malta.
[For the composition of the convoy see the event of 29 August 1941.] (36)
2 Sep 1941 Around 0700 B/2, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) ended her 23rd war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (36)
10 Sep 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) conducted practive attacks off Malta on HMS Gloxinia (Lt.Cdr. A.J.C. Pomeroy, RNVR) and HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR).
After the exercises, HMS Ursula went to the Malta Dockyard where she was docked in No.2 graving dock. (49)
12 Sep 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) is undocked. (49)

16 Sep 1941 Around 1315 B/16, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 24th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was to patrol off Tripoli, Libya in position 32°53'N, 13°45'E. She was part of a patrol line with HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) (in position 032°52'N, 15°03'E), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) (in position 32°57'N, 14°53'E) and HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wright, DSC, RN) (in position 33°01'N, 14°42'E).
An important fast Italian troop convoy had departed Taranto for Tripoli that day.

The convoy in question was made up of the Italian troop transports Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933), Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932) and Vulcania (24469 GRT, built 1928) which were on passage from Taranto to Tripoli. They were escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Antonio da Noli, Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani.
On departure from Malta a practice attack was made on HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). (50)
16 Sep 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ursula during her 24th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) see the map below.

18 Sep 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) attacked the Italian troop transport / liner Vulcania (24469 GRT, built 1928) about 30 nautical miles east of Tripoli, Libya in approximate position 32°55'N, 13°52'E. Four torpedoes were fired but all missed their target although it was thought that one hit was obtained.
[For the composition of the convoy Vulcania was part of see the event for 16 September 1941.]
0200 B/18 - Dived in position 32°58'N, 13°50'E, and patrolled at 70 feet listening for HE.
0610 B/18 - Received the contact report of HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN) timed 0419 B/18 in which she reported to have damaged to troop transports.

0625 B/18 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat with a MAS boat alongside. Stopped two miles to the southward. Later altered course to the eastward to get on the expected line of approach of the convoy.
0656 B/18 - Sighted a large transport with a Navigatori-class destroyer as escort. [This was most likely Antoniotto Usodimare.]

0705 B/18 - In approximate position 32°55'N, 13°52'E fired four torpedoes from 3500 yards at what is thought to be the troop transport / liner Vulcania. It was thought the one hit was obtained. [This was not the case though].
0712 B/18 - Altered course to the eastwards. No counter attack had developed.
0725 B/18 - Came to periscope depth and sighted the target 6 miles to the westward with a list of 5° to starboard. Went deep to reload the torpedo tubes.

0751 B/18 - Suspended reloading the torpedo tubes. Two destroyers were heard approaching from the west at 24 knots. Two depth charges were dropped astern. The destroyers passed close up the starboard side and continued to the eastwards at high speed.
0824 B/18 - Reloading continued.
0914 B/18 - Completed reloading.
1040 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth and sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat hunting 5 miles to the westward in the position of the attack. Went deep.
1100 B/18 - The torpedo boat passed overhead.
1040 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth. The torpedo boat was seen still hunting to the south-eastward.
1200 B/18 - An Oriani-class destroyer was sighted proceeding along the coast towards Tripoli at 20 knots.
1225 B/18 - An armed yacht was seen hunting to the south-westward.
1235 B/18 - This yacht was seen approaching. Went deep to evade.
1235 B/18 to 1405 B/18 - The yacht and another vessel, thought to be a torpedo boat, hunted the submarine. No depth charges were dropped.
1430 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
1540 B/18 - Heard four loud explosions fairly close.
1710 B/18 - Sighted the yacht hunting inshore near Khoms.
2045 B/18 - Surfaced in position 33°02'N, 14°12'E. Set course for position 32°53'N, 14°12'E.
2100 B/18 - Sighted signalling ahead. Stopped.
2125 B/18 - Again sighted signalling ahead. Considered it to be a light on shore. Proceeded.
2322 B/18 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2140 B/18, recalling HMS Ursula. Set course to return to Malta. (50)
20 Sep 1941 Around 0800 B/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 24th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (50)

23 Sep 1941 Around 1935 B/23, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 25th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the Southern approaches to the Strait of Messina.

At 2112 B/23, a possible submarine was sighted for which the rudder was put hard to starboard. At 2118 B/23, HMS Ursula proceeded on her previous course of 072°. HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) had departed Malta half an hour before HMS Ursula and may thus have been the 'submarine' sighted. [No Italian submarines were in the area.]
[No patrol report is available for this patrol. The log for September list no attacks but for October no log is available. As far as is known to us no attacks were carried out.]
For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during this patrol see the map below. [Map is incomplete, only the positions for September can be shown.]

25 Sep 1941 At 2155 B/25, in position 37°49'N, 15°34'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) heard turbine HE on bearing 300°. HMS Ursula dived to avoid being detected. At 2232 B/25, HMS Ursula surfaced. Nothing was sighted though. (49)
26 Sep 1941 At 0235 B/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) heard turbine HE on bearing 140°. Nothing was sighted though.
At 1010 B/25, south-south-west of Cape dell'Armi, in approximate position 37°54'N, 15°39'E, a sailing vessel was sighted bearing 090° at a range of three miles. Course of the vessel was 285°.
At 1230 B/25, smoke was sighted and five minutes later a cruiser and seven destroyers were sighted.
At 1300 B/25, the cruiser, thought to be a heavy cruiser of the Trento-class, and seven destroyers were seen to proceed up the Straits of Messina. They passed out of range.

These were the heavy cruiser Trieste (Third Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Corazziere, Carabiniere, Ascari, Lanciere, Aviere and Camicia Nera which sailed from Taranto at 2200 B/25-2300 B/25 for La Spezia but were diverted to counter Operation HALBERD, they were joined by the heavy cruisers Trento and Gorizia which sailed from Messina at 1410 B/26 while Aviere and Camicia Nera were detached to Messina and arrived there at 1818 B/26-1830 B/26. The three cruisers and four remaining destroyers arrived at La Maddalena at 0015 B/29-0416 B/29

At 1400 B/26, a destroyer / torpedo boat was sighted bearing 297°. enemy course was 010°. This was thought to be possibly the Italian submarine chaser Albatros which is possible as she had sailed from Messina at 1900 B/25 for an A/S search and returned at 1500 B/26. It is however not known to us if she carried out the A/S search to the south or north of the Straits. Obviously, her sortie on the next day was north of the Straits. (49)
27 Sep 1941 At 1305 B/27, south of the Straits of Messina, in approximate position 37°58'N, 15°32'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) a schooner was sighted bearing 150°. Enemy course was 360°.
At 1455 B/27, a hospital ship was seen proceeding on course 025° northwards along the Sicilian coast.
This was most probably the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) which had sailed from Augusta at 1045 B/27 for Palermo (arrival time not known to us).
At 1945 B/27, a hospital ship was seen proceeding up the Calabrian coast.
This was most probably the Italian hospital ship Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which had sailed from Benghazi at 1500 B/25 and arrived at Naples at 0915 B/28.
At 2315 B/27, HE was picked up.
At 2355 B/27, in position 37°44'N, 15°34'E, HMS Ursula dived to evade a small torpedo boat. HMS Ursula surfaced at 0042 B/28. (49)

29 Sep 1941 At 0445 B/29, south of the Straits of Messina, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) sighted a hospital ship proceeding down the Sicilian coast. It was thought to be the Italian California (12767 GRT, built 1920).

This must have been Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, 1928) which arrived at Augusta at 0920 B/29 from Tripoli (after searching for the survivors of the Albatros, north of the Straits (so she was indeed moving southwards).
At 1300 B/29, a two-funnelled ship was seen proceeding from Catania towards Cape Spartivento.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (49)
30 Sep 1941 At 0630 B/30, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) sighted a schooner proceeding down the middle of the Straits of Messina.
At 0655 B/30, a submarine was seen proceeding up the Straits of Messina. An attack was not possible and the enemy passed out of range.
[We have been unable to identify this 'submarine'. None should have been in the area.]
At 1500 B/30, a schooner, proceeding under sail, was seen bearing 270° at a range of 3 miles.
[No further details for this patrol are available as there is neither a logbook or patrol report.] (49)
6 Oct 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 25th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (51)

17 Oct 1941 Around 1230 A/17, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 26th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the east coast of Tunisia off the Gulf of Hammamet in area 'G' joining positions 37°24'N, 15°52'E, 37°41'N, 16°51'E, 38°33'N, 16°28'E and 38°16'N, 15°29'E.
[No logs are available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (36)

18 Oct 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Beppe (4859 GRT, built 1912) some 45 nautical miles west of Lampedusa Island in position 35°25'N, 11°39'E. The damaged Beppe is towed to Tripoli, Libya.

Beppe was in a convoy with was further made up of the Italian transports Caterina (4838 GRT, built 1920), Marin Sanudo (5081 GRT, built 1926), Paolina (4894 GRT, built 1911), Probitas (5084 GRT, built 1918) and Amba Aradam (405 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the Italian destroyers Folgore, Fulmine, Antoniotto Usodimare, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani sailed from Naples at 0955 B/16-1335 B/16. At 1500 B/16, Probitas which had developed engine defects turned back, escorted by Fulmine. They arrived at Naples at 1648 B/16-1652 B/16).

At 0910 B/18, Beppe was hit in the forepeak, her rudder was out of the water. Vincenzo Gioberti picked up some of the crew and her military passengers, in all 68 persons. With some difficulty, Beppe managed to do 4 knots. Soon, it was realised that she needed towing and the destroyer attempted to do so but the cable quickly broke. At 0400 B/19, Beppe and Vincenzo Gioberti were joined by the destroyer Nicoloso da Recco which had sailed from Tripoli with the destroyer Sebenico at 1900 B/18 to assist them. Vincenzo Gioberti was short of water and Nicoloso da Recco signalled her to proceed to Tripoli where she arrived at 1707 B/19. At 2030 B/18, the Italian tug Ciclope sailed from Tripoli and joined them at 0900 B/19. In the meantime, Sebenico proceeded to reinforce the escort of the convoy which had come under air attack from Swordfish aircraft of 830 Squadron (FAA) at 2210 B/18. The steamer Caterina had been hit by a torpedo; the destroyer Alfredo Oriani stood by her. The German tug Max Berendt sailed from Tripoli at 0430 B/19 to tow her, followed by the Italian tug Marsigli at 0500 B/19. The torpedo boats Generale Antonino Cascino and Calliope sailed from Zuara and Sidi Bilal respectively at 0445 B/19 to assist the stricken vessel, but Caterina sank at 1720 B/19 in position 350° - Tripoli Light - 62 miles. Generale Antonino Cascino picked up 195 survivors, there were four victims.

The convoy which now consisted of Marin Sanudo, Paolina and Amba Aradam, screened by the destroyers Folgore and Antoniotto Usodimare arrived at Tripoli at 1130 B/19-1155 B/19, followed by Generale Antonino Cascino at 1525 B/19, Alfredo Oriani and Sebenico at 2205 B/19-2230 B/19, Max Berendt at 0525 B/20 and sailed again to assist Beppe. Nicoloso da Recco arrived at Tripoli at 1445 B/20. Beppe towed by Ciclope, Marsigli and Max Berendt and escorted by Calliope arrived at 0610 B/21-0700 B/21.
0706 A/18 - In position 35°27'N, 11°45'E sighted smoke bearing 306°. Started attack.

0802 A/18 - In position 35°25'N, 11°39'E fired four torpedoes at two merchant vessels of about 6000 tons. Ranges were 6000 and 7000 yards. It was thought that two hits were obtained, one on each target.
0825 A/18 to 0900 A/18 - One of the escorts carried out counter-attacks in which nine depth charges were dropped. No damage was done to Ursula.
0912 A/18 - Sighted a merchant vessel stopped in the position of the attack. Altered course towards and started to reload No.3 torpedo tube.
1005 A/18 - The vessel was seen to be lower in the water.
1100 A/18 - Nothing in sight. It was thought the vessel had sunk.
1830 A/18 - Surfaced and set course for Malta. (36)
19 Oct 1941 Around 1205 A/19, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) arrived at Malta to embark four new torpedoes.
At 2030 A/19, she departed again to resume her 26th war patrol in her original patrol area (area 'G'). (36)

20 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1041 A/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN); If possible establish patrols as follows from dusk tonight 20/10. HMS Unbeaten in position 073° - Cape Murro di Porco - 7.5 miles. HMS Ursula in position 118° - Cape Murro di Proco - 11.5 miles. Large destroyers might pass to the south-east during night. After attack or at 0430 A/21 proceed in execution of previous orders.
This referred to a troop transport run by the Italian destroyers Antonio da Noli, Nicolò Zeno and Emanuele Pessagno from Augusta to Benghazi.

They were transporting German troops; Antonio da Noli: 9 officers and 258 other ranks. Nicolò Zeno: 7 officers and 263 other ranks. Emanuele Pessagno: 4 officers and 262 other ranks. They sailed from Augusta at 1950 B/20-2025 B/20 and arrived at Benghazi at 1600 B/21-1840 B/21. (52)

22 Oct 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) carried out a reconnaissance of the coast North of Cape Spartivento to select a suitable bridge to bombard. At 0900 A/22, a suitable target was seen near position 38°06'N, 16°14'E.
[Italian sources show that the bridge and telegraph line sustained some damage but seven unexploded shells were found near the bridge.]
1419 A/22 - Ursula surfaced 1500 yards from the bridge. 77 Rounds were fired and the bridge was damaged by 22 direct hits.
1439 A/22 the action was broken off when the gun jammed and an aircraft that was seen to the seaward. (1)

26 Oct 1941 At 1830 A/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1047 A/26 in which she had been ordered to arrive off Malta at 0630 A/28. She was to proceed through positions 36°57'N, 16°15'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E on the way back. (52)
28 Oct 1941 Around 0830 A/28, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 26th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (36)

6 Nov 1941 Around 1500 B/6, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 27th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Misrata, Libya in area 'D 1' enclosed by lines joining the following positions; 32°45'N, 15°21'E, 32°45'N, 15°00'E, 32°45'N, 16°30'E, 32°00'N, 16°30'E.
En-route to her patrol area, HMS Ursula was to proceed through position 35°30'N, 14°14'E and position 34°10'N, 14°14'E.
[No logs are available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (53)
8 Nov 1941 At 1430 B/8, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) entered her patrol area. (50)

11 Nov 1941 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) attacked a convoy with three torpedoes about 20 nautical miles south-east of Misrata, Libya in position 32°07'N, 15°26'E. No hits were obtained and HMS Ursula was depth charged by the escorts.

This convoy was made up of the transport Pertusola (Italian, 1502 GRT, built 1882), which was towing the Italian auxiliary schooner V 236 / Imperia (222 GRT, built 1912), and the transport Ascianghi (Italian, 610 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the torpedo boats Generale Antonio Cascino and Calliope). The convoy had sailed from Benghazi at 1700 B/9-1800 B/9 and arrived at Tripoli at 1400 B/12-1510 B/12. A torpedo passed under the bridge of Pertusola without exploding. Calliope attacked the submarine with two depth charge patterns, recording three explosions and a large oil slick and believed the submarine sunk.
1350 B/11 - Sighted smoke.
1400 B/11 - Sighted three schooners escorted by RD-type minesweepers proceeding northbound close inshore of Ras Khara.

These were the schooners Galileo Galilei (?) and Luigi Razza (113 GRT, built 1938) escorted by the minesweepers RD 1 and RD 16, which sailed from Benghazi on 8 or 9 November and arrived at Tripoli at 0900 B/12.
1615 B/11 - Sighted smoke to the south.

1730 B/11 - Sighted a convoy of two merchant vessels (one of 2000 tons, one of 1000 tons that was towing a sailing vessel). Escort was provided by two torpedo boats, one was seen to be the Generale Antonio Cascino and the other was a more modern one of the Spica class.
1752 B/11 - In position 32°07'N, 15°26'E fired three torpedoes at the 2000 tons merchant vessel from . Both missed as the tracks were sighted and evaded by the target.
1755 B/11 - Both torpedo boats altered towards. Ursula went deep and rigged for depth charging.
1800 B/11 to 1810 B/11 - Fourteen depth charges were dropped. All rather close but Ursula sustained no damage. (50)

14 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1149 B/14, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) to leave patrol P.M./l6 through position 33°00'N, 15°00'E, 35°20'N, 14°00'E and 35°30'N, 14°10'E to arrive Marsamxett (Malta) during daylight/18. (54)

16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1745 B/16, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) to remain on patrol in area D until dusk on the 18th and thence to patrol within 10 miles of position 33°00'N, 14°50'E by dawn/19. (54)

16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1635 B/16, the Capt. S.10 send the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) proceed as ordered in the signal timed 1221 B/16. HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) proceed towards Cephalonia. HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) are to remain on patrol. Further instructions will follow. (54)

18 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1118 B/18, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) to return to Malta through positions 35°00'N, 15°00'E, 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 36°00'N, 14°53'E to arrive before 1800 B/21.

In a signal timed 1545 B/18, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Ursula to leave patrol forthwith and return to Malta passing through positions 33°00'N, 14°50'N, 35°20'N, 14°14'E thence as ordered by Capt. S.10.

In a signal timed 2305 B/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula; Owing to own force movements follow route given as given in my signal timed 1118 B/18 to arrive before 1800 B/20 or at 0730 B/21. (54)
20 Nov 1941 Around 0945 B/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 27th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
HMS Ursula returned from patrol suffering from fuel leaks. Before she was to proceed to Gibraltar she was docked to effect repairs. [Date of docking and undocking is not know to us.] (53)
30 Nov 1941 P.M., HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. She is to proceed to the U.K. for a refit.
On leaving Malta she was to proceed through position 38° 00'N, 11°44'E and then through 38°05'N, 09°00'E around 2300 B/2. Further routing would be signalled by the Capt. S.8.
[No logbook or passage report is available so details may be missing.] (51)

1 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2208 A/1, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS Regent (Lt. W.N.R. Knox, DSC, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN); Onward route. Regent through the following positions. K) 37°20'N, 00°00'E, L) 35°40'N, 02°45'W, M) 35°40'N, 05°00'W. Adjusting speed to pass through position K at 0200 A/6 and L 0700 A/7 to arrive Gibraltar 0900 A/8. URSULA through the following positions; N) 38°05'N, 06°00'E, P) 36°55'N, 00°00'E, L) see above, M) see above. Adjusting speed to pass through position P at 0200 A/7 and L at 0700 A/8 to arrive at Gibraltar at 0900 A/9. Both submarines are to report on crossing 03°00'E if unable to comply with above time within one hour. S.B.R. arranged. Submarines were to change wireless watch at 0001 A/6 to normal watch on Gibraltar broadcast shifting to read M.S. routines. (55)

4 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1853 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) informed the Capt. S.8 that her position at 1900 A/4 was 37°42'N, 04°00'E. Permission is requested to pass points 'P' and 'L' 24 hours early to arrive at Gibraltar at 0900 A/8.
No reply came to this signal. (55)

5 Dec 1941 Not having received an answer to her signal timed 1853 A/4 which had been passed via Rosyth Radio at 1954 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) sent a new signal timed 1746 A/5 to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.). HMS Ursula gave her position at 1800 A/5 as 37°16'N, 01°36'E. Permission was requested to pass points 'P' and 'L' twenty-fours early to arrive at Gibraltar 0900 A/8. A weather report was also included.

In a signal timed 2310 A/5, F.O.C.N.A. replied as follows; Reply to your 1746 A/5. Your 1954 A/4 not received. Remain on patrol within 15 miles of position 'P' leaving to conform with my 2208 A/1. U-Boats patrol your vicinity. HMS Clyde on patrol within 30 miles from the African coast (to the east of Oran). Own aircraft on A/S patrols up to longitude 00°45'W. Shift W/T watch forthwith. (55)

6 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1108 A/6, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) of the following; U-boats believed to be patrolling in the vicinity Alboran Island. To avoid own night patrols do not cross 04°40'W before 0730 A/9 thence proceed on surface direct to Gibraltar. Bombing restrictions are arranged. (55)

8 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1208 A/8, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) of the following; Two destroyers on north and south patrol 20 miles to the east of Gibraltar, aircraft may co-operate. Comply strictly with the instructions in my signal timed 1108 A/6.

In a signal timed 1610 A/8, F.O.C.N.A. informed HMS Ursula of the following; HMS Hermione and six destroyers (these were HMS Maori, HMS Blankney and HMS Exmoor which were to join HMS Laforey, HMS Arrow and HrMs Isaac Sweers already on A/S patrol to the east of Gibraltar) left Gibraltar 1600 A/8 sweeping east at 17 knots returning to Gibraltar at 0800 A/9. HMS Ursula was to remain on patrol within 10 miles of position 35°40'N, 03°20'W until further orders.

In a signal timed 2136 A/8, F.O.C.N.A. sent the following orders to HMS Ursula; Cancel the instructions in my signals 1108 A/6 and 2208 A/1. Leave patrol at 0400 A/9 and proceed direct on the surface to Gibraltar. Endeavour to arrive in daylight. Report E.T.A. T.B.R. arranged. (55)
9 Dec 1941 Around 1520 A/9, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) arrived at Gibraltar. (56)

14 Dec 1941 As her refit in the U.K. can start only around mid-January 1942, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) is sent out on a anti-uboat patrol to the east of Gibraltar within 15 miles of Alboran Island. This is her 28th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean).
[No logs are available for this period so no map can be displayed. Also no patrol report is available.] (57)

14 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1636 A/14, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A) informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) of the following; U-boat attacked by aircraft in position 36°35'N, 01°45'W at 1605 A/14.

In a signal timed 2236 A/14, (F.O.C.N.A) informed HMS Ursula of the following; Aircraft reports sighting enemy submarines on 13 December. At 1047 A/13 in position 36°34'N, 02°40'W. At 1124 A/13 in position 36°15'N, 02°40'W. (55)

15 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1236 A/15, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) of the following; Aircraft report submarine on surface in position 35°38'N, 03°00'W, at 1000 A/15 steering 290°.
In a signal timed 1306 A/15, F.O.C.N.A. informed HMS Ursula of the following; Aircraft report submarine surfaced and dived again in position 35°30'N, 03°12'W at 1050 A/15. (55)

16 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 0236 A/16, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) to leave patrol forthwith and return to Gibraltar through positions 35°55'N, 04°00'W and 35°55'N, 05°00'W thence direct. Report E.T.A. T.B.R. arranged for. (55)
16 Dec 1941 Around 1755 A/16, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 28th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (56)
17 Dec 1941 Around 0800 A/17, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 29th war patrol. She is ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay and end this patrol in the U.K.

It was thought the German battlecruisers Scharnhorst, Gneisenau and the heavy cruiser Prinz Eugen were ready to leave Brest for operations in the North Atlantic and / or an attempt to return to Germany.
HMS Ursula was escorted out by HMS Imperialist (T/Lt. P.E. Kitto, RNR) until around 1815 A/18 when they parted company to the west-south-west of Lisbon in position 38°30'N, 10°00'W.
[No logs are available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (1)
20 Dec 1941 Around 0800 A/20, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) dived in the vicinity of her assigned patrol position [position '13']. (1)

21 Dec 1941 At 2359 A/21, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) left patrol for Portsmouth proceeding through position '14' in accordance with the orders in the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2139 A/20. (58)
