[See the event of 1 December 1942 for the continuation of the events.] (78)

1 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sank the Italian auxiliary A/S schooner V 135 / Togo (108 GRT) with scuttling charges about 5 miles south of Capo Mele, Italy (Approximate position 43°54'N, 08°17'E).
According to Italian documents the crew of 16 abandoned the vessel, three of them were wounded.
0030 A/1 - While proceeding away from the coast after the train wrecking mission sighted a darkened ship ahead. Position was Cape Mele 120°, 4 nautical miles. Manned the gun and closed to 750 yards.
0100 A/1 - Opened fire with the deck gun from 500 yards. The 6th round was a hit. This hit was followed by more hits. The crew was seen to abandon ship.

0130 A/1 - Ursula secured alongside the schooner. It was the 'Togo' of about 100 tons. She was fitted with 2 .3" Breda machine guns, 12 small depth charges. All documents that could be found were taken on board Ursula and scuttling charges were placed.
0215 A/1 - Slipped and proceeded clear.
0240 A/1 - A magnificent explosion extending 200 feet into the air. The schooner was completely destroyed.
0630 A/1 - Dived in position 44°04'N, 08°24'E. Closed the coast near Albenga and examined the coastal railway.
1028 A/1 - Sighted an armed tug and two schooners in line abreast passing to the northward. They were presumably sweeping the channel as they were about 1.5 miles from the shore.

1100 A/1 - Proceeded towards Alassio to observe the results of last nights train wrecking. Unfortunately visibility was poor and the site of the wreck was behind the town and nothing could be seen. There was however a large goods train at Alassio station which appeared to be unable to proceed any further.

1928 A/1 - Surfaced in position 43°58'N, 08°20'E and closed the railway viaduct at Albenga. The railway runs across a massive stone viaduct for about 2.5 miles before entering the town from the north and these are no houses in the vicinity and the road runs about a mile clear. It was decided that this was an excellent place to commence a 'train shoot' - particularly as a train squarely hit would topple off the viaduct into the sea.
2100 A/1 - Stopped 400 yards off the centre of the viaduct in 3 fathoms of water, and prepared to shoot at the next train. It was a particularly dark night with no moon or stars and cloudy.

2145 A/1 - Two white rocket flares were suddenly fired from a position about 350 yards on the starboard bow. The surrounding country and HMS Ursula were lit up with disconcerting clarity. Lt. Lakin decided to retire immediately before the next flare would be accompanied by something more solid. Ursula dived and made of at full submerged speed.
2146 A/1 - Hardly had Ursula turned to seaward and there was a rumble when a train crossed the viaduct.

2200 A/1 - No further flares had been fired so it was decided to return to the viaduct in a different spot. These was some activity at the Albenga end and eventually after someone had inspected the entire track with a torch a single engine crossed bravely and slowly but after that no more trains were sighted.
At 2240 A/1, HMS Ursula retired to seaward to charge the battery. (1)

1 Dec 1942 In a signal 1546 A/1, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN); Leave patrol P.M. on 4 December and return to Gibraltar, diving by day by reverse of outward route. Passing position 'U' at 1800 A/4, position 'T' at 2230 A/7, position 'S' at 0130 A/9, position 'R' at 2130 A/9 and arriving at Gibraltar 1600 A/10 proceeding on the surface at discretion of the Commanding Officer on the 10th.
[We have been unable to identify these positions HMS Ursula was to pass through.] (79)

2 Dec 1942 At 0645 A/2, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) dived in position 43°49'N, 08°01'E and proceeded to examine Oneglia where there should be a large olive oil producing factory. On closing the massive cluster of oil storing tanks were seen in the centre of the town.
HMS Ursula then proceeded towards San Lorenzo where an ideal deserted place for a train shoot was discovered.

At 1740 A/2, retired to seaward and surfaced early to charge the battery. At 2230 A/2, when in position 43°50'N, 07°56'E, HMS Ursula commenced to close the coast at San Lorenzo to 400 yards. The coast was unlit and quite deserted, situated between two villages. At 2300 A/2, a westbound goods train appeared. Fire was opened from a range of 500 yards. The first round was a very near miss just above the engine, which burst on the rock face of the rainly immediately behind the cab. The driver of the train immediately switched out all lights. Ursula continued to fire allowing for the trains advance. One, possibly two hits were obtained on the train, and several more on the posts supporting the overhead wires, which all came down with a run causing a series of magnificent 4000 volt sparks and electric blue flashes along the whole length of the train. 13 Rounds in all were fired, and then the gin showed signs of its old complaint - jambing. By this time the train had rolled round the corner of the next cape into the village of San Stefano. [Italian sources show that one wagon was hit and electric cable was down on a length of 500 metres.] At 2330 A/2, HMS Ursula made a detour out to sea and approached Oneglia which was five miles to the northward. (1) 3 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) bombarded olive oil storage tanks at Oneglia, Italy. Later on this day HMS Ursula sank the German transport Sainte Marguerite II (1855 GRT, built 1921) about 30 nautical miles south-east of Cannes, France in position 43°11'N, 07°19'E. This vessel was bound from Marseilles, France to Naples, Italy. The German survivors were left with the impression that the submarine was American but no US submarines operated in the Mediterranean during the war. Closed to within 150 yards of Oneglia pier. Nothing ashore stirred. There were a few lights on in houses and a car or two, whose headlights assisted Ursula in identifying the target. 0125 A/3 - Opened fire from 700 yards. Round 1 was a bit low and round 2 was over. Round 3 felled a 250 feet tall chimney with a resounding clatter of bricks and a cloud of dust. The ensuing 8 to 10 rounds surpassed the expectations and all were direct hits on the olive oil tanks, each shell bursting with an unusually large red flash, and soon a cloud of dense black smoke arose. But then the gun again jambed. After five minutes of struggling the shell was extracted and Ursula carried on firing. After firing three more rounds, which all hit, a starshell burst about 7000 yards out to sea illuminating both the town and Ursula. Intermittent popping of guns began and Lt. Lakin decided the party was over and gave order to retire at full speed on the surface while throwing overboard six smoke candles which formed a protective smoke screen. Another star shell was fired further out to sea and some shells started to fall about three miles away. Then a single search light opened up about 1000 yards on the port beam. This swept over Ursula three times. A little while later this searchlight found Ursula and two guns situated on the top of Cape Berta opened fire which was rather accurate. So at 0150 A/3, HMS Ursula dived in position 43°52'N, 08°02'E. 0322 A/3 - Surfaced in position 43°48'N, 07°59'E. All was quiet. It was disappointing that olive oil was not sufficiently inflammable to cause a spectacular fire but even so it was felt that some damage was done to the 'Italian stomach'. 0630 A/3 - Dived in position 43°26'N, 07°42'E. 1805 A/3 - Surfaced in position 43°11'N, 07°19'E and almost immediately sighted a darkened ship bearing 270°. Range was 3.5 nautical miles. Enemy course was 050°. Closed at full speed. Brought the tubes to the ready and manned the gun. The ship appeared to be in ballast and was of about 2000 tons. 1830 A/3 - Turned to starboard and brought the gun to bear. Range was 750 yards. 1831 A/3 - Opened fire. Several hits were obtained but after a few minutes the gun again jammed. 1835 A/3 - Fired one torpedo from about 500 yards. The torpedo ran under. 1840 A/3 - The ship was now stopped and the gun was in working order again so three more rounds were fired. 1850 A/3 - Closed the ship and noticed that the crew was abandoning her. The master and chief engineer were taken from the boat. 1900 A/3 - Went alongside the target and boarded her. Some provisions were taken from her as well as documents. 1940 A/3 - The seacocks and condeser inlets to the engine room were opened as well as the flood connection to both holds. 2040 A/3 - Cast off and waited for the ship to sink. 0030 A/4 - The ship had still not sunk. She was boarded again and scuttling charges were placed. Half an hour later the charges went off and the ship sank almost immediately by the bows. (1) 4 Dec 1942 At 0105 A/4, in position 43°10'N, 07°14'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted ship bearing 270°. Ursula closed to investigate. At 0130 A/4, the ship was identified as the Swiss chartered ship Nuestra Senora del Carmen (Spanish, 3482 GRT, built 1895). (1) 5 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2110 A/5, the following orders were issued to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) by the Capt. S.8; Proceed by previous route adjusting speed to pass 39°27'N, 01°26'E at 0001 A/7, Gape Palos at 0600 A/, Gape de Gata at 0600 A/9 to arrive at Gibraltar at 1100 A/10. Do not pass 04°55 W before 0600 A/10. (79) 6 Dec 1942 At 1115 A/6, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0942 A/6 which stated the following; German controlled Danish steamship Grete (1563 GRT, built 1931) expected to leave Cartagena P.M. on 6th December for Marseilles. At 1300 A/6, HMS Ursula altered course to close the expected line of advance to Marseilles of the Grete. The German controlled was however not seen. (80) 7 Dec 1942 At 0206 A/7, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2230 A/6 of which the second paragraph contained the following orders for her; HMS Ursula is to pass 38°45'N, 00°41'E at 0800 A/7. 38° 30'N, 01°26'E at 2030 A/7. 38°42'N, 03°02'E at 0800 A/8 thence direct to 37°01'N, 03°06'E to made rendezvous with escort at 0900 A/9 for onward passage to Algiers. At 0400 A/7, course was altered to comply with the above orders. At 1700 A/7, a schooner was sighted which was proceeding towards Ibiza. Decided to try a little mild interference in the name of the enemy. At 1750 A/7, in position 38.41'N, 01.02'E, HMS Ursula surfaced and hoised the Nazi flag taken from the Sainte Marguerite II. Proceeded to close the schooner hoping to obtain some bread and fish. At 1830 A/7, the schooner was brought to with a few bursts of Vickers over the mast. They were induced to lower a boat which came alongside and took over the boarding party, which included Lt. Tweedie who speaks Spanish. It was now rather dark, so the 6" Aldis was 'accidentally' shone on the flag, to make quite certain of our 'identity'. The boarding party soon retirned with the news that the schooner was carrying a cargo of rough salt and that there seemed to be no quantities of food of any kind on board. A bottle of wine, found out later to be of bad quality, was passed and shortly afterwards company was parted with a great deal of Heil-ing. They had no info on German submarines in the vicinity. It is feared that as an attempt to prejustice the Axis cause, this episode had failed. (80) 9 Dec 1942 Around 1130 A/9, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) ended her 33rd war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers. She was escorted in. Identity of the escort, which had joined at 0830 A/9, is not known. (1) 21 Dec 1942 At 1600 A/21, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for her 34th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Marettimo Island proceeding through position 38°01'N, 04°01'E at 0200 A/22 thence dived by day to pass through position 38°20'N, 08°00'E at 1800/23. Further routing would be signalled by Capt. S.10. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ursula during this patrol see the map below. (1) 22 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1147 A/22, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN); From position 38°20'N, 08°00'E at 1800 A/23 direct to patrol within 10 miles of position 38°12'N, 11°50'E by 0300 A/25. (79) 25 Dec 1942 At 2130 A/25, north-west of Marettimo Island in position 38°08'N, 11°40'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) heard fast turbine HE to the south-west. At 2134 A/25, a single ship was sighted at extreme range. This appeared to be a destroyer going from Cagliari towards Marittimo or it was on patrol. Enemy course was 110° at 15 knots. The enemy vessel was lost from sight after three minutes. At 2155 A/25, the HE faded. This was possibly the Italian destroyer Grecale carrying PoW's which sailed from Tunis at 0825 B/25 and arrived at Palermo at 0015 B/26. (1) 26 Dec 1942 At 2135 A/26, north-west of Marettimo Island in position 38°08'N, 11°40'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sighted two destroyers zigging in quarter line at a range of 4000 yards. Enemy base course was 160°, speed 12 to 15 knots. They disappeared in the direction of Marettimo. The place and time of sighting was (almost) identical with the previous night. There is nothing that matches unless there is an error somewhere: The Italian destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Antonio Pigafetta and Legionario, carrying troops, sailed from Palermo at 2000 B/26-2030 B/26 and arrived at Tunis and La Goulette at 0730 B/27-0852 B/27. The Italian torpedo boat Orione sailed from Palermo at 1920 B/26 escorting the steamer Zenobia Martini (Italian 1454 GRT, built 1911) to Pantelleria. The Italian destroyer Saetta sailed from Palermo at 0726 B/26 and arrived at Trapani at 1038 B/26. (1) 26 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0801 A/26, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) of the following; At 0615 A/26 two small merchant ships and one escort to the north-west of Cape San Vito in position 38°26'N, 12°28'E on course 240° at 5 knots. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (79) 28 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German transport Gran (4140 GRT, built 1938, former Norwegian Gran offsite link) about 12 nautical miles north-west of Marettimo Island, Italy in position 38°09'N, 11°54'E. Gran was en-route from Napels to Bizerte and was escorted by the Italian destroyer Lampo and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade. 0530 A/28 - In position 38°09'N, 11°51'E sighted a large merchant ship escorted by two destroyers. Range 10000 yards. Started attack. 0542 A/28 - Dived. 0553 A/28 - In position 38°09'N, 11°54'E fired three torpedoes from 750 yards. Loud explosions were heard after 32, 38 and 45 seconds. These were followed by breaking up noises. Meanwhile Ursula went to 150 feet and retired from the scene. 0606 A/28 - Depth charging started. 0623 A/28 - A destroyer dropped a number of small acoustic charges. Intermittent HE was heard from the other destroyer but no hunt followed. 0715 A/28 - Started to slowly come to periscope depth as it was now daylight. 0750 A/28 - Observed two destroyers stopped in the firing position. There was no sign of the merchant ship. A large number of assorted aircraft were circling overhead. 0850 A/28 - Both destroyers, which were seen to be rather smale so were probably Spica-class torpedo boats, passed at 3000 yards, steering a course of 021° at 21 knots. Both were painted grey and brown camouflage. Their decks were covered with survivors, who were sitting on the boats, gun shields, forecastle and quarter deck. They had a close air escort of Cant 506B's and an umbrella of six Ju 88's, 2 Savoia 85's and several other unknown aircraft. 1012 A/28 - Observed a Cant 506B circling the firing position, now 4 to 5 miles astern. 1013 A/28 - The Cant aircraft was seen to drop two bombs. The splashes were seen and explosions were heard. Two more aircraft were now seen patrolling in the area. 1100 A/28 - All was quiet now. Went to 50 feet and commenced reload. 1215 A/28 - Reload completed. 1613 A/28 - Received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1226 A/28 which stated the following; Force Q leaves Bone dusk 28th through position 38°10'N, 10°50'E at 0001 A/29 to operate to eastward returning to Bone passing 09°00'E at 0700 A/29. 1659 A/28 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1621 A/28 which stated the following; Your vicinity tonight. Clear present area to be in position 38°40'N, 12°10'E by 0400 A/29. If a northbound target is sighted it may be attacked. 1815 A/28 - Surfaced in position 38°12'N, 11°46'E. Proceeded 35 miles to the north-east in accordance with the orders received. (1) 29 Dec 1942 At 0045 A/29, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) arrived in her new patrol area (near 38°40'N, 12°10'E). (1) 30 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) was in rammed and damaged while she was in the process of attacking a southbound convoy of three large transports escorted by four destroyers North of Cape St. Vito, Sicily, near position 38°43'N, 12°40'E. Ursula had penetrated the destroyer screen and was on the point of firing torpedoes from close range when the convoy as a whole changed course and overran Ursula destroying both periscopes and standards, as well as damage to the conning tower. The attack had to be broken off. The convoy attacked was on passage to Bizerta and made up of the transports Alfredo Oriani (3152 GRT, built 1942), Manzoni (4550 GRT, built 1942) and Mario Roselli (6835 GRT, built 1942), escorted by the destroyers Maestrale, Corsaro and Lampo and the torpedo boats Sirio and Pallade. This convoy had sailed from Palermo at 0050 B/30-0200 B/30 and arrived at Bizerte at 1637 B/30-1800 B/30. In the reports of the Maestrale and Lampo, which are available to us, there is no mention of any ship of the convoy ramming a submarine / submerged object. It is thus not known which ship of the convoy struck HMS Ursula. 0023 A/30 - Received a signal, timed 2325 A/29, from Capt. S.10 ordering Ursula to proceed to a new patrol position in 360° - Cape San Vito - 12 nautical miles. Immediately set new course and proceed at full speed for this new patrol position. 0504 A/30 - Shortly after arriving in this patrol area sighted a convoy of three merchant ships escorted by four destroyers. Range was 9000 yards. Course 240°. Speed 15 knots. Started attack. 0509 A/30 - Dived and closed at full submerged speed. Started an attack on the leading merchant ship, the biggest, thought to be of about 5000 tons. 0513 A/30 - The leading destroyer passed ahead. Ursula went a bit deeper then periscope depth when the destroyer passed. 0515 A/30 - Returned to periscope depth. Found that the situation had changed. The convoy had made a zig towards of about 35°. 0520 A/30 - The target was now 600 yards off and came towards. Ursula had to be taken deep but she hang for over a minute at 25 feet. 0522 A/30 - When at 29 feet Ursula was hit by the ship on the periscope standards and bridge. 0525 A/30 - The rear destroyer of the convoy passed overhead. 0600 A/30 - The HE of the convoy faded away. 0601 A/30 - Surfaced and proceeded at full speed to clear the area. The damage was; both periscopes and periscope standards bent and useless. The upper telegraphs were broken, centre section of jumping wire carried away, night light smashed and superficial damage to the bridge. 0621 A/30 - Passed enemy report, timed 0500 A/30, to Capt. S.10. 0643 A/30 - Dived in position 38°43'N, 12°40'E. 0838 A/30 to 1100 A/30 - Heard many underwater explosions. 1150 A/30 - Heard fast HE crossing ahead. This sounded like two destroyers on an easterly course. 1828 A/30 - Surfaced in position 38°53'N, 12°38'E and proceeded to the northward. 2100 A/30 - Passed situation report, timed 2000 A/30, to Capt. S.10. Altered course to return to Algiers. Due to the damage sustained the patrol had to be broken off. (1) 30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2100 A/30, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) to be in position 38°16'N, 12°20'E by 2100 A/30. Enemy torpedo boat patrols were to be expected as was enemy shipping around midnight. In a signal timed 2000 A/30, HMS Ursula signalled the following to the Capt. S.10. This signal was also repeated to the Capt. S.8.; At 0522 A/30 was rammed by merchant ship during diving moonlight attack on three medium merchant ships escorted by four destroyers in position 360° - Cape San Vito - 12 miles. Both periscopes out of action and standards broken also superficial damage to bridge. Received routings, dived satisfactorily. Present position 39°11'N, 12°34'E. Course 245°. Speed of advance 120 miles per day. At 0553 A/28 sank southbound 6000 ton troop transport during similar attack in position 38°09'N, 11°51'E. three hits. five torpedoes remaining. A weather report was also included in the signal. (79) 30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2209 A/30, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); Aircraft are patrolling your area tonight after moonrise in endeavour to bomb enemy torpedo boats but enemy aircraft must be expected hunting you. (79) 31 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0029 A/31, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN); Your 2000 A/30. Fine work. Proceed through position 38°31'N, 10°44'E, thence course 270° to cross 08°00'E at 2001 A/l, thence as ordered by Capt. S.8. In a signal timed 1206 A/31, the Capt. S.8, sent the following orders to HMS Ursula; Proceed diving by day. Pass through positions 38°31'N, 08°00'E at 2001 A/1, 38°01'N, 04°48'E at 0001 A/3, thence on surface at your discretion to rendezvous with escort for Algiers in position 37°01'N, 03°17'E at 1100 A/3. (79) 2 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1432 A/2, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) of the following; 1) Your escort will be a motor launch. 2) Large fast convoy and Force H will arrive at Algiers at 0700 A/3 from the westward. 3) Three tank landing ships escorted by four minesweeepers will arrive at Algiers at 1100 A/3. Also a slow convoy will arrive at Algiers at 1400 A/3. All from westwards. 4) HMS Welshman (Capt. W.H.D. Friedberger, DSO, RN)leaves Algiers before daylight on 3 January proceeding coastwise to the east. In a signal timed 1800 A/2, HMS Ursula informed the Capt. S.8 of the following; Your signal timed 1206 A/31. Delayed by weather. Expect to arrive at rendezvous position at 0300 A/4. In a signal timed 2104 A/2, the Capt. S.8, sent the following to HMS Ursula; Your signal timed 1800 A/2 acknowledged. Adjust speed so as to pass through position 38°01'N, 04°48'E at 1800 A/3 to arrive at rendezvous not before 0730 A/4. (81) 3 Jan 1943 In a singal timed 1614 A/3, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) of the following; 1) Your escort will be HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. T.E. Williams, RNR). 2) Four H.S.L. leaves Algiers dawn 4th to eastwards. French convoy sails 0500 A/4th to westward. 3) HMS P 54 (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) leaves Algiers 1600 A/3 course 037°. HMS Sturgeon (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) leaves Algiers 0830 A/4 for exercise off Carbon. (81) 4 Jan 1943 Around 1100 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) ended her 34th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers. The damage Ursula had sustained required extensive repairs. Also she suffered from generator problems yet again. She was escorted in by HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. T.E. Williams, RNR) which had joined around 0740 A/4. (82) 20 Feb 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) carried out independent exercises off Algiers. She was escorted by two M/L's. (83) 21 Feb 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Algiers with HMS Lightning (Cdr. H.G. Walters, DSC, RN) and HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN). (83) 22 Feb 1943 Around 1800 A/22, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for Gibraltar. She is to return to the U.K. She was routed through positions 'A' (38°05'N, 01°35'E. at 0800 A/23. 'B' (135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles) at 0800 A/24. 'C' (180° - Cape De Gata - 5 miles at 0500 A/25. 'D' (180° - Cape Calaburras - 5 miles at 0600 A/26th to arrive at Gibraltar at 1030 A/26. Position 'D' was not to be passed before 0600 A/26. For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during this passage see the map below. (1) 26 Feb 1943 Around 1230 A/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Algiers. On arrival D/G trials were carried out. (1) 1 Mar 1943 At 1230 A/1, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K. At 1340 A/1, a collision with an unidentified Spanish trawler was narrowly avoided. HMS Ursula was to pass through the following positions; 'T' (35°49'N, 06°06'E) at 1845 A/1. 'U' (35°53'N, 06°15'E) at 2000 A/1. 'V' (36°37'N, 07°24'E) at 0700 A/2. 'W' (36°51'N, 09°13'E) at 0500 A/3. 'C' (42°59'N, 12°26'E) at 0330 A/7 shifting W/T watch at 0330/7 thence as ordered by F.O.S. (Flag Officer Submarines). In signals timed 1126 A/4 and 1529 A/4, HMS Ursula was ordered by F.O.S. to proceed on route 'Red' diving by day to make rendezvous with the escort in position 'X' (180° - Bishops Rock - 10 miles) at 0700 A/13. [No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (1) 6 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1216 A/6, the Flag Officer Gibraltar informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) that four or five Uboats may be crossing your course between 42°N and 45°N. (84) 8 Mar 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) attacked a German Uboat with four torpedoes in position 44°48'N, 12°13'W. No hits were obtained. The Uboat attacked was most likely the eastbound U-462 which was in approximate position 45°09'N, 12°25'W (Grid BE 9354) at 1200 B/8 and 45°09'N, 11°55'W (Grid BE 9364) at 1600 B/8. She reported nothing though. 1430 A/8 - Heard HE bearing Red 150°. The Officer of the Watch sighted the conning tower of a Uboat bearing Red 165°, course uncertain. 14450 A/8 - Lt. Profit now also sighted the Uboat throught the Low Power periscope (The attack periscope was defective). Bearing Green 25°. Enemy course 040°. Range 2300 yards. Started attack. 1451 A/8 - In position 44°48'N, 12°13'W fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards. No hits were obtained. 1647 A/8 - Surfaced and passed a contact report timed 1451 A/8. (1) 13 Mar 1943 At 1030 A/13, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN), makes rendezvous off Bishops Rock with her escort, the ASW trawler HMS Asie (Skr. E.A. Mutten, RNR). (1) 14 Mar 1943 Around 1930 A/14, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Fort Blockhouse - Gosport) from Gibraltar. Bennett, DSC, RNVR), HMS Spurs (A/Skr.Lt. E.J. Day, RNR) and HMS Arab (T/Lt. J.S.H. Lloyd, RNVR). On completion of the exercises HMS Ursula proceeded to Rosyth. (100) 17 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Methill with HMS Stoke City (T/Lt. J.P.P. Tyndall, RNVR). (100) 18 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Methill, first with HMS Castleton (Lt. S.E. Jagger, RN) and HMS Ludlow (T/Lt. J.A. Graham, RNVR), later with HMS Polka (T/Lt. I.A. Thorpe, MBE, RNVR) and HMS Leicester City (T/Lt. C.E. Irving, RNVR) and finally with HMS Valse (T/Lt. E.J. Newell, RNVR) and HMS Spurs (A/Skr.Lt. E.J. Day, RNR). (100) 19 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted RD/F exercises off Methill. Upon completion of these exercises HMS Ursula proceeded to Dundee. (100) 27 Apr 1944 Around 0600 B/27, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) departed Dundee for Blyth where she arrived later around 1800 B/27. At Blyth she is immediately docked in No.2 graving dock to change the propellers. (100) 28 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) is undocked. (100) 29 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) proceeded from Blyth to Dundee. (100) 6 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) proceeded from Dundee to Rosyth. (101) 9 May 1944 On board HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN), the training of the new Russian crew which is to to take her over, begins. (101) 19 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted exercises off the River Tay eastuary. (101) 21 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted exercises off the River Tay eastuary. (101) 25 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted exercises off the River Tay eastuary. (101) 26 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted exercises off the River Tay eastuary. (101) 30 May 1944 At 1120 B/30, at the Rosyth Dockyard, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN), is decommissioned by the Royal Navy and transferred to the Soviet Union. Return to the Allied Warships section
At 2230 A/2, when in position 43°50'N, 07°56'E, HMS Ursula commenced to close the coast at San Lorenzo to 400 yards. The coast was unlit and quite deserted, situated between two villages.

At 2300 A/2, a westbound goods train appeared. Fire was opened from a range of 500 yards. The first round was a very near miss just above the engine, which burst on the rock face of the rainly immediately behind the cab. The driver of the train immediately switched out all lights. Ursula continued to fire allowing for the trains advance. One, possibly two hits were obtained on the train, and several more on the posts supporting the overhead wires, which all came down with a run causing a series of magnificent 4000 volt sparks and electric blue flashes along the whole length of the train. 13 Rounds in all were fired, and then the gin showed signs of its old complaint - jambing. By this time the train had rolled round the corner of the next cape into the village of San Stefano. [Italian sources show that one wagon was hit and electric cable was down on a length of 500 metres.]
At 2330 A/2, HMS Ursula made a detour out to sea and approached Oneglia which was five miles to the northward. (1)
3 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) bombarded olive oil storage tanks at Oneglia, Italy.
Later on this day HMS Ursula sank the German transport Sainte Marguerite II (1855 GRT, built 1921) about 30 nautical miles south-east of Cannes, France in position 43°11'N, 07°19'E.

This vessel was bound from Marseilles, France to Naples, Italy. The German survivors were left with the impression that the submarine was American but no US submarines operated in the Mediterranean during the war.
Closed to within 150 yards of Oneglia pier. Nothing ashore stirred. There were a few lights on in houses and a car or two, whose headlights assisted Ursula in identifying the target.

0125 A/3 - Opened fire from 700 yards. Round 1 was a bit low and round 2 was over. Round 3 felled a 250 feet tall chimney with a resounding clatter of bricks and a cloud of dust. The ensuing 8 to 10 rounds surpassed the expectations and all were direct hits on the olive oil tanks, each shell bursting with an unusually large red flash, and soon a cloud of dense black smoke arose. But then the gun again jambed. After five minutes of struggling the shell was extracted and Ursula carried on firing. After firing three more rounds, which all hit, a starshell burst about 7000 yards out to sea illuminating both the town and Ursula. Intermittent popping of guns began and Lt. Lakin decided the party was over and gave order to retire at full speed on the surface while throwing overboard six smoke candles which formed a protective smoke screen. Another star shell was fired further out to sea and some shells started to fall about three miles away. Then a single search light opened up about 1000 yards on the port beam. This swept over Ursula three times. A little while later this searchlight found Ursula and two guns situated on the top of Cape Berta opened fire which was rather accurate. So at 0150 A/3, HMS Ursula dived in position 43°52'N, 08°02'E.

0322 A/3 - Surfaced in position 43°48'N, 07°59'E. All was quiet. It was disappointing that olive oil was not sufficiently inflammable to cause a spectacular fire but even so it was felt that some damage was done to the 'Italian stomach'.
0630 A/3 - Dived in position 43°26'N, 07°42'E.

1805 A/3 - Surfaced in position 43°11'N, 07°19'E and almost immediately sighted a darkened ship bearing 270°. Range was 3.5 nautical miles. Enemy course was 050°. Closed at full speed. Brought the tubes to the ready and manned the gun. The ship appeared to be in ballast and was of about 2000 tons.
1830 A/3 - Turned to starboard and brought the gun to bear. Range was 750 yards.
1831 A/3 - Opened fire. Several hits were obtained but after a few minutes the gun again jammed.
1835 A/3 - Fired one torpedo from about 500 yards. The torpedo ran under.
1840 A/3 - The ship was now stopped and the gun was in working order again so three more rounds were fired.
1850 A/3 - Closed the ship and noticed that the crew was abandoning her. The master and chief engineer were taken from the boat.
1900 A/3 - Went alongside the target and boarded her. Some provisions were taken from her as well as documents.
1940 A/3 - The seacocks and condeser inlets to the engine room were opened as well as the flood connection to both holds.
2040 A/3 - Cast off and waited for the ship to sink.
0030 A/4 - The ship had still not sunk. She was boarded again and scuttling charges were placed. Half an hour later the charges went off and the ship sank almost immediately by the bows. (1)
4 Dec 1942 At 0105 A/4, in position 43°10'N, 07°14'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted ship bearing 270°. Ursula closed to investigate.
At 0130 A/4, the ship was identified as the Swiss chartered ship Nuestra Senora del Carmen (Spanish, 3482 GRT, built 1895). (1)

5 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2110 A/5, the following orders were issued to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) by the Capt. S.8; Proceed by previous route adjusting speed to pass 39°27'N, 01°26'E at 0001 A/7, Gape Palos at 0600 A/, Gape de Gata at 0600 A/9 to arrive at Gibraltar at 1100 A/10. Do not pass 04°55 W before 0600 A/10. (79)

6 Dec 1942 At 1115 A/6, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0942 A/6 which stated the following; German controlled Danish steamship Grete (1563 GRT, built 1931) expected to leave Cartagena P.M. on 6th December for Marseilles.
At 1300 A/6, HMS Ursula altered course to close the expected line of advance to Marseilles of the Grete. The German controlled was however not seen. (80)

7 Dec 1942 At 0206 A/7, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2230 A/6 of which the second paragraph contained the following orders for her; HMS Ursula is to pass 38°45'N, 00°41'E at 0800 A/7. 38° 30'N, 01°26'E at 2030 A/7. 38°42'N, 03°02'E at 0800 A/8 thence direct to 37°01'N, 03°06'E to made rendezvous with escort at 0900 A/9 for onward passage to Algiers.
At 0400 A/7, course was altered to comply with the above orders.
At 1700 A/7, a schooner was sighted which was proceeding towards Ibiza. Decided to try a little mild interference in the name of the enemy.
At 1750 A/7, in position 38.41'N, 01.02'E, HMS Ursula surfaced and hoised the Nazi flag taken from the Sainte Marguerite II. Proceeded to close the schooner hoping to obtain some bread and fish.

At 1830 A/7, the schooner was brought to with a few bursts of Vickers over the mast. They were induced to lower a boat which came alongside and took over the boarding party, which included Lt. Tweedie who speaks Spanish. It was now rather dark, so the 6" Aldis was 'accidentally' shone on the flag, to make quite certain of our 'identity'. The boarding party soon retirned with the news that the schooner was carrying a cargo of rough salt and that there seemed to be no quantities of food of any kind on board. A bottle of wine, found out later to be of bad quality, was passed and shortly afterwards company was parted with a great deal of Heil-ing. They had no info on German submarines in the vicinity. It is feared that as an attempt to prejustice the Axis cause, this episode had failed. (80)
9 Dec 1942 Around 1130 A/9, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) ended her 33rd war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in. Identity of the escort, which had joined at 0830 A/9, is not known. (1)

21 Dec 1942 At 1600 A/21, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for her 34th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Marettimo Island proceeding through position 38°01'N, 04°01'E at 0200 A/22 thence dived by day to pass through position 38°20'N, 08°00'E at 1800/23. Further routing would be signalled by Capt. S.10.

22 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1147 A/22, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN); From position 38°20'N, 08°00'E at 1800 A/23 direct to patrol within 10 miles of position 38°12'N, 11°50'E by 0300 A/25. (79)
25 Dec 1942 At 2130 A/25, north-west of Marettimo Island in position 38°08'N, 11°40'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) heard fast turbine HE to the south-west.

At 2134 A/25, a single ship was sighted at extreme range. This appeared to be a destroyer going from Cagliari towards Marittimo or it was on patrol. Enemy course was 110° at 15 knots. The enemy vessel was lost from sight after three minutes.
At 2155 A/25, the HE faded.
This was possibly the Italian destroyer Grecale carrying PoW's which sailed from Tunis at 0825 B/25 and arrived at Palermo at 0015 B/26. (1)

26 Dec 1942 At 2135 A/26, north-west of Marettimo Island in position 38°08'N, 11°40'E, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) sighted two destroyers zigging in quarter line at a range of 4000 yards. Enemy base course was 160°, speed 12 to 15 knots. They disappeared in the direction of Marettimo. The place and time of sighting was (almost) identical with the previous night.

There is nothing that matches unless there is an error somewhere: The Italian destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Antonio Pigafetta and Legionario, carrying troops, sailed from Palermo at 2000 B/26-2030 B/26 and arrived at Tunis and La Goulette at 0730 B/27-0852 B/27.
The Italian torpedo boat Orione sailed from Palermo at 1920 B/26 escorting the steamer Zenobia Martini (Italian 1454 GRT, built 1911) to Pantelleria.
The Italian destroyer Saetta sailed from Palermo at 0726 B/26 and arrived at Trapani at 1038 B/26. (1)
26 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0801 A/26, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) of the following;
At 0615 A/26 two small merchant ships and one escort to the north-west of Cape San Vito in position 38°26'N, 12°28'E on course 240° at 5 knots. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (79)

28 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German transport Gran (4140 GRT, built 1938, former Norwegian Gran offsite link) about 12 nautical miles north-west of Marettimo Island, Italy in position 38°09'N, 11°54'E.
Gran was en-route from Napels to Bizerte and was escorted by the Italian destroyer Lampo and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade.
0530 A/28 - In position 38°09'N, 11°51'E sighted a large merchant ship escorted by two destroyers. Range 10000 yards. Started attack.
0542 A/28 - Dived.

0553 A/28 - In position 38°09'N, 11°54'E fired three torpedoes from 750 yards. Loud explosions were heard after 32, 38 and 45 seconds. These were followed by breaking up noises. Meanwhile Ursula went to 150 feet and retired from the scene.
0606 A/28 - Depth charging started.
0623 A/28 - A destroyer dropped a number of small acoustic charges. Intermittent HE was heard from the other destroyer but no hunt followed.
0715 A/28 - Started to slowly come to periscope depth as it was now daylight.
0750 A/28 - Observed two destroyers stopped in the firing position. There was no sign of the merchant ship. A large number of assorted aircraft were circling overhead.

0850 A/28 - Both destroyers, which were seen to be rather smale so were probably Spica-class torpedo boats, passed at 3000 yards, steering a course of 021° at 21 knots. Both were painted grey and brown camouflage. Their decks were covered with survivors, who were sitting on the boats, gun shields, forecastle and quarter deck. They had a close air escort of Cant 506B's and an umbrella of six Ju 88's, 2 Savoia 85's and several other unknown aircraft.
1012 A/28 - Observed a Cant 506B circling the firing position, now 4 to 5 miles astern.
1013 A/28 - The Cant aircraft was seen to drop two bombs. The splashes were seen and explosions were heard. Two more aircraft were now seen patrolling in the area.
1100 A/28 - All was quiet now. Went to 50 feet and commenced reload.
1215 A/28 - Reload completed.

1613 A/28 - Received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1226 A/28 which stated the following; Force Q leaves Bone dusk 28th through position 38°10'N, 10°50'E at 0001 A/29 to operate to eastward returning to Bone passing 09°00'E at 0700 A/29.

1659 A/28 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1621 A/28 which stated the following; Your vicinity tonight. Clear present area to be in position 38°40'N, 12°10'E by 0400 A/29. If a northbound target is sighted it may be attacked.
1815 A/28 - Surfaced in position 38°12'N, 11°46'E. Proceeded 35 miles to the north-east in accordance with the orders received. (1)
29 Dec 1942 At 0045 A/29, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) arrived in her new patrol area (near 38°40'N, 12°10'E). (1)

30 Dec 1942 HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) was in rammed and damaged while she was in the process of attacking a southbound convoy of three large transports escorted by four destroyers North of Cape St. Vito, Sicily, near position 38°43'N, 12°40'E. Ursula had penetrated the destroyer screen and was on the point of firing torpedoes from close range when the convoy as a whole changed course and overran Ursula destroying both periscopes and standards, as well as damage to the conning tower. The attack had to be broken off.

The convoy attacked was on passage to Bizerta and made up of the transports Alfredo Oriani (3152 GRT, built 1942), Manzoni (4550 GRT, built 1942) and Mario Roselli (6835 GRT, built 1942), escorted by the destroyers Maestrale, Corsaro and Lampo and the torpedo boats Sirio and Pallade. This convoy had sailed from Palermo at 0050 B/30-0200 B/30 and arrived at Bizerte at 1637 B/30-1800 B/30.

In the reports of the Maestrale and Lampo, which are available to us, there is no mention of any ship of the convoy ramming a submarine / submerged object. It is thus not known which ship of the convoy struck HMS Ursula.

0023 A/30 - Received a signal, timed 2325 A/29, from Capt. S.10 ordering Ursula to proceed to a new patrol position in 360° - Cape San Vito - 12 nautical miles. Immediately set new course and proceed at full speed for this new patrol position.
0504 A/30 - Shortly after arriving in this patrol area sighted a convoy of three merchant ships escorted by four destroyers. Range was 9000 yards. Course 240°. Speed 15 knots. Started attack.
0509 A/30 - Dived and closed at full submerged speed. Started an attack on the leading merchant ship, the biggest, thought to be of about 5000 tons.
0513 A/30 - The leading destroyer passed ahead. Ursula went a bit deeper then periscope depth when the destroyer passed.
0515 A/30 - Returned to periscope depth. Found that the situation had changed. The convoy had made a zig towards of about 35°.
0520 A/30 - The target was now 600 yards off and came towards. Ursula had to be taken deep but she hang for over a minute at 25 feet.
0522 A/30 - When at 29 feet Ursula was hit by the ship on the periscope standards and bridge.
0525 A/30 - The rear destroyer of the convoy passed overhead.
0600 A/30 - The HE of the convoy faded away.

0601 A/30 - Surfaced and proceeded at full speed to clear the area. The damage was; both periscopes and periscope standards bent and useless. The upper telegraphs were broken, centre section of jumping wire carried away, night light smashed and superficial damage to the bridge.
0621 A/30 - Passed enemy report, timed 0500 A/30, to Capt. S.10.
0643 A/30 - Dived in position 38°43'N, 12°40'E.
0838 A/30 to 1100 A/30 - Heard many underwater explosions.
1150 A/30 - Heard fast HE crossing ahead. This sounded like two destroyers on an easterly course.
1828 A/30 - Surfaced in position 38°53'N, 12°38'E and proceeded to the northward.
2100 A/30 - Passed situation report, timed 2000 A/30, to Capt. S.10. Altered course to return to Algiers. Due to the damage sustained the patrol had to be broken off. (1)

30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2100 A/30, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) to be in position 38°16'N, 12°20'E by 2100 A/30. Enemy torpedo boat patrols were to be expected as was enemy shipping around midnight.

In a signal timed 2000 A/30, HMS Ursula signalled the following to the Capt. S.10. This signal was also repeated to the Capt. S.8.; At 0522 A/30 was rammed by merchant ship during diving moonlight attack on three medium merchant ships escorted by four destroyers in position 360° - Cape San Vito - 12 miles. Both periscopes out of action and standards broken also superficial damage to bridge. Received routings, dived satisfactorily. Present position 39°11'N, 12°34'E. Course 245°. Speed of advance 120 miles per day. At 0553 A/28 sank southbound 6000 ton troop transport during similar attack in position 38°09'N, 11°51'E. three hits. five torpedoes remaining. A weather report was also included in the signal. (79)

30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2209 A/30, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); Aircraft are patrolling your area tonight after moonrise in endeavour to bomb enemy torpedo boats but enemy aircraft must be expected hunting you. (79)

31 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0029 A/31, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN); Your 2000 A/30. Fine work. Proceed through position 38°31'N, 10°44'E, thence course 270° to cross 08°00'E at 2001 A/l, thence as ordered by Capt. S.8.

In a signal timed 1206 A/31, the Capt. S.8, sent the following orders to HMS Ursula; Proceed diving by day. Pass through positions 38°31'N, 08°00'E at 2001 A/1, 38°01'N, 04°48'E at 0001 A/3, thence on surface at your discretion to rendezvous with escort for Algiers in position 37°01'N, 03°17'E at 1100 A/3. (79)

2 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1432 A/2, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) of the following; 1) Your escort will be a motor launch. 2) Large fast convoy and Force H will arrive at Algiers at 0700 A/3 from the westward. 3) Three tank landing ships escorted by four minesweeepers will arrive at Algiers at 1100 A/3. Also a slow convoy will arrive at Algiers at 1400 A/3. All from westwards. 4) HMS Welshman (Capt. W.H.D. Friedberger, DSO, RN)leaves Algiers before daylight on 3 January proceeding coastwise to the east.
In a signal timed 1800 A/2, HMS Ursula informed the Capt. S.8 of the following; Your signal timed 1206 A/31. Delayed by weather. Expect to arrive at rendezvous position at 0300 A/4.

In a signal timed 2104 A/2, the Capt. S.8, sent the following to HMS Ursula; Your signal timed 1800 A/2 acknowledged. Adjust speed so as to pass through position 38°01'N, 04°48'E at 1800 A/3 to arrive at rendezvous not before 0730 A/4. (81)

3 Jan 1943 In a singal timed 1614 A/3, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) of the following; 1) Your escort will be HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. T.E. Williams, RNR). 2) Four H.S.L. leaves Algiers dawn 4th to eastwards. French convoy sails 0500 A/4th to westward. 3) HMS P 54 (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) leaves Algiers 1600 A/3 course 037°. HMS Sturgeon (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) leaves Algiers 0830 A/4 for exercise off Carbon. (81)

4 Jan 1943 Around 1100 A/4, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) ended her 34th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers. The damage Ursula had sustained required extensive repairs. Also she suffered from generator problems yet again.
She was escorted in by HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. T.E. Williams, RNR) which had joined around 0740 A/4. (82)
20 Feb 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) carried out independent exercises off Algiers. She was escorted by two M/L's. (83)
21 Feb 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Algiers with HMS Lightning (Cdr. H.G. Walters, DSC, RN) and HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN). (83)
22 Feb 1943 Around 1800 A/22, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for Gibraltar. She is to return to the U.K.

She was routed through positions 'A' (38°05'N, 01°35'E. at 0800 A/23. 'B' (135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles) at 0800 A/24. 'C' (180° - Cape De Gata - 5 miles at 0500 A/25. 'D' (180° - Cape Calaburras - 5 miles at 0600 A/26th to arrive at Gibraltar at 1030 A/26. Position 'D' was not to be passed before 0600 A/26.
For the daily positions of HMS Ursula during this passage see the map below.
26 Feb 1943 Around 1230 A/26, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Algiers. On arrival D/G trials were carried out. (1)
1 Mar 1943 At 1230 A/1, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K.
At 1340 A/1, a collision with an unidentified Spanish trawler was narrowly avoided.

HMS Ursula was to pass through the following positions; 'T' (35°49'N, 06°06'E) at 1845 A/1. 'U' (35°53'N, 06°15'E) at 2000 A/1. 'V' (36°37'N, 07°24'E) at 0700 A/2. 'W' (36°51'N, 09°13'E) at 0500 A/3. 'C' (42°59'N, 12°26'E) at 0330 A/7 shifting W/T watch at 0330/7 thence as ordered by F.O.S. (Flag Officer Submarines).

In signals timed 1126 A/4 and 1529 A/4, HMS Ursula was ordered by F.O.S. to proceed on route 'Red' diving by day to make rendezvous with the escort in position 'X' (180° - Bishops Rock - 10 miles) at 0700 A/13.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (1)
6 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1216 A/6, the Flag Officer Gibraltar informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) that four or five Uboats may be crossing your course between 42°N and 45°N. (84)
8 Mar 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) attacked a German Uboat with four torpedoes in position 44°48'N, 12°13'W. No hits were obtained.

The Uboat attacked was most likely the eastbound U-462 which was in approximate position 45°09'N, 12°25'W (Grid BE 9354) at 1200 B/8 and 45°09'N, 11°55'W (Grid BE 9364) at 1600 B/8. She reported nothing though.
1430 A/8 - Heard HE bearing Red 150°. The Officer of the Watch sighted the conning tower of a Uboat bearing Red 165°, course uncertain.
14450 A/8 - Lt. Profit now also sighted the Uboat throught the Low Power periscope (The attack periscope was defective). Bearing Green 25°. Enemy course 040°. Range 2300 yards. Started attack.
1451 A/8 - In position 44°48'N, 12°13'W fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards. No hits were obtained.
1647 A/8 - Surfaced and passed a contact report timed 1451 A/8. (1)
13 Mar 1943 At 1030 A/13, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN), makes rendezvous off Bishops Rock with her escort, the ASW trawler HMS Asie (Skr. E.A. Mutten, RNR). (1)
14 Mar 1943 Around 1930 A/14, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Fort Blockhouse - Gosport) from Gibraltar.
[As no logs are available for the months of March and April 1943 some details for these months will be missing.] (1)
20 Mar 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) is docked at Portsmouth. [We do not (yet) now the date she was undocked.] (85)

4 Apr 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) proceeded from Portsmouth to Portland. She makes the passage together with HMS Taku (Lt. A.J.W. Pitt, RN) and HMS Sickle (Lt. J.R. Drummond, RN). They were escorted by HMS Kingston Turquoise (Ch.Skr. H.E. Dodd, RNR).

6 Apr 1943 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Profit, DSC, RN) proceeded from Portland to Plymouth. She makes the passage together with HMS Taku (Lt. A.J.W. Pitt, RN) and HMS Sickle (Lt. J.R. Drummond, RN). They were escorted by HMS Kingston Turquoise (Ch.Skr. H.E. Dodd, RNR).

17 Dec 1943 Around 0900 A/17, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) departed Blyth for Rothesay. She makes the passage together with HMS Trusty (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN) and HMS Uther (Lt. P.S. Beale, RN). They were escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR) until 1305 A/18 when HMS Sardonyx (T/A/Lt.Cdr. E. Playne, RNVR) took over the escort. Also HMS Telemachus (Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN) joined the 'convoy'. (94)

19 Dec 1943 In very bad weather, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN), HMS Trusty (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN), HMS Uther (Lt. P.S. Beale, RN) and HMS Telemachus (Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN) loose contact witch their escort HMS Sardonyx (T/A/Lt.Cdr. E. Playne, RNVR). All ships sought shelter in Stornoway.
They all departed for the Clyde the next day, around 0900 A/20, when the weather had cleared. (94)

8 Mar 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) is undocked at Kames Bay. She immediately returned to Rothesay. (99)
18 Mar 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted trials and exercises in the Clyde area. (99)

19 Mar 1944 Around 1500 A/19, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) departed Rothesay for Scapa Flow. She makes the passage together with HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) which is to continue on to Blyth. They were escorted by HMS Pennywort (T/Lt. A.W. James, RNR). (99)
21 Mar 1944 Around 0930 A/21, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow where she is to be used for A/S exercises. (99)
22 Mar 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow. (99)

23 Mar 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Meteor (Lt.Cdr. D.J.B. Jewitt, RN) and HMS Wakeful (Lt.Cdr. G.D. Pound, DSC, RN) and later with HMS Milne (Capt. I.M.R. Campbell, DSO, RN) and HMS Opportune (Cdr. J. Lee-Barber, DSO and Bar, RN). (99)

24 Mar 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Saumarez (Capt. P.G.L. Cazalet, DSC, RN) and HMCS Algonquin (A/Lt.Cdr. D.W. Piers, DSC, RCN) and later with HMS Meteor (Lt.Cdr. D.J.B. Jewitt, RN) and HMS Ursa (Cdr. D.B. Wyburd, DSC, RN). (99)

25 Mar 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Ursa (Cdr. D.B. Wyburd, DSC, RN) and HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. P.B.N. Lewis, DSC, RN) and later with HNoMS Svenner (Lt.Cdr. T. Holte). (99)

26 Mar 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. P.B.N. Lewis, DSC, RN) and HMS Wakeful (Lt.Cdr. G.D. Pound, DSC, RN) and later with HMCS Sioux (A/Lt.Cdr. E.E.G. Boak, RCN). (99)
28 Mar 1944 Around 0830 A/28, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Dundee. She was escorted by HMS Scalby Wyke (Skr. A. Mackay, RNR). (99)
29 Mar 1944 Around 0920 A/29, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) arrived at Dundee from Scapa Flow. (99)

11 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted gunnery exercises off Dundee with HMS ML 229 (T/Lt. W.C.R. Walsh, RNVR) and A/S exercises off Methill with HMS ML 201 (T/Lt. G.T. Bruce, RNVR) and HMS HDML 1070 (T/Skr. J. Clark, RNR). (100)
12 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Methill with HMS ML 201 (T/Lt. G.T. Bruce, RNVR) and HMS HDML 1036 (T/Skr. D.G. Spindler, CGM, RNR). (100)
13 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Methill with HMS ML 201 (T/Lt. G.T. Bruce, RNVR) and HMS HDML 1047 (T/Skr. P. Reid, RNR).
14 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Methill with HMS ML 201 (T/Lt. G.T. Bruce, RNVR), three HDML's and HMS Stoke City (T/Lt. J.P.P. Tyndall, RNVR). (100)

15 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Methill, first with HMS Westminster (Lt.Cdr. H.G. Bowerman, DSC, RN) and HMS Valorous (Lt. M.G. Macleod, RN) and later with HMS Preston North End (T/Lt. D.K. Bennett, DSC, RNVR), HMS Spurs (A/Skr.Lt. E.J. Day, RNR) and HMS Arab (T/Lt. J.S.H. Lloyd, RNVR).
On completion of the exercises HMS Ursula proceeded to Rosyth. (100)
17 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Methill with HMS Stoke City (T/Lt. J.P.P. Tyndall, RNVR). (100)

18 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Methill, first with HMS Castleton (Lt. S.E. Jagger, RN) and HMS Ludlow (T/Lt. J.A. Graham, RNVR), later with HMS Polka (T/Lt. I.A. Thorpe, MBE, RNVR) and HMS Leicester City (T/Lt. C.E. Irving, RNVR) and finally with HMS Valse (T/Lt. E.J. Newell, RNVR) and HMS Spurs (A/Skr.Lt. E.J. Day, RNR). (100)
19 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted RD/F exercises off Methill. Upon completion of these exercises HMS Ursula proceeded to Dundee. (100)

27 Apr 1944 Around 0600 B/27, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) departed Dundee for Blyth where she arrived later around 1800 B/27. At Blyth she is immediately docked in No.2 graving dock to change the propellers. (100)
28 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) is undocked. (100)
29 Apr 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) proceeded from Blyth to Dundee. (100)
6 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) proceeded from Dundee to Rosyth. (101)
9 May 1944 On board HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN), the training of the new Russian crew which is to to take her over, begins. (101)
19 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted exercises off the River Tay eastuary. (101)
21 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted exercises off the River Tay eastuary. (101)
25 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted exercises off the River Tay eastuary. (101)
26 May 1944 HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) conducted exercises off the River Tay eastuary. (101)
30 May 1944 At 1120 B/30, at the Rosyth Dockyard, HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN), is decommissioned by the Royal Navy and transferred to the Soviet Union. (101)
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
